Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                                                           15 | P a g e
                                                                                                                                               Confidential
        ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT (ESIA) REPORT

                                 EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
Project background

Kien Giang (now An Giang) is a coastal province of the Mekong Delta (MD) with a relatively
long coastline (about 200 kilometers) that is directly affected by climate change (CC),
particularly sea level rise (SLR). After many years of transitioning the production structure
from rice cultivation to a combination of rice and aquaculture, the value of products produced
in the same conversion zone has increased. The transformation structure is gradually being
implemented in the right direction, resulting in the creation of several effective coastal rural
areas and the prospect of agriculture that combines sustainable, comprehensive, and
harmonious development with nature. Currently, the province has more than 90,000ha of
aquaculture, with nearly 71,500ha dedicated to rice-aquaculture farming. The central
government has recently mobilized several capital sources for Kien Giang (now An Giang)
province to carry out transportation and irrigation projects for aquaculture development. This
has improved investment efficiency, as well as socioeconomic development and social security
of the province. However, due to a lack of capital, no coastal area in the province has been
adequately and fully invested in infrastructure for optimal value-added production. In addition,
the situation of riverbank erosion, coastal erosion, saltwater intrusion, etc. in recent years in the
province has become complicated and the trend is becoming more and more serious. The
province has implemented solutions to urgently overcome serious landslides in some coastal
sections, affecting the dyke body, saltwater prevention and freshwater sluice structures, etc.
However, this requires investment costs. The investment is very large and the province has not
been able to meet it, so there are still many sections with particularly dangerous landslides that
have not been treated. This has greatly affected people's lives, production, and coastal
infrastructure. Therefore, investing in the project “Kien Giang sustainable fisheries
development project” to promote socio-economic development of the province and the region
to adapt to climate change, protect the environment and gradually associate with the
development of sustainable, quality and diverse fisheries is necessary.
The project aims to the following specific objectives: (i) To build and improve infrastructure,
innovate technology, strengthen management capacity, and organize production to promote the
fisheries sector in the direction of value enhancement, deep international integration,
environmental friendliness, and adaptation to climate change; and (ii) To encourage and attract
all economic sectors to contribute to the sustainable development of the province's fishery
sector, and promote socio-economic development associated with environmental protection.
The project's objectives will be achieved through the implementation of 02 components
consisting of (i) Building new basic infrastructure and upgrading current infrastructure for
shrimp farming area in the south of NH80 of Hon Dat district (now Binh Giang and Binh Son
communes), including: 23 new sluice gates with a width of 8 to 10 m; a new road of 7.03 km;
25.18km of medium and low voltage lines and 18 upgraded sluice gates; and (ii) Technological
innovation, increased management capacity, and improved production organization in brackish
shrimp farming. The project will be implemented in 2021 – 2026 with total investment cost of
25.45 million USD.

Legal and technical basis for ESIA




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                  16 | P a g e
                                                                                                     Confidential
          ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT (ESIA) REPORT

The environmental assessment carried out during project preparation confirmed that the
project’s environmental risks were classified as moderate. The environmental and social
standards (ESS) applied to the project are as follows: ESS1: Assessment and management of
environmental and social risks and impacts; ESS2: Labor and working conditions; ESS3:
Resource Efficiency and and pollution prevention and management; ESS4: Community Health
and safety; ESS5: Land acquisition, restrictions on land use and involuntary resettlement; ESS6:
Biodiversity conservation and sustainable management of living natural resources; ESS7:
Indigenous Peoples-Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local
Communities, ESS8: Cultural heritage, and ESS10: Stakeholder engagement and information
disclosure. The Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) document is prepared
according to ESF. Scope of the ESIA is focused on the project’s structural components
involving construction activities.

Project description

The project is to build new basic infrastructure and upgrading current infrastructure for shrimp
farming area in the south of NH80 of Hon Dat district (now Binh Giang and Binh Son
commune)1, including:
     Building 23 sluice gates with a width of 8 to 10 m and management houses to separate fresh
     and brackish water areas for agricultural restructuring, aquaculture, and rice cultivation;
     Building a delta grade-V road with a total length of about 7.03 km (running along the East
     bank of Binh Giang 1 canal, connecting NH80 to the sea dyke);
     Installing 25.18km (including 21.54km medium-voltage power lines, 3.64km low-voltage
     power lines), and 30 transformer stations to operate sluices;
     Upgrading the gates and operation devices of 5 sluices along the sea dike, and 13 in-field
     sluices along the Don Dong canal for the current aquaculture area in the project area;
     Other ancillary works.

In addition, technological innovation, increased management capacity, and improved
production organization in brackish shrimp farming are non-structural solutions are
implemented in the project.

Social and environmental baselines

In November 2023, 10 air samples, 20 surface water samples, 10 groundwater samples, 10
wastewater samples, 16 soil and sediment samples, and 20 aquatic life samples were taken and
analyzed to evaluate the background environment quality in the project area. The analysis
results reveal that the ambient ai, groundwater quality in the project region is reasonably good,
with no indicators of pollution resulting from the local population's socioeconomic
development and livelihood. Also, the majority of surface water samples in the area are still
relatively good (meeting water quality Class B standards), with only the locations near NH 80
showing slight signs of pollution (meeting water quality Class C standards). Water sources
heavily contaminated with bacteria often only meet Class C water quality standards. This also
reflects the current distribution of the population in the project area mainly along canals and
the low sanitation conditions. Most domestic wastewater of local people is directly discharged

Hon Dat District ceased to exist as an administrative unit of Vietnam effective June 30, 2025.
1



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                    17 | P a g e
                                                                                                       Confidential
        ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT (ESIA) REPORT

into the canals, which is the main cause of bacterial contamination in the water sources in the
area. Besides, the analysis results indicate heavy organic pollution in the wastewater in the area,
with COD values ranging from 31.5 to 127 mg/L. All collected wastewater samples are
untreated domestic wastewater discharged into the environment from households and
residential areas. However, the amount of wastewater from these sources is low, so the impact
on the receiving water quality is low also. Futhermore, all 16 soil and sediment samples from
the project area have heavy metal concentrations within standards. This indicates that the heavy
metal content in all soil samples is suitable for various land use purposes such as agriculture,
residential, industrial, commercial, and service activities. Additionally, the organic matter
content in the soil indicates that the soil samples are on average, ranging from 1.72 to 2.88%.
The pH values of the soil samples range from 3.20 to 4.79, classified as moderately to heavily
acidic soil.
The majority of the new sluices are situated in sparsely inhabited regions, encompassed by
vacant land and a few trees. There are 2 water supply plants in the project area, namely Binh
Giang commune plant and Binh Son commune plant. The 2 plants take surface water to supply.
The water intake point of the Binh Giang plant is 200m from the T6-1 sluice and 320m from
the VR-1 sluice for the Binh Son plant. Around the sluices to be improved in the frame of the
project, there are no residents living nearby. The sluice gates are operated automatically based
on the tidal differences, which affects water source regulation for production. Additionally,
some of the wooden sluice gates are currently decayed, causing saltwater leakage into
freshwater production areas.
The road is situated on the eastern side of Binh Giang 1 canal, commencing at NH80 and
terminating at the seadike. It spans a total length of 7.03 kilometers. Currently, it is unpaved
and of poor quality, making it challenging for people to travel, especially during the rainy
season. The east side of the road is Binh Giang 1 canal and the west side of the road is rice
fields and fruit gardens of local people. There are total of 74 households living along the road
but they concentrate in the section from NH80 to canal 200. Houses in this area are mainly
level-4 houses, temporary houses (corrugated iron roof houses, and corrugated iron huts). As a
result, contractors need to be careful not to damage these houses.
There are a few sensitive receptors within a radius of 300m from the project items, including
schools, churches, temples, and water intake points of water supply plants, etc.

Environmental and Social Impacts and Risks and Mitigation measures

ESIA has identified both the potential negative and positive impacts and risks of the project
during 3 phases (preparation, construction and operation).
During preparation phase, the negative potential impacts and risks include (i) impacts due to
land acquisition. About 15.46 ha of land will be used for the project, but only 2.77 ha will be
acquired from 57 households, in which 12 households will be relocated; (ii) impacts on
livelihood; (iii) land use change; (iv) impacts due to UXOs. Most impacts are temporary, local
and reversible due to medium scale construction. These impacts and risks can be mitigated by
applying the approved resettlement plan (RP) of the project and the project owner will contract
with relevant unit to clear UXOs before commencing construction activities and must closely
monitor and consult with local people about the land acquisition and clearance and UXO
clearance.



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 18 | P a g e
                                                                                                    Confidential
        ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT (ESIA) REPORT

During construction phase, dust, exhaust gas, noise, vibration; wastewater; solid waste;
ecosystem; labor influx; improper access, hard-working conditions, health and safety of
workers and locals; local flooding; local traffic; land subsidence, soil erosion; raw material
supply, etc. are generic impacts and risks of the project. These impacts can be considered as
low to moderate for each item and can be minimized by the application of Environmental and
Social Code of Practices (ESCOPs). In addition, the project implementation causes specific
impacts and risks, including impacts on domestic water supply, impacts on livelihoods, impacts
on water traffic, impacts by the relocation of infrastructure, impacts on ethnic minorities (EMs)
and impacts on sensitive receptors, etc. These impacts and risks can be mitigated by specific
measures including consultation with local authorities about the project activities and
construction plan, the arrangement of a temporary road, construction in the dry season and the
offseason for water taking; informing people of construction time; not using high-noise
machinery and equipment that emit a lot of dust and noise through the sensitive areas during
major religious holidays/study time; spraying sufficient water to reduce dust during dry and
windy days; providing safety measures as installation of fences, barriers warning signs, lighting
system against traffic accidents; prohibiting gathering of construction materials within 100m in
front of the sensitive objects; cleaning up construction areas at the end of the day, especially
construction around the sensitive structures; immediately addressing any issue/problem caused
by the construction activities; reinstating and providing adequate compensation for any impacts
which will be identified as caused by the construction activities.
During operation phase, the generic impacts and risks are dust, air emmision; noise and
vibration; domestic wastewater and solid waste; increase of traffic accidents; land use change;
and damage to traffic roads on the bridges over the sluices. The generic impacts and risks can
be mitigated by training and raising the awareness of the road and bridge’s users, arranging
suitable speed limit signs in the crowed areas; planting and maintaining trees on both road sides
to prevent noise from spreading into local people’s houses along the road; regular maintenance
of the project items, preparing an Emergency Contingency Plan; etc. In addition, the project
operation cause specific impacts and risks, including impacts on water drainage; risks of the
road damage; impacts due to electromagnetic field; degradation of surface water quality;
impacts of saline and/or brackish water on construction works; damage to sluice gates; increase
in water use conflict; impacts on aquatic life; impacts on waterway where the sluices are built;
impacts on flood drainage; canal bank erosion; risk that failure of livelihood models adversely
impacting welfare of beneficiary households; farmers’ resistance to adapting to livelihood
models; and Risk of land salinization. The impacts and risks are mitigated by specific measures
including communicate, raise local awareness of environmental protection, especially water
quality in canals; consulting local people and authorities about the operational procedure for
the sluice gate; early notify local people and authorities regarding sluice gate operation (opening
and closing times); conduct water/ecology monitoring at the monitoring sites to provide
baseline data prior to sluice gate operations, no later than 1 month before completion of
construction; collect packages, excessive foods during the argicultural and aquacultural
production; prohibit littering in surrounding area or water sources; etc.

Environmental and social management plan

The project’s ESMP includes measures to minimize negative impacts, roles and responsibilities
for ESMP implementation, monitoring and supervision, environmental compliance framework,
reporting organization, and control program. Environmental capacity building program and
ESMP implementation costs. The estimated cost is about 233,461 USD not including the cost



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 19 | P a g e
                                                                                                    Confidential
        ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT (ESIA) REPORT

of construction related mitigations measures which will be included in the civil works contract
during implementation.
During the construction process, ESMP requires the participation of a number of stakeholders
and agencies, each with a unique role and responsibility, including the An Giang PPMU,
Department of Agriculture & Environment of An Giang province, Contractor, Construction
supervision consultant (CSC), Independent environmental and social monitoring consultant
(IMC) and local community.

Public consultation and Information disclosure

Public consultation activities were conducted in 2 communes (Binh Giang and Binh Son) of
former Hon Dat district, Kien Giang (now An Giang) province January 2023. The consultation
was conducted with representatives of the government agencies and mass organizations, such
as: Representatives of People's Committee, Vietnam Fatherland Front Committee, Women's
Union, households affected by the project. The local authority and people in the project
communes were completely in agreement with the implementation of the project, which will
bring about socio-economic and environmental benefits. However, 2 affected communes all
require proper environmental sanitation during construction, traffic safety and travel condition,
control of dust and reinstatement of any damages on infrastructure.
All the draft E&S documents for the project (ESIA/ESMP, RP, EMDP, ESCP, LMP, and SEP)
in Vietnamese will be properly disclosed at the project sites, Kien Giang (now An Giang)
province’s website and the WB’s external website on 15 August 2025. The final project
safeguards instruments will be disclosed locally at the project sites, Kien Giang (now An Giang)
province’s website and the WBs external website on 15 September 2025 as well.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               20 | P a g e
                                                                                                  Confidential
         ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT (ESIA) REPORT



CHAPTER 1.                    PROJECT INTRODUCTION AND
                              DESCRIPTION
1.1. BACKGROUND AND OBJECTIVES OF THE PROJECT

1.1.1.   Background

1. Vietnam’s fisheries industry has grown to become one of the key export sectors driving the
country’s booming economy; concerted efforts will be needed, however, to address the
multitude of challenges and keep this momentum. The fisheries industry has been in a rapid
transformation becoming a commodity industry with its exports reaching close to US$ 9 billion
a year. Fishery and aquaculture commodities represent Vietnam’s 5th largest export products in
value, accounting for 4.1% of the country’s exports. The industry also contributes to
approximately 5% of the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) and provides a total of 4.7 million
jobs.
2. The ten-year fisheries sector development master plan currently in place has guided sectoral
development for the past decade. The 2013 Fisheries Development Master Plan to 2020 with
Vision to 2030 envisions the fisheries sector to become a commodity production sector with
prestigious brands and high competitiveness at the international level. The revised 2017 Law
on Fisheries provides robust legal foundations for sustainable sectoral development; its
enforcement is critical going forward. Institutionally, the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural
Development (MARD), now the Ministry of Agriculture and Environment (MAE) and the 28
coastal provinces play key roles in fisheries governance and sectoral development. The
Government of Vietnam (GoV) has proposed to the World Bank (WB) to finance the
Sustainable Fishery Development Project (SFDP) to invest in ‘smart’ infrastructure to enable
improved management of and increased value from target fisheries. The total cost of the
project is US$414 million. The proposed project will be implemented from 2025 - 2026. The
Project comprises of four components:
  Component 1: Improving Fisheries Governance for Value Addition [IBRD: US$30 million;
   GoV: US$ 7 million].
  Component 2: Boosting Sustainable Fisheries with Smart National Infrastructure Upgrades
   [IBRD: US$150 million; GoV: US$ 37 million].
  Component   3: Boosting Sustainable Fisheries with Smart Subnational infrastructure
   development [IBRD: US$ 150 million; GoV: US$ 30 million].
  Component 4: Project management, monitoring, and evaluation [GoV: US$10 million].

3. Each of the components will be implemented by the Ministry of Agriculture and
Environment (MAE) at the central level as well as the participating provinces including Hai
Phong, Thanh Hoa, Soc Trang (now Can Tho), Bac Lieu (now Ca Mau), Ca Mau and Kien
Giang (now An Giang). Kien Giang sustainable fisheries development project is under
Component 3.
4. Kien Giang (now An Giang) is a coastal province of the Mekong Delta (MD) with a
relatively long coastline (about 200 kilometers) that is directly affected by climate change (CC),
particularly sea level rise (SLR). After many years of transitioning the production structure
from rice cultivation to a combination of rice and aquaculture, the value of products produced


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                21 | P a g e
                                                                                                   Confidential
         ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT (ESIA) REPORT

in the same conversion zone has increased. The transformation structure is gradually being
implemented in the right direction, resulting in the creation of several effective coastal rural
areas and the prospect of agriculture that combines sustainable, comprehensive, and
harmonious development with nature. Currently, the province has more than 90,000ha of
aquaculture, with nearly 71,500ha dedicated to rice-aquaculture farming.
5. The central government has recently mobilized several capital sources for Kien Giang (now
An Giang) province to carry out transportation and irrigation projects for aquaculture
development. This has improved investment efficiency, as well as socioeconomic development
and social security of the province. However, due to a lack of capital, no coastal area in the
province has been adequately and fully invested in infrastructures for optimal value-added
production.
6. In addition, the situation of riverbank erosion, coastal erosion, saltwater intrusion, etc. in
recent years in the province has become complicated and the trend is becoming more and more
serious. The province has implemented solutions to urgently overcome serious landslides in
some coastal sections, affecting the dyke body, saltwater prevention and freshwater sluice
structures, etc. However, this requires investment costs. The investment is very large and the
province has not been able to meet it, so there are still many sections with particularly dangerous
landslides that have not been treated. This has greatly affected people's lives, production, and
coastal infrastructure.
7. Therefore, investing in project “Kien Giang sustainable fisheries development project” to
promote socio-economic development of the province and the region to adapt to climate
change, protect the environment and gradually associate with the development of sustainable,
quality and diverse fisheries is necessary.

1.1.2.    Project Objectives

1.1.2.1. Overall objective

8.   The overall objective of the project is to strengthen fisheries management and increase
product value through smart infrastructure upgrades.

1.1.2.2. Specific objectives

9.     The project aims to the following specific objectives:
  To build and improve infrastructure, innovate technology, strengthen management capacity,
     and organize production to promote the fisheries sector in the direction of value
     enhancement, deep international integration, environmental friendliness, and adaptation to
     climate change.
  To encourage and attract all economic sectors to contribute to the sustainable development
     of the province's fishery sector, and promote socio-economic development associated with
     environmental protection.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 22 | P a g e
                                                                                                    Confidential
         ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT (ESIA) REPORT

1.1.3.    Project components

1.1.3.1. Structural components (SCs)

10. The project is to build new basic infrastructure and upgrading current infrastructure for
shrimp farming area in the south of NH80 of Hon Dat district (formerly), including:
  Building 23 sluice gates with a width of 8 to 10m (including 3 bridges of HL93 load each; 8
   concrete bridge of 0.65HL93 load each; and 12 pedestrian bridges of girder rural road I400
   each) and 4 management houses to separate fresh and brackish water areas for agricultural
   restructuring, aquaculture, and rice cultivation (see details in Table 2);
  Building a delta grade-V road with a total length of about 7.03 km (running along the East
   bank of Binh Giang 1 canal connecting NH80 to the sea dyke);
  Installing 25.18km (including 21.54km medium-voltage power lines, 3.64km low-voltage
   power lines), and 30 transformer stations to operate sluices;
  Upgrading the gates and operation devices of 5 sluices along the sea dike, and 13 in-field
   sluices along the Don Dong canal for the current aquaculture area in the project area;
  Other ancillary works.

b) The connecting road

14. The project’s connecting road has a length of 7.03km and 36 culverts along the road to
take water from the Binh Giang 1 canal into the fields.
  Technical specifications of the road:

        Delta V road;
        Calculated axle load: 100kN;
        Length: L=7.03km;
        Centerline elevation: +2.5m to +3.2m.
  Cross-sectional scale of the road (Figure 4):

        Width of the carriageway         : 2.75m x 2 = 5.5m;
        Sidewalk width                   : 1.75m x 2 = 3.5m;
         ++ Reinforced sidewalk           : 1.25m x 2 = 2.5m;
         ++ Soil sidewalk                 : 0.50m x 2 = 1.0m;
         Total                            : 9.0m;
  Road surface structure:

        3-layer asphalt, 4.5cm thick, asphalt standard 5.5kg/m 2;
        Crushed stone 15cm thick, K ≥ 0.98;
        Type I crushed stone aggregate, 20cm thick, K ≥ 0.98;
        Geotextile strength 12 kN/m;
        Backfill sand with compacted upper layer of 50cm K ≥ 0.98;
        Backfill sand with compacted lower layer K ≥ 0.95;

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                           27   Page
                                                                                           Confidential
                    ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

          Peel off weathering 30cm thick, cover with sand to K ≥ 0.95.




Figure 4: Cross-section of the connecting road
15. On the road, 36 new water intake culverts will be installed at the existing culverts. These
provide water from Binh Giang 1 canal to the fields (Figure 5). The culverts are made of
centrifugal concrete with a diameter D=0.6m, a length of 20m and load-bearing of H30-XB80.
The culverts are placed on abutments reinforced with Melaleuca piles with a density of 16
piles/m2.




Figure 5: Typical longitudinal section of a culvert of D600

18. There are 12 households to be relocated due to land acquisition of the project. However,
there will be no construction of resettlement areas, the displaced households will be resettled at
the current residential line along the Binh Giang 1 road if they want. The residential line was
invested by state fund and be managed by Kien Giang (now An Giang) PPC. It has about vacant
land plots and sufficient for the project’s displaced households (DHs).

1.1.3.2. Non-structural activities

19.        Non-structural solutions implemented in the frame of the project:
  Establish and strengthen production cooperation groups, including:

           Farmers’ groups to manage their members’s activities in the direction of green agro
            products.
           Cooperatives to negotiate the formation of production chains, and operate the chains
            effectively via the application of national and international standards into the
            production.
  Disseminate the activities of the project to build consensus and support from the people,
   reduce the rate of people utilizing groundwater in agricultural production, and enhance
   people's understanding of production linked with natural resource protection and
   regeneration, including:
           Workshops, seminars, and propaganda panels to share experiences and communication
            of the project in 02 project communes.
           Propaganda on mass media about the project.
  Develop demonstration livelihood models.

  Conduct training classes, transfer production techniques to improve capacity, and apply
   scientific and technical advances to meet market needs and reduce greenhouse gas
   emissions.
  Connect and replicate the project livelihood models, introduce and register product offtake
   businesses to help farmers apply sustainable and effective livelihood models that meet the
   needs of product offtake businesses and reduce production costs by the procurement of

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                     30    Page
                                                                                                       Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   substantial quantities of goods from retailers who supply goods of reputable origin and
   quality, including:
        At least 2 value chains (1 chain for one commune), with signed product offtake contracts
         for rice output certified green standards such as VietGAP, SRP, Organic, ASC, BAP.
        At least 2 value chains (1 chain for one commune), with signed product offtake contracts
         for rice output certified green standards such as VietGAP, SRP, Organic, ASC, BAP.
  Supporttraining, consultation, and certification of national and international standards
   VietGAP, SRP, Organic, ASC, BAP) requested by the businesses of the value chains.
  Support the application of smart technology to adapt to climate change in production and
   apply technology in production to minimize chemical usage and carbon emissions.
  Building electronic traceability - QR code for 04 product chains.

  Evaluate the effectiveness of project livelihood models.


1.1.3.3. Auxiliaries of the project

20. Construction sites: Project construction sites will be established for the sluice system.
Construction sites will be mostly located on agricultural land, vacant land, or public land of the
communes and will include worker camps for 10-20 workers (including 10 workers for each
sluice and 5-10 workers for nearby road sections and power lines), machinery gathering yards,
material storage yards, etc.
21. The construction sites for the electrical systems and the road will be situated in the
adjacent sluice construction sites. When required, construction materials will be gathered along
the existing roads while ensuring that local traffic flow remains smooth.

1.1.4.    Master Planning and Related Projects in the Project Areas

1.1.4.1. Master planning

a) Relationships of the project and other project plans and programs approved by the state
   agencies

22. According to Kien Giang (now An Giang) province's plan No. 46/KH-UBND dated March
21, 2019, high-tech areas for intensive shrimp farming and large-scale areas for shrimp-rice and
improved extensive shrimp farming will be established with reasonable organization and
synchronous infrastructure and technical services in the period of 2021 and 2025. The total area
of brackish shrimp farming in Kien Giang (now An Giang) province is expected to reach
129,450ha during this period, with industrial and semi-industrial shrimp farming accounting
for 10,000 ha, shrimp-rice farming for 94,400 ha, and extensive shrimp farming for 25,050 ha.
The estimated contribution of the aquaculture sector to the Gross Regional Domestic Product
(GRDP) of Kien Giang province is expected to reach around 58-60% by 2025. The southern
region of NH80 (from Rach Gia to Ba Hon - Kien Luong) is planned for the development of
brackish water shrimp farming with a total area of 29,000 hectares, of which 21,000 hectares is
for Hon Dat district (former), including the transition/development of 5,500 hectares for
brackish water shrimp farming.
23. Considering the local circumstances, it is crucial to invest in infrastructure that supports
fishery exploitation and aquaculture. This will help the province and region with socio-

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               31   Page
                                                                                               Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

economic development, adapt to climate change, protect the environment, and integrate with
the sustainable, high-quality, and diverse development of the aquaculture sector. Therefore, the
project is in line with the real production needs at the local level and both central and local
government planning and production development directions (Table 6).

       Giang (now An Giang) province       is also prioritized.

b) Consistent with the socio-economic development trends of the project area

24. The project region has a high potential for socioeconomic growth. However, there has
been insufficient and disjointed funding for the building of basic infrastructure, particularly
hydraulic works. Therefore, this area remains largely untapped. Every year, the entire area
suffers from severe saltwater intrusion and a shortage of fresh water, posing numerous
challenges to agricultural production, including low productivity, unstable outputs, and
disruption of seasonal schedules. This leads to low socioeconomic growth in the area. Due to
the poor quality of groundwater in the area, it is critical to provide fresh water for domestic,
industrial, and aquacultural activities while also limiting saline intrusion.
25. Gradually closing the western coastal dike in Kien Giang (now An Giang) is crucial in
light of the current circumstances, as it serves to proactively regulate salinity, retain freshwater,
prevent inundation, and mitigate water pollution. This aligns with the objectives of the Program
to Consolidate and Upgrade the Coastal Dyke System from Quang Ngai to Kien Giang (now
An Giang) under Decision No. 667/QD-TTg dated May 27, 2009, by the Prime Minister, while
also creating conditions for the socio-economic development of Hon Dat district (former)
according to the overall socio-economic development plan of Kien Giang (now An Giang)
province.
26. It is important to change the way production works to fit the changing water conditions
in the project area. Since the salinity intrusion incident in 2016, farmers have been shifting from
growing 2 crops of rice to brackish aquaculture or shrimp-rice rotation. For example, in Binh
Giang commune, 1,100 hectares have been shifted over to brackish aquaculture, with 20
hectares set aside for industrial shrimp farming. Based on how efficiently production is going
now, this is a good way to adapt to changes in production conditions and also make production
more valuable.
27. Furthermore, the project area is included in the province of Kien Giang (now An Giang)'s
plan to transition to new production models (such as rice-shrimp farming). This is because it is
an area suitable for shrimp farming under saline conditions (provided there are infrastructure
solutions to control salinity without adversely affecting the freshwater ecosystem in the
northern part of NH80). This transition, part of the province's larger strategy, presents
significant opportunities for production and commodity consumption. As a result, economic
improvement is inevitable.

c) Consistent with the current production situation of the area

28. The project area encompasses roughly 5,500 hectares of natural land, with around 3,400
hectares for rice cultivation and 20 hectares for industrial shrimp (Figure 6). The predominant
farming model in this area remains centered on two rice crops (two rice crops and one
freshwater aquaculture crop). According to research findings on production within the project
area, the present production model yields average earnings of 30 to 40 million VND per hectare.



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                  34   Page
                                                                                                  Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

29. Since approximately 2015, production in the region between the Don Dong canal and the
coastal dike has transitioned from rice to rice-shrimp. It is worth mentioning that the rice-shrimp
cultivation model has expanded to encompass an estimated 1,100 hectares as of 2019. The rice-
shrimp cultivation model offers several benefits, including sustainability, environmental
pollution reduction, and adaptation to the challenges posed by rising sea levels and increased
saltwater intrusion. Additionally, the rice-shrimp farming demonstrates higher efficiency
compared to monoculture rice farming. According to research conducted in the coastal rice-
shrimp farming areas of the MD, this model generates earnings ranging from 38 to 68 million
VND per hectare. Specifically, replacing one rice crop with one shrimp crop can result in
revenues of 25 to 30 million VND per hectare. Hence, as a production paradigm, rice-shrimp
farming can boost economic efficiency per farmed area in comparison to monoculture rice
farming. In this farming model, the main intercropping target is shrimp.




Figure 6: Map of current argicultural landuse in the Long Xuyen Quadrangle

d) Consistent with the water conditions in the area

30. Production in the project area primarily relies on rainwater and Hau river’s freshwater.
This region often experiences freshwater shortages during the dry season, exacerbated by
saltwater intrusion and prolonged heat, leading to freshwater scarcity for production.
Additionally, being situated along the western coast, drainage during heavy rainfall can be
challenging due to low tide levels. Some areas may experience inundation for less than a week.
In neighboring areas, production still predominantly follows freshwater ecosystems, with rice
being the main crop.
31. Brackish aquaculture farming is gradually replacing mono-rice growing in the area
between the Don Dong canal and the West Sea dike. The shifted area is defined by the farming
Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                35   Page
                                                                                                Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

size with closed embankments. Farmers who want to produce brackish aquaculture in places
without closed embankments must build temporary dams and close sluices to separate fresh and
saline water.
32. Furthermore, there is currently no electricity or transportation infrastructure in this area
to facilitate the development of industrial shrimp farming and market access. As a result,
investment in infrastructure is required to improve input material quality and productivity
through better aquaculture management and increase product value. This contributes to
increased capacity and technical support for the adoption of innovative sustainable and
ecologically friendly management, exploitation, and farming technologies.
33. Additionally, in this area, there is currently no electricity system or transportation system
to support the development of industrial shrimp farming and connect to markets. Therefore, to
improve the quality of input materials, and increase productivity through better aquaculture
management for added value of the products, investment in basic infrastructure is needed.
34. In the future, changes in Mekong River water caused by upstream hydroelectric activities,
sea level rise, and land subsidence will have a severe impact on the MD's freshwater supplies.
Current trends indicate a decrease in water flow at the end of the rainy season (due to
hydropower reservoirs), resulting in earlier saltwater intrusion and increased water scarcity in
the MD during the dry season.
35. The impact of sea level rise also manifests as abnormal weather changes, prolonged
heatwaves, and the periodic occurrence of El Nino, posing a risk of saltwater intrusion and
drought in most of the agricultural areas in the MD. Additionally, rising sea levels lead to
increased saltwater intrusion further inland, especially during the dry season, affecting surface
water in rivers and canals, and even groundwater sources, making them susceptible to
salinization.
36. Furthermore, the production transition and development in the MD will increase water
usage, exacerbating water shortages, particularly in downstream and coastal areas.
Consequently, saltwater intrusion will become more predominant. Thus, transitioning
production to adapt to changes in water sources is imperative, especially in coastal areas such
as those along NH80 in Kien Giang (now An Giang) province.

1.1.4.2. Associated Facilities (AFs)

37. The Project will have impacts on land acquisition causing the relocation of 12 households.
In this project, there will be no construction of resettlement areas, displaced households will be
resettled at the current residential line along the Binh Giang 1 road. The residential line is being
managed by the State.
38. The residential line is considered an associated facility because it is: (a) directly and
significantly related to the project; (b) carried out, or planned to be carried out,
contemporaneously with the project; and (c) necessary for the project to be viable and would
not have been constructed, expanded or conducted if the project did not exist.
39. The residential line is considered as a AF of the project. As a result, the line will be
subjected to a due diligence report to review any outstanding issues throughout its land
acquisition and resettlement execution.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 36   Page
                                                                                                 Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

1.1.4.3. Other Projects in Proposed Project Area

40.   There are no on-going projects and future projects proposed in the project area.

1.2.3.      Relevant World Bank Environmental and Social Standards and Guidelines

48. The World Bank Environmental and Social Framework 2 (ESF), which became effective on
October 1, 2018, sets out the WB’s commitment to sustainable development, through a Bank
Policy and a set of Environmental and Social Standards (ESSs) that are designed to support
Borrowers’ projects, to end extreme poverty and promote shared prosperity.
49. The Bank is committed to supporting Borrowers in the development and implementation
of projects that are environmentally and socially sustainable, and to enhancing the capacity of
Borrowers’ environmental and social frameworks to assess and manage the environmental and
social risks and impacts of projects. To this end, the Bank has defined ten specific
Environmental and Social Standards (ESSs), which are designed to avoid, minimize, reduce, or
mitigate the adverse environmental and social risks and impacts of projects.
50. The project’s environmental and social risk has been rated as Moderate. Nine out of the ten
Environmental and Social Standards (ESSs) in the ESF are relevant to the project, including:
     ESS1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts

     ESS2: Labor and Working Conditions

     ESS3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management

     ESS4: Community Health and Safety

     ESS5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement

     ESS6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources

     ESS7: Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local
     Communities
     ESS8: Cultural Heritage

     ESS10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure

51. The scope of application of ESSs is presented below:

1.2.3.1. ESSs applicable for the project

a) ESS1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts

52. The ESS1 requires the Borrower to be responsible for assessing, managing, and monitoring
E&S risks and impacts associated with each stage of a project supported by WB to achieve
E&S outcomes consistent with the ESSs.
53. For the project, it is expected that the project will bring about numerous environmental
benefits, i.e. positive impact on waste via investments and better regulatory enforcement. The
overall project E&S risks are assessed as moderate and the reasons are as follows:
     On the social side, the main social risks and impacts would consist of loss of lands, assets
     on land, livelihoods, and other properties due to temporary and permanent land acquisition
     and relocation; disruption in the livelihood activities of farmers (due to
     upgrading/construction and operation of aquaculture infrastructure, and improved
     enforcement capacity); occupational health and safety (OHS), gender-based violence (GBV)

2
    https://www.worldbank.org/en/projects-operations/environmental-and-social-framework

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                      44   Page
                                                                                                      Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

   and Sexual Exploitation or Abuse (SEA)/Sexual Harassment (SH) due to labor influx; and
   related risks of community safety and disruption; increase of water use conflict between rice
   farming and brackish water aquaculture; farmers’ resistance to adapting in livelihood
   models; risk that failure of livelihood models adversely impacting welfare of beneficiary
   households; uneven access to project benefits among vulnerable groups such as poor
   households, female headed households, and EM households; relocation of graves; and risks
   to human health due to potential overuse of antibiotics in livelihood activities. Although the
   potential impacts/risks of the project are diverse, they are all manageable and could be
   mitigated/compensated through appropriate E&S assessment and mitigation plans to be
   developed during the project preparation or implementation.
  The   main environmental risks and impacts during the project implementation include
   (i) safety risk relating to UXOs; (ii) environmental pollution due to generation of dust, waste,
   waste water and excavated materials; (iii) water quality degradation and impacts to aquatic
   species; (iv) risks of erosion and subsidence during construction process; (v) impacts on
   areas of sensitivity such as natural habitats and modified natural habitats; (vi) risks of erosion
   and subsidence during the construction of sluices and traffic roads. The key environmental
   risks and impacts during operation include (i) soil erosion and embankment subsidence; (ii)
   threatening to aquatic biodiversity due to potentially escaping artificial and alien species into
   water bodies; (iii) contamination of aquatic systems, water sources due to
   chemicals/antibiotics usage for shrimp cultivation/breeding area and untreated wastewater
   directly discharged into the environment; (iv) air pollution and noise due to operation of
   vehicles on land; and (v) water pollution and disturbance of aquatic life due to improper
   waste management. The risks and impacts are predictable, mostly moderate, localized, and
   could be mitigated and managed. The implementing agencies have certain experience with
   safeguard policies but do not have knowledge and experience in the application of ESF
   during project implementation.
  Given the type, location, sensitivity of the project, and the capacity of the implementing
   agency, the potential environmental and social risks and impacts are rated as Moderate.
54. Although Vietnam has an advanced E&S Framework, there are gaps between the national
environmental and social assessment (ESA) regulation and practice and good international
practice. Discussion on the ESSs application to the project is provided below while Table 9
describes key gaps between the national EIA requirements and the ESSs.

b) ESS2: Labor and Working Conditions

55. ESS2 recognizes the importance of employment creation and income generation in the
pursuit of poverty reduction and inclusive economic growth. Project owner scan promotes
sound worker-management relationships and enhances the development benefits of the project
by treating workers in the project fairly and providing safe, secure, and healthy working
conditions.
56. With the current design, it is expected that the project activities will require the use of
direct, contracted, and Primary Supply workers (local workers are prioritized) to support the
project management, and to carry out the upgrading of existing sluice gates, construction
sluices, rural roads, and installation of medium and low voltage lines. It is estimated that the
construction/upgrading project items are scattered and mostly of small and medium work scale
with the number of workers at each construction site estimated at about 5-10 people. Most risks


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                  45   Page
                                                                                                  Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

relate to occupational and community health and safety issues, and other risks associated with
the construction activities including OHS, GBV/SEA/SH, and to the operation of project items.
57. To be consistent with the ESS2 requirement, as part of the ESA process, a project Labor
Management Procedures (LMP) has been prepared as a stand-alone document and referred to
in the project ESIA. The LMP sets out how the project workers will be managed under the
requirements of national laws and ESS2.

c) ESS3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management

58. The ESS recognizes that economic activity, and extensive agricultural and fisheries
production often generate pollution to air, water, and land, and consume finite resources that
may threaten people, ecosystem services, and the environment at the local, regional, and global
levels. This ESS sets out the requirements to address resource efficiency and pollution
prevention and management throughout the project life cycle. Given the type and scale of the
project, several resources and construction materials will be required, and adverse impacts on
human health and the environment are expected to be moderate. The risks and impacts related
to the released pollutants, waste generation, management of the use of antibiotics and
chemicals, impacts on the community, and resource use efficiency will be assessed and
mitigation and management measures will be proposed during the preparation of each project
as part of project Environmental and Social Assessment (ESA) process in the sections below.
59. The excavated materials from sluices' foundation construction could be polluted with
organic substances or naturally polluted by acidic sulfate. However, the amount of excavated
soil in each sluice is small and it can be reused for backfilling. Therefore, the adverse impacts
and risks relating to excavating, storage, transportation, and disposal of excavated materials
will be small.
60. Contamination of aquatic systems during operation by chemical residues may include the
remains of veterinary drugs (e.g. antibiotics) that may have been applied to the cultivated
species, and toxic substances such as formalin and malachite green, a cancer-causing agent, that
may have been that are used to treat finfish for parasites and their eggs for fungal growth. Risk
of exposure to hazardous materials and chemicals such as a variety of chemicals may be used
in the operation of an aquaculture facility to treat and/or control disease organisms or to
facilitate production (e.g. lime, diluted chlorine, or salt). To mitigate these risks and impacts,
an Integrated Pest Management Plan has been included in the project ESIA process.
61. The project implementation will produce emissions due to exhaust gases during
construction and operation, an estimate of gross Green House Gas (GHG) emissions will be
required, provided that such estimation is technically and financially feasible. The project will
adopt measures, specified in the WBG EHSG and other Good International Industry Practices
(GIIP), for efficient use of raw materials and optimization of energy use, to the extent
technically and financially feasible.

d) ESS4: Community Health and Safety

62. The ESS4 addresses the health, safety, and security risks and impacts on project-affected
communities and the corresponding responsibility of the project owner to avoid or minimize
such risks and impacts, with particular attention to people who, because of their circumstances,
may be vulnerable. Given its nature and scale, the project is not expected to discharge a large
number of hazardous materials, or dredged materials without proper management affecting

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               46   Page
                                                                                               Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

community health. It is also not expected to expose local people to any high or substantial risks
of waterway and road traffic accidents, electricity uses as well as social disturbance due to labor
influx. The project does not involve the construction or rehabilitation of dams or depend on
water supply from existing dams.
63. Therefore, standard measures in the World Bank Group’s Environment, Health, and Safety
Guidelines (EHSG) to ensure the community health and safety of communities during the
construction and operation of project-financed infrastructure should suffice. These include
measures in general facility design and operation, communication and training, and the
measures to address physical hazards, chemical hazards, personal protective equipment, special
hazard environments, and monitoring. In addition, measures to prevent Gender-based violence
(GBV), sexual exploitation, abuse, and harassment (SEA/SH) will be implemented, including
requiring workers to sign a Code of Conduct and establishing a Grievance Redress Mechanism
(GRM) capable of receiving and referring SEA/SH-related complaints in a safe and confidential
manner.

e) ESS5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement

64. This ESS recognizes the adverse impact of the project on local people, especially those
considered to be vulnerable or disadvantageous. Involuntary resettlement will be avoided.
Where involuntary resettlement is unavoidable, it will be minimized and appropriate measures
to mitigate adverse impacts on displaced persons (and on host communities receiving displaced
persons) will be carefully planned and implemented.
65. Given that sluice gate improvement will be carried out within the confines of existing sluice
gates, no land will be acquired for the items. The installation of medium and low-voltage power
lines mainly uses temporary land during construction. Only the construction of sluices and
traffic routes acquire land with a total acquired land of 15.46ha. This lead to the relocation of
12 households, permanent loss of approximately 93,907 trees of various types, as well as on-
land assets such as fences, yards, mooring areas, and drilled wells. Using the mitigation
hierarchy, the alternative analysis during the project designing stage will be made to avoid or
reduce the land acquisition and restriction of land use as much as possible to minimize the
negative physical and socioeconomic impacts on local people especially the vulnerable, RP and
EMDP has been prepared in compliance with the project RPF and EMPF.

f) ESS6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural
   Resources

66. The ESS6 recognizes that protecting and conserving biodiversity and sustainably managing
living natural resources are fundamental to sustainable development and it recognizes the
importance of maintaining core ecological functions of habitats, including forests, riverine,
littoral, coastal, and marine areas, and the biodiversity they support. The ESS6 also addresses
sustainable management of primary production and harvesting of living natural resources and
recognizes the need to consider the livelihood of project-affected parties, including Indigenous
Peoples whose access to, or use of, biodiversity or living natural resources may be affected by
the project.
67. As the project's nature and scope, some activities of the project will be carried out on the
existing canals and rivers that have been used as waterways for fish capturing, storm sheltering;
and water supply and drainage for fishery cultivating and breeding. These rivers and canals can

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                47   Page
                                                                                                Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

be considered aquatic ecosystems and homes for aquatic species but would be classified as
modified habitats under ESS6. All project construction activities are not expected to pose any
adverse impacts on the natural conservation/protected areas.
68. The relevance of this ESS will be further discussed and assessed below. Mitigation
measures will be in place to protect or minimize the adverse impacts on aquatic ecosystems
during construction (e.g. increased water turbidity, accidental pollution from spillage of
construction materials, and leakage of oil and grease).

g) ESS7: Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional
   Local Communities

69. The ESS7 aims to ensure that the development process fosters full respect for the human
rights, dignity, aspirations, identity, culture, and natural resource-based livelihoods of ethnic
minorities (equivalent definition to Indigenous Peoples). ESS7 is also meant to avoid adverse
impacts of projects, or when avoidance is not possible, to minimize, mitigate, and/or
compensate for such impacts.
70. A number of EM households including Khmer, have been living with King people in the
project area for years. Therefore, an EMDP has been prepared as a stand-alone document and
referred to this project ESIA. The EMDP is consistent with the project EMPF.

h) ESS8: Cultural Heritage

71. The ESS8 recognizes that cultural heritage provides continuity in tangible and intangible
forms between the past, present, and future. It sets out measures designed to protect cultural
heritage throughout the project life cycle. Given the nature, scale, and location of the Project,
it is not expected to cause significant adverse impacts on tangible and intangible cultural
heritage. As the project may involve a moderate scale of excavation/dredging activities, there
is a potential for chance finds during the Project construction period. A chance finds procedure
has been included in the project ESMF and will be included in the project ESMP.

i) ESS10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure

72. The ESS10 recognizes the importance of open and transparent engagement between the
project owner and project stakeholders as an essential element of good international practice.
Effective stakeholder engagement can improve the environmental and social sustainability of
projects, enhance project acceptance, and make a significant contribution to successful project
design and implementation. For the Project, key stakeholders are the beneficiary communities
and households, the project-affected people, the implementing agencies at the national level
(MOF, MOC, MOIT, MEA, Ministry of Home Affairs (MOHA), and Ministry of Ethnic and
Religious Affairs (MOERA), etc.), the local authorities (including PPC, DAE, Department of
Home Affairs (DOHA), and Department of Ethnic and Religious Affairs (DOERA), DOF,
DOC, DOIT and CPCs of Binh Giang and Binh Son).
73. A Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) has been developed for the project. This is to ensure
transparency and meaningful consultation with the affected and interested parties. Stakeholder
engagement and consultations will be conducted throughout the project cycle. This includes
discussions of project design and impacts among potential beneficiary communities, as well as
provincial-level multi-stakeholder discussions on these issues during the preparation phase.


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                              48   Page
                                                                                              Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

Due attention will be given to identifying the “disadvantaged or vulnerable” project-affected
individuals or groups during stakeholder identification and analysis. The SEP, along with other
E&S instruments, will be subject to public consultation and disclosure per requirements of
ESS10 and will be treated as a live document to be regularly updated along the pace of project
implementation. Also, the SEP outlines the GRM for the project.

1.2.3.2. Other policies

74. The Project is also subject to comply with the legal operational policies on Projects on
International Waterways (OP/BP 7.50).
75. World Bank Group Environmental, Health, and Safety Guidelines 3 will also apply to this
project:
  World Bank-financed projects should also consider the World Bank Group Environmental,
   Health, and Safety Guidelines (known as the "EHS Guidelines"). The EHS Guidelines are
   technical reference documents with general and industry-specific examples of Good
   International Industry Practice.
  The   EHS Guidelines contain the performance levels and measures that are normally
   acceptable to the World Bank Group and are generally considered to be achievable in new
   facilities at reasonable costs by existing technology. The environmental assessment process
   may recommend alternative (higher or lower) levels or measures, which, if acceptable to the
   World Bank, become project- or site-specific requirements. This project should conform to
   the general EHS Guidelines and industry-specific EHS Guidelines on Water and Sanitationn.

1.2.4.    Gap Analysis between Vietnam Legal Regulations and the WB's ESF

76. Vietnam's regulations on environmental and social assessment are different from the WB's
ESF. Although Vietnam has made efforts to narrow the differences, there are still differences
between the two systems. Table 9 describes the differences and gap-filling measures.

                       impacts. The Bank carry out regular                   coordinate with Construction Management
                       implementation support mission including E&S          Agencies to supervise the compliance of
                       implementation monitoring. The E&S monitoring         environmental management activities stated in EIA.
                       and reporting is based on provisions and
                       arrangement specified in the loan agreement and
                       described in the other project documentation, to
                       determine whether the Borrower’s compliance
                       with environmental covenant (primarily with
                       EMP) is satisfactory.
                      Based on the result of monitoring, any corrective
                       and preventive actions will be identified and
                       incorporated into an amended ESCP in a manner
                       acceptable to the Bank. The borrower will
                       implement, monitor and report on these actions
                       following the amended ESCP.

                         ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

1.3. PROJECT DESCRIPTION

1.3.1.       Project location

77. The Project will be carried out in the communes of Binh Giang and Binh Son of former
Hon Dat district, Kien Giang province (now An Giang). It will cover an area of about 5,500ha
and is limited by the Vam Ray Canal, Canal 200 (which runs along NH80), Tuan Thong Canal,
and the West Sea dyke, as follows (Figure 7):
  To the northeast is Canal 200 (near NH 80);

  To the northwest is Tuan Thong canal;

  The Southeast is Vam Ray canal;

  The southwest is the West Sea dike.

78. Layout of the project items are depicted in Figure 8.

1.3.1.2. Location of the connecting road

80. The connecting road runs above the existing embankment on the left bank of the Binh
Giang 1 canal and connects to NH80 and the sea dike (Table 11 and Figure 9):
  The section from the start of NH80 to Canal 200: the route links to NH80 near Ranh Hat
   Pagoda (between Binh Giang 1 bridge and Ranh Hat bridge). The route runs behind the
   residential houses along NH80, and is adjacent to the supporting wire of the
   telecommunication tower, about 40m from NH80 towards the sea. This segment deviates
   from the Binh Giang 1 canal embankment due to obstructions caused by the Cultural House
   of Ranh Hat hamlet (Figure 10).
Table 11: Coordination of the road
                                                                    Coordinates VN2000 104030’
  No.                       Descriptions                                  Projection zone 30
                                                                      X (m)                 Y (m)
      1     Starting point connecting to NH80                      1137220.33            527939.92
      2     End point connecting the sea dike                      1131080.07            524877.37
  The section along Canal 200 doesn't follow the existing bank along Binh Giang 1 canal
   because of the Ranh Hat hamlet cultural house, the ongoing Ranh Hat water plant project,
   and the new Canal 200 bridge (rural traffic bridge with vehicle lane width of 3m). Instead,
   it's skewed toward the field, 5 to 12m from the Canal 200 bridge.




Figure 9: The connecting road location on Google Earth
  The section from canal 200 to Don Dong canal: the route runs close to the embankment
   along Binh Giang 1 canal, in front of a row of residential houses, and parallel to the existing
   road, about 12 to 15m from the edge of the Binh Giang 1 canal (Figure 11).


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                        59   Page
                                                                                                        Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
  The section from Don Dong canal to the canal along the sea dike:

    The sub-section near Don Dong canal: The sub-section follows the embankment of the
     Binh Giang 1 canal, toward the fields and around 10-15 meters from the Binh Giang 1
     canal embankment, about 12 to 15m from the edge of the Binh Giang 1 canal (Figure 12).
    The sub-section near the canal along the sea dyke: the sub-section close to the embankment
     of the Binh Giang 1 canal, in front of Giong Ke Secondary School and a row of residential
     houses along the existing road, about 7 to 10m from the edge of the Binh Giang 1 canal
     (Figure 13).
  The segment intersecting with the canal along the dike follows the Binh Giang 1 canal
   embankment and coincides with the existing rural traffic bridge with a vehicle lane width of
   2m, which has been in operation for a long time and is presently being degraded.
  The section from the canal along the sea dike to the sea dike: the route connects to the road
   over the sea dike at Km 21+450 towards Rach Gia City (Figure 14).
