1.3.3.    Construction methods

1.3.3.1. Construction of the connecting road

  Positioning the road;

  Excavating and peeling the surface soil and excavating road molds;

  Filling the roadbed with sand in each layer, combined with foundation reinforcement at
   design locations;
  Building the road surface in layers;

  Installing traffic lights and signing boards;

  Completing the road and handing over the road.


1.3.3.2. Sluice gate construction

a) Flow diversion

85. The project’s sluices are located within the production area, which contains an intricate
network of canals with few boats going through. In theory, when constructing the major
portions of a sluice, it is feasible to block the entire flow for a length of time. However, entirely
obstructing the canal bed will cause environmental problems as well as interfere with nearby
residents’water intake needs. As a result, a flow diversion solution is required to ensure that
environmental flow continues over the construction site.
86. The construction of a sluice is anticipated to require a duration of around 10-12 months.
Out of these tasks, the construction of the sluice body, which involves redirecting the flow of
water, will take around 6 months. In order to adhere to the planned timetable, it is anticipated
that the sluice body will be built within a steel sheet pile cofferdam located directly in the canal
bed. This will include diverting the flow of water through the remaining space on both banks,
taking into account the size, structure, and flow diversion needs of the sluice body.
87. In addition, sluices shall be built close to each other at the same time, but rather
alternately, to provide water diversion and water supply for production.

b) Main construction steps

88.   Construction of hydraulic structures at site:
  Step 1: Build construction roads and electricity lines to the construction site.

  Step  2: Implement the construction categories, material supply for abutments, and
   preliminary dredging of the minimum channel for equipment to be used for construction.
  Step 3: Pile the bottom of the sluice, bridge abutments, and load-reducing floors.

  Step 4: Install waterproofing piles of sheet steel for the construction.

  Step 5: Construct the frame in stages to pour concrete into the sluice compartment.

  Step 6: Install prestressed piles and reinforced piles of sluice sides and embankment walls.

  Step 7: Pour concrete into plinth beams and floors of sluice sides.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                   71   Page
                                                                                                   Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
  Step 8: Build concrete bridge abutments, retaining walls of the bridgehead, and load-
   reducing floors.
  Step 9: Install bridge girders, construct concrete bridge deck and railing barriers.

  Step 10: Build the road structure connecting to the bridge.

  Step 11: Dredge the river bed to the designed elevation, spread geotextile, and drop gabions
   to reinforce the upstream and downstream channels.
  Step 12: Build management houses and other categories in the management area, completion
   of the campus, railings, and completion of the construction.
89.   Manufacturing and installating mechanical and hydraulic equipment of the valve gates:
  Step 1: Process and manufacture valve gate at the factory.

  Step 2: Transport to the construction location and complete assembly.

  Step 3: Crane to install into positions.

  Step 4: Order, transport, and install opening and closing equipment of valve gate.

  Step 5: Test operation.


c) Dredging canal beds within the construction area

90. The sludge dredging of the canal bottom within the construction area is carried out using
a wire bucket excavator placed on a floating buoy system. Dredged sludge is transported to a
designated disposal area within the construction area.

d) Construction methods for underwater reinforced concrete piles

91. The piles under the pillars, valve beams, and sluice sides are driven underwater by a
hammer on the barge. Because it is built underwater, to ensure the exact position of the pile, a
guiding floor frame system is required. In addition, the piles of battery piers, valve beams, and
boat locks are buried below the water level so there must be guiding piles.
92.   The construction sequence is as follows:
  Gather piles at a convenient location on the construction site or put them on barges;

  Drive positioning piles and build the system of floor frame using cranes and vibrating
   hammers as designed;
  Determine the position of the pile center using a theodolite;

  Use a 25T crane on a 200T barge to hoist and install the pile in the correct position, fixing
   the pile with a system of guiding bars;
  Install the diesel hammer head to drive the pile down until the top of the pile is at a height
   of + 2.00m, then stop;
  Install guiding piles, continue driving piles down to design height;

  After completing the number of piles for 1 tracking system, proceed to rotate the tracking
   system for the next section.
93.   Driving piles on land:



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 72   Page
                                                                                                 Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
Piles of the bridge abutment and load-reducing floors at the bridgehead are driven by a system
of land-based hammers, without the need for guiding piles or guiding tracking systems:
  Gather piles at a convenient location on the construction site;

  Install system of piling rig + hammer rack

  Determine the position of the pile center using a theodolite;

  Install the pile into the hammer rack by using a 25T crane;

  Use a diesel hammer to drive the pile down to the design height

  After finishing hammering, move the hammer to the next pile position.


e) Construction method for waterproofing piles

94.   Construction steps:
  Identify the center of the piling route;

  Drive positioning piles, build the deck frame with a vibrating hammer and crane on the
   barge;
  Use a vibrating hammer to combine and drive piles to an elevation of +1.00m;

  Position the guiding bar into the guide deck;

  Use a vibrating hammer combined with a guiding bar (sound closing) to drive the pile to the
   designed height;
  After completing the number of piles for 1 track system, proceed to rotate the track system
   for the next section.
95. Construction methods: This is an important structure of construction to ensure stable
seepage at the bottom, so the construction of waterproofing piles must ensure continuity. The
piles must be hooked together and aligned, and the height of the top of the pile must not be
different. The deviation is too large (error about ±5cm). Driving of the waterproofing pile is
done on the water surface, so it is necessary to use cranes and hammers to drive piles placed on
a floating system, using a system of guiding floor including locating piles with a segment
distance of 4m/pile, and a clamping system of shaped steel to control horizontal and vertical
displacement. The pile is lowered using the vibration method. The piles are constructed below
the water surface so they must have clamps to connect the piles. In addition, due to the short
length of the piles, for the piles to be threaded together, it is necessary to construct in ladder
order, of which the first pile must be a 12m long pole protruding above the water each time
rotating the deck frame. To ensure the level of the row of piles is flat, the first pile after lowering
to the designed height must be hung to the floor system by cables to avoid falling when lowering
the next pile. The following pile bars are supported on the previous pile bar by a locating gauge
structure welded to the top of each pile bar.

f) Construction methods of coffer dams

96. The pillars, valve beams, and sluice bodies are constructed within a piling frame of sheet
steel right at the canal bed, including the following steps:
  Drive positioning piles, build floor system, and construct piles of sheet steel;




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                     73   Page
                                                                                                     Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
  Drive larsen piling frame by a vibrating hammer system on the barge;

  Install temporary anti-slip system;

  Excavation of soil within the enclosure to the designed elevation;

  Clean the foundation’s pit, pour concrete for the sluice’s foundation;

  Proceed to complete the lowering of the supporting floors;

  Check the safety of the piling frame;

  Pound pile’s heads, clear the site, construct structures;

  Dismantle the piling frame and move to the next segment.


g) Concrete and reinforced concrete

97.   Bottom plate, pillar; Valve towers, abutment structure, load reduction floors:
  Bottom plate steel, battery pier, and valve towers are processed in the material yard at the
   construction site and then transported and installed at the construction site.
  Concrete for battery piers, valve beams, and valve towers is produced at the construction site
   by mixing plants or using fresh concrete purchased from factories in the area.

h) Girders

  Bridge girders are constructed at factories and transported to the construction site by
   waterway;
  Use a crane to install the beam into position on the pier;

  Install steel reinforcement and formwork for bridge decks and bridge railings.


i) Construction of sluice sides and upstream and downstream walls

  Drive prestressed reinforced concrete piles by a vibratory hammer combined with vertical
   hydraulic scouring on a barge. Use the guiding system to position the pile line.
  Install steel reinforcement, and beam formwork to brace the top of the embankment, and
   carry the sluice manually.
  Construction of prefabricated paving slabs, installed on the floor of sluice sides.

  Finish the side surface according to the design.


j) Measures of spreading geotextile fabric under gabions

98. Geotextiles used for the project are mainly lined under gabions to reinforce the upstream
and downstream of the sluice. Construction is carried out entirely underwater on floating
devices with the following technical measures:
  Check the positioning buoy system before construction;

  The fabric is wound into a roll with a core tube inserted into the roller and wheel. After
   loading the fabric from the tube, the entire width of the fabric is braced to the river bottom
   thanks to the rotating tail shaft attached to the frame pressing down on the edge of the fabric.



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                  74   Page
                                                                                                  Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
  The fabric spreading starts from the edge of one side (adjacent to the position of the roof
   beam) to the other side. The edges of the fabric are held by sharp iron pins hammered into
   the river bottom. Steel staples are placed along the edge of the fabric, stapled every 30 cm.
   The diver checks the edge of the fabric and staples the horizontal edge of the fabric to the
   end of the construction length.
  After completing a horizontal lane, move the equipment upstream so that one fabric edge
   overlaps the other one by 1 meter and proceed with the work according to the steps above
   until the entire canal bank is covered.
  The diver inspects after the fabric covering is completed.

Notes:
  Carefully check the flatness of the canal bottom as per the designed drawing before starting
   to spread the filter fabric.
  The filter fabric is spread when the flow velocity is V < 2m/s.


k) Construction measures of gabions to reinforce the channel

  Position vertical construction lanes using a positioning buoy system on the river.

  Extend the cable to keep the winch moving the equipment along the construction lane.

  Start rolling the carpet. After setting the first stone carpet at the base of the revetment in the
   correct position, start moving equipment 5m into the shore before starting to drop the next
   gabions until the designed elevation is reached.
  After releasing the first vertical carpet, move the equipment upstream and begin releasing
   the carpet for the next embankment roof in the correct order above.
  Reposition the buoy system to continue construction.

  Always check the carpet position, and make sure the carpets are always next to one another
   with no gaps between the baskets.
  After the construction of each vertical lane, an inspection by a diver must be conducted. At
   locations of the carpet delivery, if not guaranteed to be adjacent, 20x30 loose stones must be
   dropped to fill the gap and divers must tie the carpet covers before proceeding with the next
   construction.

1.3.3.3. Construction methods for the road

  Positioning construction routes;

  Digging organic soil and dig road molds;

  Building sand embankment for the roadbed in each layer, combined with reinforced
   foundation at design locations;
  Building road surface structure in layers;

  Installing traffic signal structures;

  Completing and handing over        the construction items.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                   75   Page
                                                                                                   Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
1.3.3.4. Construction methods for the sluice gate upgradation

  Complete of the sluice gates at the factory;

  Use a crane assembly to lift the gates onto a barge;

  Transport the gates to the construction sites;

  Use the crane assembly on the barge to lift the sluice gates onto two support beams at the
   top of the pin structure at the gate slot;
  Temporarily secure the sluice gate firmly on top of the structure;

  Install the sliding guide rails on the gate tower;

  Install the opening and closing equipment on the gate tower;

  Inspect, remove temporary parts, and operate the hydraulic system to lift the gate to the open
   position;
  Remove the horizontal support beams from the top of the structure;

  Operate the cylinders to lower the sluice gates into the closed position;

  Monitor and inspect the gate's water tightness and the operation of the opening and closing
   equipment.

1.3.3.5. Construction methods of medium and low voltage lines

a) Construction of poles

a.1). Earthwork
99.   Construction standards:
  Construction and acceptance of foundation work: TCVN 9361-2012;

  Earthwork - Construction and acceptance regulations TCVN 4447-2012 and other related
   standards and regulations.
100. Construction steps:
  Once the foundation has been leveled by the design, the contractor checks the central line
   before excavating the foundation pit. The opening of the slope is suitable for the geography
   and depth of each foundation. Both mechanical and manual repair techniques are used for
   foundation excavation. In case of chance finds the contractor shall stop excavating
   immediately and inform the construction supervision consultant (CSC).
  Sheet pilling to protect foundation walls.

  Steel sheet pilling.

  Sheet drawing: When the foundation is finished, the piles are removed. A presser is also
   used for removing piles, although the removing order is reversed compared to the pressing
   procedure. It should be noted that when pulling out piles, holes in the earth will be made. To
   correct this, the contractor will re-insert the piles with sand and water, allowing the sand to
   readily fill the holes and return the construction ground to the project owner.
a.2). Construction of reinforced concrete foundation slab and footing
  Concrete lining for the foundation pit.


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                76   Page
                                                                                                Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
  Placing reinforced steel and joining foundation formwork.

  Concreting the pit (Figure 17).




Figure 17: Layout of a power pole
a.3). Construction of reinforced concrete foundation stump
  Preparing formwork and reinforced concrete

  Concreting the stump

a.4). Installation of anchor bolts
  Anchor   bolts are installed at the same stage as formwork installation and stump
   reinforcement alignment.
  Gavari is used to fix anchor bolts.

  Place each anchor bolt in the hole and use the Electronic Control Unit to temporarily screw
   it into Gavari to prepare for position and elevation alignment.
  Mark the center line of the stump on the Gavari to install the anchor bolts.

  Use an electronic total station and leveling machine to align anchor bolts.

  After the alignment is complete, a welding machine is used to secure the anchor bolts to the
   stump, and tape or sleeves are used to keep the bolt heads from sticking to the concrete while
   concreting the stump.
  After the concrete has hardened, Gavari is removed.

a.5). Installation of earthing system
  Determine the length and direction of earthing.

  Dig earth ditches

  Line ground wires

  Do acceptance and move to the next steps.

  Backfill earthing ditches

  Measure the grounding resistance.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               77   Page
                                                                                               Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
a.6). Backfilling
101. Manual backfilling is performed, with an additional 20 cm layer of soil being backfilled
after each 20cm of compaction, in accordance with the design drawing. The backfilling soil is
meticulously compacted using a compactor to achieve the design density.

b) Installation of poles

102. Installation of electric poles will be conducted by experienced contractors from Vietnam
Electricity contractors with the following activities:
b.1). Installation of base plates
103. Erect climbing pillars on the surface of the foundation pit according to the diagram and
proceed to erect the base section. The climbing poles are built on the foundation level using
self-made forks and winch systems, then holding the poles with ropes at the top of the poles,
pits, and brake winches. Anti-sink, anti-slip, and fixed base with wood and cable. Install the
base plate onto the anchor bolts after leveling. Do not tighten the nuts too tight, leave them a
little loose. Next, install the 4 main bars of the base section and tighten the anchor bolts to the
base plate, install the main tie bars to create a solid frame, then install the small tie bars, and
then install the web tie bars (anti-torsion, positioning); tighten the connecting bolts.
b.2). Installation of the middle part of the poles
  Proceed sequentially as with the bottom part.

  Move the pole.

  Complete the middle part.

b.3). Installation of the top of poles
104. After completing the middle section and temporarily tightening the bolts, the contractor
proceeds to erect the top section.
b.4). Completing the installation of poles
  Tighten all the bolts of the column bars by arranging 4 workers to work simultaneously on
   opposite sides at the same height to tighten the bolts and nuts from top to bottom with a
   typical wrench or force wrench (At least one force wrench is turned around to make sure
   that the nuts on all four sides of the column are tight).
  Make the bolts below 10 meters high and the base plate's anchor bolts immobile (Figure 18).




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 78   Page
                                                                                                 Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT




Figure 18: Installation of electric poles

c) Methods of pulling and tensioning electric wires

105. The construction process for laying cables involves manual methods combined with
machinery and follows the following sequence (Figure 19):
  Prepare and arrange construction tools and machines according to the approved plan.

  Hang pulleys as scaffolding to support wires over current roads and power lines.

  Temporarily increase anchors and tie anchors at both ends.

  Lay primer cable.

  Winches for collecting bait cables and pulling conductors.

  Press the left-hanging anchor.

  Press wire connections using a 100-ton hydraulic press.

  Tension the rope to achieve the designed deflection.

  Press the right suspension lock.

  Lower the rope into the support lock at the positions of the support columns.

  Construct the crossbeam and complete the finishing touches.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               79   Page
                                                                                               Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT




Figure 19: Pulling and tensioning electric wires

1.3.3.6. Solar power supply solutions

106. Solar energy is proposed as the power source for lighting along the Binh Giang 1 canal,
traffic and management bridges, embankments, and management houses, which has a minimum
capacity of 100W and is mostly used at night, as below:
  Solar lamps with a minimum power of 100W are used to light up the Binh Giang 1 road and
   the traffic/work bridges over the sluices. The minimum lamp parameters are as follows:
     o   LED chip: high power SMD 3030;
     o   Solar panels: 6V/22W poly;
     o   Storage battery: 18,000mAh;
     o   Lighting time: 10 to15 hours/day;
  A solar panel system with a battery storage system is used to light up the management
   houses. The technical specifications of the solar panel system are as follows:
     o   Solar battery capacity: ≥ 3kW;
     o   Grid-tied inverter with storage system (or grid tie with limiter with battery storage):
         5kW;
     o   Solar power storage system (Lithium battery or telecom battery): maximum storage of
         50kWh.
107. Benefits of this power source include using renewable energy, which is abundant in the
sun, and helping to protect the environment by not producing carbon emissions like thermal,
gas, or liquid electrolysis, which are common methods for producing electricity.


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                80   Page
                                                                                                Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

1.3.6.     Demand for Raw Materials, Fuels and Disposal Sites

1.3.6.1. Construction materials and supply sources

110. According to the project’s FS report, the demand for raw materials required for the project
construction is depicted in Table 17.
111. Sources: All material sources have been granted with the exploitation and business license
issued by the relevant authorities:
  Filling soil: As mentioned in Table 17, the filling activities of the project will require
                             3
   approximately 158,146.7 m of soil. This is a small amount, therefore, excavated soil will
   be reused for all project items to mitigate impacts caused by its disposal. However, it is
   necessary to evaluate the quality of excavated soil before use.
  Sand, and stone: purchased at licensed shops in An Giang province, etc.

  Steel, cement: steel will be purchased in An Giang, Can Tho, and Ho Chi Minh City while
   cement will be purchased in An Giang.
  Melaleuca piles: purchased at licensed shops in An Giang

112. An Giang PPMU/Construction Supervision Consultant (CSC) will be responsible for
verifying the quality, capacity, and legal status of the suppliers, including the environment and
social requirements (official certificates for EIA, labor contracts), before approving them as the
primary suppliers for the project, following the bidding process. Each awarded contractor will
select their own suppliers for construction materials. All materials are tested for quality in
accordance with current regulations in place. If the materials don't match the technical

standards, the contractor has to find other reliable suppliers. Materials and technical supply

113. The project will not require the establishment of any new quarry, as it will not involve
the large-scale extraction of sand, stone, and soil materials. This means that none of the quarries
are an exclusive source for the project.
114. Transportation methods: Given that the project is situated in an area with a good
waterway, construction materials, fuel, tools, and machinery will be transported by waterway
via the Rach Gia-Ha Tien (RG-HT) canal, the Rach Soi-Long Xuyen canal, flood drainage
canals, etc.
115. The project will use 200-ton barges to transport materials for construction. The estimated
construction period is 6-24 months, with the barges operating 26 days each month. Therefore,
the total number of barge trips to transport materials for the construction activities will be
calculated as 2.36 trips per day.
116. These materials, including cement, stone, sand, and steel, will primarily be purchased
from Hon Dat town and Kien Luong (former), with an average distance to the construction site
of 3-30 km per trip (Figure 20).

1.3.6.2. Fuel and power supply

117. Electricity: Electricity for the project construction will come from the national grid
through the installation of power lines and 150 kVA transformer stations in the area where the
sluice gates are planned to be built. These take power from the 16 kV medium voltage grid to
lower the voltage of 0.4 kV for project construction and operation. Furthermore, to ensure the
consistent progress and functionality of the project in the event of power disruptions, 200-
kilowatt-hour automatic generators will be used.



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                         83    Page
                                                                                                           Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
118. Fuel: The fuel need for operating construction machineries, equipment and trucks will be
provided by the local gas stations located within a 1km radius of the construction site (Figure
20).




Figure 20: Potential construction material transportation routes

1.3.6.3. Water supply

119. The contractors will sign contracts to purchase water from local houses as agreed upon
for use in construction, since the locations are not too far from residential areas. Prior to
commencing construction, the contractors will perform surveys of the freshwater supply sites
in the vicinity, collect water samples, and send them for analysis to guarantee the water quality.
Supply contracts will only be signed if the water meets the quality criteria for domestic use
(QCVN 01-1:2018/BYT) and mixing concrete use (TCVN 4506:2012), in accordance with the
construction plan.

1.3.6.4. Solid wastes and disposal sites

120. Workers’s domestic waste: This garbage would be generated by construction workers on-
site. During the construction phase, it is projected that approximately 80kg will be produced
every day. Hon Dat District URENCO will collect and transfer the waste to the authorized
landfill every three days.
121. Construction waste:
  Excavated soil:

      From the construction of 23 sluices: The soil excavated from the foundation pits, roughly
       11,679 m³ per sluice, will be stored within the construction area. After the material has
       been dried, it will be utilized for leveling the site and filling in the foundations, banks,
       and canal banks both upstream and downstream of the sluice.
      For road construction: all excavated roadbed soil will be directly used to construct the
       road slopes, ensuring no waste is generated in the environment.For powerline: all
       excavated soil will be directly used to backfilling the surface.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 84   Page
                                                                                              Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
  Other construction waste: such as formwork, cement bags, iron, etc. will be collected and
     sold to informal sectors. The remaining will be collected and transported by the legal entity
     of Binh Giang commune landfill and Binh Son commune landfill every 3 days
122. Workers’s domestic waste: This garbage would be generated by construction workers on-
site. During the construction phase, it is projected that approximately 80kg will be generated
every day. The remaining will be collected and transported by the legal entity of Binh Giang
commune landfill and Binh Son commune landfill every 3 days.
123. Hazardous waste: This waste generated during construction will be collected and
temporarily stored in the storage area located in the construction area before prior to being
transported to an appropriate waste treatment facility in accordance with the agreement between
the contractor and the waste treatment facility.

1.3.6.5. Demand on workers

124. The implementation of the project is expected to mobilize 210 people, including 200
construction workers and 10 professional consultants. The work items are dispersed on a large
scale and the number of construction workers at each site is 5-10 people and 16 workers’ camps
during the project construction. See details in Table 18.

1.4. ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL ASSESSMENT METHODS

1.4.1.   Rapid assessment method

129.The method was issued by the World Health Organization (WHO) in 1993. The basis of
this method is the nature of materials, technologies, and rules of natural processes as well as
experiences in rating pollution load.
130.In Vietnam, this method is introduced and applied in many ESIA reports, performing the
relatively accurate calculation of the pollution load in the context of limited measurement and
analysis instruments. In this report, the pollution load coefficients are taken under the EIA
guidelines of the World Bank (Environmental Assessment Sourcebook, Volume II, Sectoral
Guidelines, Environment, World Bank, Washington D.C 8/1991) and Handbook of Emission,
Non-Industrial and Industrial source, Netherlands). This method was used in Chapter 3.

1.4.2.   Impact identification method

131.This method is applied through the following specific steps: describe the environment
system; identify the project components that affect the environment; and identify the full range
of related waste streams, and environmental issues to serve the detailed evaluation. The method
was applied to Chapter 1 and Chapter 3.

1.4.3.   Mapping method

132.This method is applied to assess the spatial interventions of the project. It can also be used
to evaluate the scope of influence of the project. This method was used to show the project
locations and sampling locations on the administrative map and was applied throughout the
entire report.

1.4.4.   Impact matrix method

133.This method is to simultaneously list the activities of the project with a list of
environmental factors that may be affected. Combining these lists in the form of rows and
columns, we obtain an environmental matrix. It then shows more clearly the causal relationship
between the activities of the project and the environmental factors impacted simultaneously in
the matrix plots. Depending on usage, the environment matrix can be divided into the following
categories: simple matrix, complex matrix, and complex quantitative matrix.
134.In this report, the quantitative matrix method is used, in which the inventory is listed for
the activities of the project and the column is the list of environmental factors affected. Each
matrix of matrix evaluates the possible impact of project activity on an environmental factor.
This method was applied to Chapter 3.

1.4.5.   Household survey method

135.A household (HH) survey is conducted after the completion of the review of secondary
data and field observations. Before fieldwork, a household questionnaire was developed to
guide the collection of additional data. Questionnaires developed include (i) guide questions
(for focus group discussion and community consultation); and (ii) household survey (for an

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                90   Page
                                                                                                Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
interview with selected households). This method was used to assess the current socio-
economic situation of households affected by the project implementation as a basis for
proposing mitigation measures. It was utilized in Chapter 2 of the report.

1.4.6.   Focus group discussion and public consultation

136.Community consultations are conducted in the form of focus group discussions and
community meetings, or posted on relevant agency websites to validate findings from fieldwork
as well as household interviews. Additionally, the community consultation method is used to
gather community opinions on environmental and social impacts, as well as management and
mitigation measures mentioned in the report. This method is utilized in Chapters 2 and 6 of
the report.

1.4.7.   The method of inheritance, analysis, and synthesis of information and data

137. This method was used to identify and evaluate the natural and socio-economic conditions
of the project area through data and information collected from various sources such as
statistical yearbooks, socio-economic reports, the current environmental status, and relevant
studies. Additionally, inheriting existing studies and reports to utilize all available findings and
identify additional limitations is crucial. During the preparation of the report, data and
information about the project information and socio-economic conditions in the project area
were collected from relevant and reliable sources. This method was used in Chapters 1 and 2.

1.4.8.   Secondary data assessment method

138. This method involves evaluating existing documents related to the project area and
reviewing available information from project documents. Particularly important is reviewing
data and information available from the project's feasibility study and other relevant
information, as well as statistical data.

1.4.9.   Site survey method

139. Site surveys are mandatory for ESIA/EIA to determine the current state of the project
area, and relevant surrounding objects for selecting sampling locations, assessing the current
state of the environment, hydrology, climate conditions, land use, vegetation cover, and
biodiversity in the project area. In addition, site survey are a valuable source of information to
verify the results of secondary data review for social assessment. It aimed to collect and
supplement existing information to contribute to household surveys and group discussion
questions. This method was used in Chapters 1, 2, and 3 of the report.

1.4.10. Comparison method

140. This method involves assessing environmental quality, water quality, pollutant loads, etc.,
based on comparison with relevant environmental standards and regulations from the Ministry
of Health and the MONRE (now Ministry of Agriculture and Environment), as well as related
studies and experiments. This method was used in Chapters 2 and 3 of the report.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                  91   Page
                                                                                                  Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
1.4.11. Expert adjustment method

141. Based on their knowledge and experience with environmental and social impact
assessment (ESIA), the ESIA specialists and other experts from universities and institutions
discussed and agreed on the ESIA findings. This method was applied throughout the report.

1.4.12. Monitoring and analysis methods

142. To assess the current environmental status of the project area, the samples of air, soil,
sediment, surface and aquatic water, wastewater, and groundwater, etc. were taken and
analyzed in the project area. Sample analysis results were used as background data to evaluate
the impact of project implementation on the environment and issues that the project owner need
to pay attention to during project implementation. This method was used in Chapter 2.

1.4.13. Mathematical model method

143. Various models are presently available on a global and national scale for the investigation
of flow hydrodynamics-related issues. One dimension (1D), two dimensions (2D), or three
dimensions (3D) may comprise these models. The 1D model MIKE 11 + NAM has been
selected for the project area based on the available data. In Chapter 4 of the report, the model
is used to evaluate how the project will affect the local water resources.
144. The model has included the entire river and canal network of the Long Xuyen Quadrangle
(LXQ) and Ca Mau Peninsula (CMP), with special attention to the detailed canals within the
region. The canal and works system is developed in this model using conventional simulations,
whereas cells in flooded areas are simulated as multiple-gated spill cells (appropriate for actual
flood circumstances). Flowing through surface spill gates or sluices simulates the flow process
from canals/rivers to cells and vice versa; sluices within cells are replicated as time-operated
regulating sluices. This model inherits findings from the SIWRR’s “Cai Lon - Cai Be Irrigation
System Phase 2” project, which was completed in 2020. However, topography data and
hydrological measurement documents from projects done in the area have been collected to
update the detailed canal system information for the project.
145. The detailed model for the CMP and LXQ regions includes 3,824 river and canal sections,
and 1,892 works (all types, including hypothetical works for overflowing the banks of field
plots).
146. Boundary conditions of the CMP region model: the upstream boundary is the actual flow
measured at Chau Doc and Vam Nao stations. The downstream boundary of the model is the
water level at the East and West coast outlets. The internal boundary within the model is the
flow due to rain calculated from the MIKE Nam model, evaporation and water intake for
production are taken according to the results of the water demand calculation (Figure 22).




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                92   Page
                                                                                                Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

                                                          Digitalized points in rivers/canals

CHAPTER 2. NATURAL, SOCIO-ECONOMIC CONDITIONS AND
           CURRENT ENVIRONMENTAL STATUS OF THE
           PROJECT AREA
2.1. NATURAL CONDITIONS

2.1.1.       Geographical Location

147. Before the merger, Hon Dat district is located on NH80, connecting Rach Gia city with
Ha Tien town. It is the district with the largest natural area of Kien Giang (now An Giang)
province with the district capital being Hon Dat town. Location of the district: the southwest
borders the Gulf of Thailand, the northwest borders Kien Luong district, the southeast borders
Rach Gia city, the east borders Tan Hiep district, and the northeast borders Thoai Son district
(An Giang province).
148. Former Hon Dat district has 14 commune-level administrative units, including 2 towns:
Hon Dat (district capital) and Soc Son, and 12 communes: Binh Giang, Binh Son, Linh Huynh,
My Hiep Son, My Lam, My Phuoc, My Thai, My Thuan, Nam Thai Son, Son Binh, Son Kien,
Tho Son with the largest rice area in the province of more than 80,000 hectares. In addition,
this is also an area with development potential for aquaculture (especially the southern region
of Highway 80), stone mining, and tourism,... Former Hon Dat district's population in 2022 is
158,883 people, mainly Kinh, Khmer, and Chinese.
149. The project area is located in the western coastal area of Binh Giang and Binh Son
communes, with a total area of about 5,500 hectares (accounting for 16% of the area of the two
communes), see Figure 7.

2.1.2.       Climatic Conditions

150. The project area is situated in the LXQ’s general climate zone, which is characterized by
the tropical monsoon climate:
  Rain: During the period 2011 to 2023, the average annual rainfall in the project area reaches
   2,331mm, the highest rainfall is 2,936mm (in 2023), and the lowest is 1,593mm (in 2015).
   The period from May to November has a high concentration of rainfall, contributing to
   around 90 to 92% of the overall annual precipitation (Table 20). This is one of the MD's
   areas with reasonably high rainfall.
                                                                                      o
  Temperature: This is an area of high temperature, with an average temperature of 28 C. The
                                                            o
   month with the greatest average temperature is May (29.6 C), and the month with the lowest
   average temperature is January (26.1oC). The temperature difference between months is
   fairly large, with 5.3oC in 2013 and 4.2oC in 2021. The change in temperature between
   months is no more than 3oC.

   evaporation, and since evaporation exceeds precipitation, drought is likely. October has the
   least amount of evaporation (Table 22), and because evaporation is significantly lower than
   precipitation, flooding is possible.

  Wind: The predominant wind direction is North, followed by Northeast or Northeast. North
   and northeast winds prevail with a frequency of more than 30% at the start of the dry season
   (November and December). Westerly and southwesterly winds prevail throughout the wet
   season. The wind blows westward during the transitional month of October. The average
   yearly wind speed is 3.4m/s, and the frequency of calm wind can reach 40% in November
   and December (Table 24).

151. Comments: The project area is located in a zone that experiences moderate rainfall and
it entails excavation activities. It is recommended that the contractor prioritizes carrying out
the majority of the construction work during the dry season and minimizes work during the
rainy season in order to mitigate the impact of rainwater runoff. Further consideration should
be given to the directions of the prevailing winds. Contractors should position the concrete
mixing stations and material storage areas at the downwind side to minimize the impact on
adjacent people.

2.1.3.    Topographic and geomorphological characteristics

2.1.3.1. Topographical characteristics

152. The project area is a coastal plain. Its terrain is highly varied, with hills, mountains, seas,
and plains ranging in elevation from 0.25 to 0.75m on average (Figure 23) [FS report of the
project, 2023]. Strips of high terrain can be seen around NH 80 and primary canals. Low-lying
terrain is located primarily on the seaward side, with sub-areas reaching an elevation of -0.2m
above sea level. This region is ideal for the growth of saltwater aquaculture, particularly
saltwater and brackish water tiger shrimp production. Furthermore, the land slopes from
northeast to southwest, allowing for natural drainage toward the West Sea.




      Provincial administrative boundaries
      Districial administrative boundaries
      Roads

                                                              Source: FS report of the project, 2023
Figure 23: Topographic map of the project area

2.1.3.2. Geological characteristics

153. Geological survey results conducted by the Southern Institute of Water Resources
Research (SIWRR) at the construction sites of the project items showed that:

a) In the construction area of the sluices

154. The project area is situated in the West Coast's typical unstable soil. In addition to
significant compaction and subsidence, the soil possesses a relatively low bearing capacity.
Current geological documents from other construction projects in the project area indicate that
the project area's geology comprises the following layers:
  Leveling soil layer: This layer is characterized by less plastic clay, with a yellow-brown to
   reddish-brown color. It is in a semi-soft to semi-hard state, with a thickness ranging from
   1.4m to 2.2m


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                  97   Page
                                                                                                  Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
  Layer 1a: This layer consists of less plastic clay mixed with sand, displaying a gray-brown
   or yellow-brown color. It is in a soft plastic state and is present only in borehole K35-3 with
   a thickness of 2.6m
  Layer 1: This layer is highly plastic, containing both clay and organic matter. It appears in
   black, brown-gray, and green-gray colors, exhibiting a flowing-to-plastic consistency. The
   thickness of this layer ranges from 2.2m to 6.9m
  Layer 2c: This layer is characterized by less plastic clay with colors such as brown-red,
   yellow-brown, and white-gray. It is in a semi-hard to hard state and has a thickness ranging
   from 8.8m to 17.9m
  Layer 3: This layer consists of less plastic clay mixed with sand, occasionally forming
   aggregates. It appears in colors like brown-red, yellow-gray, and green-gray, exhibiting a
   soft plastic to semi-hard consistency. The thickness varies from 3.5m to 8.0m
  Layer 4: This layer is characterized by less plastic clay with colors such as brown-red,
   yellow-brown, and white-gray. It is in a semi-hard state and has a thickness of 5.1m, only
   appearing in borehole K37-2
  Layer 5: This layer consists of fine sand particles mixed with dust, displaying colors like
   yellow-brown, yellow-gray, and white-gray. It is in a firm to compact state, with a thickness
   ranging from 2.5m to 11.3m

b) In the construction area of the connecting road

155. Soil in the construction area of the connecting road is classified into the following layers:
  Leveling soil layer: Less plastic silt, yellow-brown or reddish-brown, in a soft to stiff plastic
   state. This layer appears from the top elevation of the layer (+1.57 to +2.85)m to the bottom
   elevation of the layer (-0.26 to +1.33)m, with a thickness varying from 1.0 to 2.7m.


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                    99    Page
                                                                                                       Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
  Layer 1: Very plastic silt, mixed with organic matter, black, brown, or green-gray, in a flow-
   plastic flow state. This layer appears from the top elevation of the layer ((-0.26 to +1.33)m
   to the bottom elevation of the layer (-7.56 to -0.76)m, with a thickness varying from (1.7 to
   7.5)m.
  Layer 2a: Few plastic clay, brown-red, gray-white, gray-black, soft plastic state. This layer
   appears from the top elevation of the layer (-2.91 to +1.33) m to the bottom elevation of the
   layer (-4.21 to -1.17) m, and the thickness of the layer varies from 1.1 to 2.5 m.
  Layer 2b: Few plastic clay, brown-red, yellow-gray, semi-hard plastic state. This layer
   appears from the top elevation of the layer (-3.88 to -1.17) m to the bottom elevation of the
   layer (-7.18 to -3.77) m, and the thickness of the layer varies from 2.6 to 4.0 m.
  Layer 2c: Few plastic clays, brown-red, yellow-brown, gray-white, semi-hard to a hard state.
   This layer appears from the top elevation of the layer (-7.56 to -2.06) m to the bottom
   elevation of the layer (-10.41 to -9.15) m, and the thickness of the layer varies from 2.8 to
   7.2m.
  Layer 3: Sandy clay with little plasticity, brown-red, gray-white, semi-hard state. This layer
   appears from the top elevation of the layer (-6.73) m to the bottom elevation of the layer (-
   8.73) m, and the thickness of the layer is (2.0) m. This layer only appears in the borehole
   GT-15.
  Layer 4: Few plastic clay, yellow-brown, gray-white, semi-hard state. This layer appears
   from the top elevation of the layer (-8.73) m to the bottom elevation of the layer (-10.43) m,
   and the thickness of the layer is 1.7 m. This layer only appears in the borehole GT-15.

156. Comments: The ground in the project’s construction areas belongs to Quaternary
sediments, with a mixed origin from both the river and sea. Specifically, soil layers 1a and 1
consist of Holocene sediment belonging to the upper subsystem (ambQ23), which has very poor
load-bearing capacity, high compression settlement, and is susceptible to structural damage
when subjected to the effects of flowing water. This makes it unsuitable for foundation
construction. Layers 2a, 2b, 2c, 3, and 4 are sediment layers from the Pleistocene upper to
Holocene lower-middle (amQ13 - amQ21-2), exhibiting a state ranging from soft to stiff, with
reasonably good load-bearing capacity, making them more suitable for foundation construction.
157. Due to the construction of the sluices and road on the weak ground (layers 1, 1a), which
is characterized by high compression settlement and poor load-bearing capacity, it is essential
to devise a reasonable ground treatment plan for the project. During construction, monitoring
settlement is crucial, and the construction should be phased appropriately to avoid detrimental
effects on the ground. Additionally, attention should be given to the consolidation settlement
process of the ground for a suitable treatment plan. For a treatment plan involving pile
foundations, it is recommended to use piles to transfer the load of the structure to areas with
good load-bearing capacity. When treating the ground with piles, the diameter, length, and
quantity of piles should be determined based on the geotechnical properties of the ground,
considering the structural load. During construction, compression tests on the piles should be
conducted to accurately determine the load-bearing capacity and adjust the range of ground
reinforcement accordingly.

2.1.4.   Hydrographical and Hydrogeological Conditions

2.1.4.1. Hydrographical conditions

158. The project area is located on the coast and are the tributaries that connect the Hau River
and West Sea. The tides in the area are a mix of diurnal and semi-diurnal, with a preference for
diurnal tides. Tide amplitudes range from 0.8 to 1.0m. Ebb tides have less fluctuation in water
level than high tides. Maintaining a low water level requires a greater amount of time than
maintaining a high water level. The average water level is close to the low tide water level.
159. Furthermore, surface water sources in the area are heavily reliant on West Sea tides and
the ability of the main canal system to carry water from the Hau River. The Giang Thanh River,
Ha Giang Canal, Nong Truong Canal, T3 Canal, etc. are responsible for transporting the
upstream water flow in Chau Doc (about 5,400m 3/s during the rainy season and 300m3/s during
the dry season) to the project area. All of these canals are interconnected with the RG-HT canal
and flow into the West Sea through several routes, including Dong Ho lagoon, Ta Sang canal,
Tam Ban canal, Ba Hon canal, Tuan Thong canal, Binh Giang canal, etc. The project area is
not suitable for freshwater supplies for rice growing due to the canal system's ability to transfer
fresh water from the Hau River and saltwater intrusion from the sea.
160. Salinity intrudes deep inland during the dry season due to the low water level of Hau river
branches, lack of rainfall, and lack of sluice gates to prevent salinity intrusion. It has a huge
impact on the area's production and people's daily lives. The highest Kien Giang Sea tide

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                   102   Page
                                                                                                Confidential
                    ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
measured at the old Rach Gia market is +1.00, while the lowest is -0.72. According to the
Southern Institute of Water Resources Research (2023), the hydrological characteristics at Rach
Gia station are described in Table 29 to Table 31.

2.1.4.2. Tidal regime

161. The hydrological regime of rivers and canals in the region is mainly influenced directly
by the inland rainfall regime and the West Sea tides, and indirectly by the East Sea tides and
adjacent areas.
Table 29: Water level in Rach Gia monitoring station

2.1.4.3. Hydrogeological conditions

162. The project area's groundwater is of low quality, with chlorine levels ranging from 400
to 1,000mg/L and water pH ranging from neutral to moderate base. The depth of exploitation
is 40-60 m. Its reserves are quite abundant but of uneven quality. Currently, it is depleting in
terms of aquifers and reserves, with symptoms of cascade. Many diverse groundwater layers
and aquifers have formed as a result of the geological features of the region. It can only be used
in shallow strata, and the amount of water that can be used in deeper layers is limited.
163. As of right now, certain areas lack a source of fresh water, leading to the continued
practice of dispersed underground exploitation that does not adhere to state planning and
regulations and thus has a negative impact on groundwater reserves and quality. If this condition
persists, it will impact existing aquifers. It is important to remember that this is a coastal region
and that the impact of saltwater intrusion on groundwater supplies is likely to be significant.


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                                    103       Page
                                                                                                                     Confidential
                      ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
164. The excavation operations of the project will be limited to surface layers, mostly for tasks
such as installing utility poles, which require digging to a depth of approximately 3-4 meters.
For this reason, the project will have a negligible effect on the groundwater layers in the project
area.

2.1.5.        Natural Resources

2.1.5.1. Land resource

165. The total natural land area in the project area is 34,726.82 ha, including (Table 32):
  Residential land: 324.65ha, accounting for 0.93% of the total land area of the area.

  Agricultural land: 32,107.20 ha, accounting for 92.46% of the total land area of the whole
     region, agricultural land still accounts for a high proportion of the land use structure.
  Forestry land: 5,418.67ha, accounting for 8.32% of the total land area of the area.

  Aquaculture land: 341.19 ha, accounting for 0.98% of the total land area of the area.

  Water surface area (rivers and canals): 894.44 ha, accounting for 2.58% of the total land
     area.

2.1.5.2. Land resource

166. In general, the land fund of the project area is mainly light alkaline soil, the potential
fertility is relatively high, the total organic content, nitrogen, and potassium is quite high, and
the level of organic decomposition is slow, especially the total P2O5 (phosphate) is slightly poor
and decreases with depth, P2O5 is easy to digest from poor to very poor. Therefore, in use, it is
necessary to pay attention to measures of sour brass, and alum washing and in production, it is
necessary to avoid bringing the alum layer above the surface layer. In recent years, the state has
taken many measures to wash acid sulfate, so currently the soil contaminated with alum is quite
few. The project owner and constractors should pay attention in the excavation in the acid
sulfate soil, especially when building the 23 sluice and the road.

2.1.5.3. Mineral resources

167. Although Hon Dat's mineral resources are limited and diversified, they have great
commercial value, including stone, clay, and peat. The district has 224.25 ha of peat mines
spread throughout 85B canal, Bao canal, Hon Dat forest field (Nam Thai Son commune), and
Than Bun canal (Binh Son commune). Currently, these mines are exploited by a variety of
firms, including Kien Giang Agriculture and Forestry Co., Ltd., AAA Company, etc.
Furthermore, a 99.98-ha stone quarry (granite) is located in Hon Soc mountain, Tho Son
commune. There is neither mineral sources nor construction material mines in the project’s
area. The supply of construction materials can be obtained from the surrounding neighboring
districts with the distance to the project’s sites is about 10-20 km.

2.1.5.4. Forest resources

168. Former Hon Dat district's forest area was from 16,284.03 ha in 2010 to 8,647.23 ha in
2021, a drop of 7,636.80 ha. This was since the protection forest decreased from 6,695.54 ha in
2010 to 6,226.65 ha in 2020 and the production forest experienced a decrease from 9,588.49 ha
to 2,420.58 ha between 2010 and 2020. The reason for the decline in production forest area is
the approved plan's transition from production forest to rice farming, which provides higher
economic efficiency and faster capital turnover than afforestation. Meanwhile, climate change
and marine erosion are to blame for the loss of protection forest areas. As a result, numerous
parts of the district and surrounding areas have been eroded to the foot of the protective dike.
This increases the strength of the southwest wind, affecting the district's sea dike. However,
project implementation will not acquire forest land or causing impact on forest resources.



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                   105   Page
                                                                                                Confidential
                             ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
2.1.5.5. Water resources

169. Kien Giang (now An Giang) is a province located downstream of the Hau River's
freshwater source and also upstream of the Gulf of Thailand's brackish water source. As a result,
several factors influence the hydrological regime of the province in general, and the project
communes in particular: the tides of the Gulf of Thailand (brackish water source), the
hydrological regime of the Hau River (freshwater source), the local rainfall regime (freshwater
source), and, most importantly, the operation of the LXQ irrigation system
(saltwater/freshwater regulation). These factors have different impacts during various periods
of the year.
170. Saltwater source: Due to its closeness to the West Sea, the project is strongly affected by
the West Sea tides. This is also a plus for developing a brackish/saltwater supply for aquaculture
growth in the region. The sea tide in this location is a mixed kind, with a variation range of 0.8-
1.0 meters, leaning toward diurnal tides. The low tide level is more stable than the high tide
level.
171. Freshwater source: from rainfall and Hau River, wherein:
     From rainfall: mainly available in the rainy season. In the project area, the rainy season lasts
           from May to November, while the dry season lasts from December to April of the following
           year. Total rainfall during the rainy season accounts for around 90-92% of total annual
           rainfall, while just 8-10% occurs during the dry season. There are years when there is no rain
           for four months in a row, such as in 2016. The average annual rainfall in the project area is
           2,166mm, with the best year receiving 2,925.2mm (1996) and the lowest receiving 1,307mm
           (2001).
     From Hau river branches’ water: During the flood season (June to November), the water
           level on the Hau River is high, providing an abundant freshwater source to the project area.
           However, many reservoirs have been built in the Mekong River's upstream countries, such
           as China and Laos, in recent years. It has reduced the Hau River's water supply during the
           wet season. Furthermore, during the dry season, the water level on the Hau River drops,
           resulting in a poor freshwater source for the project area, especially in the last months of the
           dry season such as March and April. This causes a shortage of freshwater for summer-winter
           rice production, especially in the southern area of NH 80 (Figure 24 to Figure 27).

172. Operation of the LXQ irrigation system: As previously stated, the brackish/freshwater
supply in the LXQ in general, and the project in particular, is largely dependent on the system's
operation mode. Previously, the system was not completed. As a result, saltwater intruded deep
into the inland, having a profound impact on local people’s production and daily life. The
coastal sluice system from Rach Gia to Ha Tien is currently closed, allowing for improved


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               107 P a g e
                                                                                             Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
control of seawater intrusion into the project area. In addition, the project area is placed distant
from the freshwater source and near the saltwater source. Thus, a reduction in the Hau river's
freshwater supply to the region and sluice gate-induced saline intrusion from the Wet Sea also
result in a shortage of freshwater for rice cultivation in the dry season. As a result, shifting from
a three-rice crop model to a shrimp-rice model is appropriate, with aquaculture taking place
during the high-salinity dry season and rice production taking place during the rainy season.
This production model is nature-based, long-lasting, and efficient. So, implementation of the
project will help to boost the transition.

2.1.5.6. Biological resources

a) Terrestrial flora and fauna

173. In Hon Dat district (former), there are no natural heritage sites. Currently, in 14
communes and towns of the district, only Tho Son commune has rare species that need to be
protected according to the Red Book of Vietnam and IUCN, including: White-cheeked gibbon
(Nomascus leucogenys - Vượn đen má hung), Asiatic black bear (Ursus thibetanus - Gấu ngựa),
Sun bear (Ursus malayanu - Gấu chó), Bengal slow loris (Nycticebus bengalensis -cu li lớn),
Pygmy slow loris (Nycticebus pygmaeus- Cu li nhỏ), Hawksbill sea turtle (Eretmochelys
imbricata - Đồi mồi), Kemp's ridley sea turtle (Lepidochelys kempii-vích), Asian small-clawed
otter (Aonyx cinereus- Rái cá vuốt bé), Malayan Pangolin (Manis javanica- Tê tê java), Lesser
adjutant (Leptoptilos javanicus - Già đẩy nhỏ), Sarus Crane (Grus antigone sharpii - Sếu đầu
đỏ), Long tailed macaque (Macaca fascicularis - Khỉ đuôi dài), Pig tailed Macaqu (Macaca
leonina - Khỉ đuôi lợn), Malayan snail-eating turtle (Malayemys subtrijuga - Rùa ba gờ), Giant
Asian pond turtle (Heosemys grandis - Rùa đất lớn), Yellow-headed temple turtle (Hieremys
annandalei-Rùa răng), Clouded monitor lizard (Varanus bengalensis - Kỳ đà vân) [Former Hon
Dat District People's Committee, 2022]. These species, on the other hand, don't live in the
project area. Instead, they live about 25 to 30 km away.
174. The project area does not serve as a significant habitat or breeding ground for rare/
endangered/protected animals.

b) Aquatic life

175. There are numerous water bodies in the project area, including canals and rivers. The
project includes the construction and renovation of sluices on the Don Dong canal, K3-Tuan
Thong canal, canal 200, and the canal along the National defense dike. No significant levels of
aquatic biodiversity are known to exist in any of these bodies of water.
176. The analysis results of 20 aquatic life samples in the project area showed that no rare
endangered or valuable aquatic species in the canals. The analysis results are shown in Appendix
3. Following is the summary of the aquatic life:
  Phytoplankton: There area 75 species of phytoplankton belonging to 6 phyla of algae.
   Among these, the Chlorophyta phylum predominated with 60 species (61.3%), followed by
   the Bacillariophyta with 14 species (18.7%), the Cyanophyta with 7 species (9.3%), the
   Euglenophyta with 4 species (5.3%), and both the Chrysophyta and Rhodophyta with 2
   species each (2.7%). All species identified are common in aquatic environments, with none
   being endemic or requiring conservation efforts. The presence of 75 species across the 20
   sampling sites indicates a high level of diversity in the phytoplankton in the project area.


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                   108   Page
                                                                                                Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
   The abundance of species from the Chlorophyta phylum suggests that the environment in
   the area is largely influenced by freshwater sources.
  Zooplankton: There are 32 species belonging to 3 subclasses have been identified in the
   project area, with the majority concentrated in 2 subclasses: Cladocera, with 16 species, and
   Copepoda, with 15 species, while Ostracoda has only 1 species. However, all identified
   species belong to widely distributed groups and are not considered endemic or in need of
   protection.
  Zoobenthos: in the project area, there are 29 species belonging to 5 classes, with the class of
   insects comprising 8 species (37.9%), followed by the class of bivalves with 8 species
   (27.6%), the class of Gastropoda with 4 species (13.8%), and both the class of oligochaetes
   and Crustacea having 3 species each (10.3%).
177. Construction of the 23 sluice on the canals will have impacts on aquatic life. However,
most of in-water construction activities will occur within coffer dams, so the impacts will be
limited.

2.1.6.   Climatic Changes in Kien Giang (now An Giang) Province

2.1.6.1. Salinity intrusion and drought

178. One of the most obvious effects of climate change is the unusual changes of local weather.
The increase in number and prolongation in time duration of high temperature weather waves
and drought have caused significantly impacts on people's health and other agricultural
productions, increasing forest fires.
179. Salinity intrusion: Damage from salinity intrusion has been mitigated mainly by the
construction of sluices on the sea dike and temporary dams.

2.1.6.2. Increasing severe hot weather events

180. Based on the CC and SLR Scenarios for Vietnam issued by the MONRE in 2020, and
considering climate parameters and anticipated socioeconomic development in the region, CC
and SLR scenarios for the project area have been determined using the low average greenhouse
gas concentration scenario (RCP4.5) and the high greenhouse gas concentration scenario
(RCP8.5) as outlined in Table 33 to Table 35. The results show that the maximum temperature
is predicted to increase by less than the minimum temperature. By 2100, the maximum
temperature is projected to be higher than the current record by about 2°C–2.5°C compared
with an increase of 3.5°C–4.0°C for the minimum temperature in Kien Giang (now An Giang)
province. in addition, by 2100, rainfall is expected to increase by about 3%–4% Kien Giang
(now An Giang) compared to the baseline. Rainfall tends to increase in the rainy months (by
up to 25% by 2100) and decrease in the dry months (from 30%–35%). By 2100, rainfall is
projected to increase in Kien Giang (now An Giang) with an increase of 5%–10% compared
with the baseline period. By 2100, the heaviest daily rainfall decreases in Kien Giang (now An
Giang) at a rate of about 20%–30%. However, rainy days with rainfall anomalies of half or
twice the current record will continue to exist.
