   2.1.6.3. Storms and floods

   181. The current condition of storms and floods in the project area are summarised as follows:
      Storms: In recent years, due to the impact of climate change, the coastal area of Kien Giang
      (now An Giang) province is often affected by storms and tropical depressions. Former Hon
      Dat district was not directly affected by the storm. However, due to the effects of storm
      circulation, heavy rainfalls, thunderstorms, tornadoes, and lightning occurred in some
      locations, affecting local people’s production and lives.
      Rain: Rainfall has been lower than usual during the last ten years. There has been unseasonal
      rain throughout the dry season since the beginning of 2019. The district’s total rainfall in the
      first 4 months of 2019 was mostly from 20 to 50% greater than the long-term average. In
      2018, 28 houses were fallen, 30 houses were damaged, and 02 people were killed by
      lightning strikes as a result of heavy rains accompanied by thunderstorms and tornadoes,
      causing a loss of 1.14 billion VND. Natural calamities have caused the collapse of 2 houses
      and the damage of 4 others in the communes of Binh Son, Son Binh, My Thuan, and Tho
      Son since 2019, resulting in a loss of 100 million VND.
      Flood: The following agricultural damage occurred in September and October 2018 as a
      result of rapid flood water: (i) 59 ha of Summer-Autumn rice of 13 households were
      damaged at a level of 100% (in which: damage of 52 ha belongs to 9 households at Mui Tau
      hamlet, Binh Giang commune and 7ha of 4 households in My Thuan commune); and (ii)
      15,3 ha of vegetables were damaged (8.6 ha of 12 households in My Thuan commune and
      6.70 ha of 3 households in My Hiep Son commune). It caused 3 billion VND in material
      damage.
   182. Therefore, during the design of the road and the sluices, the project owner and detailed
   design consultants should take into account the evelation of the road and the sluices to mitigate
   inundation during rains, storms and floods.

   2.1.6.4. Coastal erosion

   183. Along the west coast from Ca Mau Cape to Rach Gia, erosion dominates accretion. Only
   some sections are affected by both erosion and accretion. In this area, the average erosion rate


   Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                              110   Page
                                                                                                              Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
is from 15 to 20m/year, with certain parts reaching 40 to 50m/year during high-wave years
[Southern Institute of Water Resources Research, 2019].
184. There have been changes every year to the mudflats on the western coast of Kien Giang
(now An Giang) province since 2009. Coastal erosion and accretion are affected by both the
weather and the flow of water from canals to the sea. Currently, the total length of the eroded
coastline is about 71.7 km, with 38.3 km located in the districts of An Minh and An Bien.
Approximately 500 ha of coastal mudflats have been eroded over the last decade, with a loss of
coastal forest belt spanning in width from 60 to 300 m. Especially, the width of the protection
forest loss in certain coastal areas of former An Bien, An Minh, and Hon Dat districts is up to
20 m each year. Severe erosion is causing the coastline to encroach further from the mainland
and the sea dike. There are places where the formerly protection forests no longer exist. The
sea dike has thus been directly hit by waves, endangering the safety of nearby buildings as well
as the people's production and daily activities (Figure 28).




Figure 28: Map of the current state of erosion and accretion of the Mekong Delta coast
185. In front of the sea dike, there is a relatively good protection forest belt with a width of 50
to 1,600m, such as Avicennia marina and Sonneratia caseolaris. However, due to a variety of
factors, certain parts of the protection forest have been damaged in recent years and can no
longer operate as sea dike protection (Figure 29). The project will not impact on the coastline
and protection forests in the project area.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 111   Page
                                                                                              Confidential
                    ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT




Figure 29: Protection forests have been damaged, and dikes are located directly facing the sea
in the project area

2.2. EXISTING ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY IN PROJECT AREAS

186. As part of the ESIA preparation, the Consultant conducted a survey and collected samples
of air, surface water, groundwater, wastewater, soil, sediment, and aquatic life in the project
area in November 2023. These samples were then sent to qualified laboratories (VIMCERT
200) for analysis. The map and detailed list of the samples can be found in Appendix 2, while
the analysis findings are presented in Appendix 3. The following are concise explanations of
the analysis results.

2.2.1.      Ambient Air Environmental Quality and Noise

187. A total of 10 sampling stations were selected in the project area to as the ambient air
quality and noise level. The ambient air samples were assigned codes ranging from KK1 to
KK10 (Figure 30). The analysis parameters were temperature, humidity, wind speed, wind
direction, air pressure, total suspended particulates (TSP), nitrogen dioxide (NO 2), carbon
monoxide (CO), sulfur dioxide (SO2), ammonia (NH3), and noise level. The air quality analysis
results were compared to the QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT4 and the ambient air quality criteria set
by the World Health Organization (WHO).
188. The analysis results of 10 air samples show that the levels of pollutants such as SO 2, CO,
NO2, TSP, and NH3 are lower than the QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT (Table 1 - hourly averages)
and the WHO's ambient air quality guidelines. These findings reveal that the ambient air quality
in the project region is reasonably good, with no indicators of pollution resulting from the local
population's socioeconomic development and livelihood.




4
    QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT: National technical regulations on ambient air quality

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                112   Page
                                                                                             Confidential
                    ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT




Figure 30: Air sampling sites in the project area in November 2023

2.2.2.      Water Environmental Quality

2.2.2.1. Groundwater quality

189. A total of 10 sampling stations were selected to survey the quality of groundwater in the
project area. The collected samples were labeled from G1 to G10 (Figure 31). The groundwater
was analyzed for various parameters including temperature, pH, total dissolved solids (TDS),
hardness, sulfate (SO42-), chloride (Cl-), ammonium (NH4+), nitrite (NO2-), nitrate (NO3-), total
iron (FeTs), arsenic (As), and coliform bacteria. The analysis results were compared with QCVN
09:2023/BTNMT5.
190. The results indicated that the groundwater quality is relatively good, and all analyzed
parameters meet QCVN 09:2023/BTNMT, except for the relatively low pH value of 5.03 at
location G8. Additionally, all groundwater samples have low levels of dissolved solids, ranging
from 141 to 402 mg/L. This indicates that groundwater in the drilled wells being exploited in
the area has not been affected by saltwater.




5
    QCVN 09:2023/BTNMT: National Technical Regulation on groundwater quality

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               113   Page
                                                                                            Confidential
                    ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT




Figure 31: Groundwater sampling sites in the project area in November 2023

2.2.2.2. Surface water quality

191. To evaluate the quality of the surface water in the project area, a total of 20 sampling
points were selected, with each location being sampled at two distinct periods (during high tide
and low tide), see Figure 32. The taken samples were coded from NM1 to NM20, relatively.
The analytical parameters for surface water were temperature, pH, DO, TDS, TSS, BOD 5,
COD, Turbidity, Cl-, NH4+, NO3-, NO2-, NO43-, Mn, FeTS, Oils & Greases, Ecoli, and Coliforms.
The analysis results were compared with QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT6.
192. Analysis results of 20 surface water samples indicate that the majority of surface water
samples in the area are still relatively good (meeting water quality Class B standards), with only
the locations near NH 80 showing slight signs of pollution (meeting water quality Class C
standards). At locations where water quality is classified as Class C, the concentrations of
organic matter still meet Class B standards.
193. Even during high tide, the water was completely fresh when samples were taken. This is
because the sampling occurred when the sea dike sluice gates were closed to retain freshwater
for the fields and the LXQ was being inundated.

6
    QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT: National technical regulation on Surface water quality

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                114   Page
                                                                                             Confidential
                    ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT




Figure 32: Surface water sampling sites in the project area in November 2023
194. Despite being classified as a high acid sulfate soil area, the pH levels of the water samples
show that they are not contaminated with acid sulfate. This indicates that water exchange was
relatively good during the sampling time, and floodwater from the LXQ was very effective in
flushing acid sulfate from the area.
195. Water sources heavily contaminated with bacteria often only meet Class C water quality
standards. This also reflects the current distribution of the population in the project area mainly
along canals and the low sanitation conditions. Most domestic wastewater of local people is
directly discharged into the canals, which is the main cause of bacterial contamination in the
water sources in the area.

2.2.2.3. Domestic wastewater quality

196. To assess the quality of wastewater in the project area, 10 wastewater samples were
collected in the project area. They were labeled NT1 to NT10, respectively (Figure 33). The
analytical parameters for domesticwater were temperature, pH, DO, TDS, TSS, BOD 5, COD,
Turbidity, Cl-, NH4+, NO3-, NO2-, NO43-, Mn, FeTS, Oils & Greases, Ecoli, and Coliforms. The
analysis results were compared with QCVN 14-MT:2015/BTNMT 7.
197. The analysis results indicate heavy organic pollution in the wastewater in the area, with
COD values ranging from 31.5 to 127 mg/L. All collected wastewater samples are untreated
domestic wastewater discharged into the environment from households and residential areas.
However, the amount of wastewater from these sources is low, so the impact on the receiving
water quality is low also.




7
    QCVN 14-MT:2015/BTNMT: National technical regulation on domestic wastewater.

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 115   Page
                                                                                              Confidential
                     ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
198. It is noted that the wastewater samples are heavily contaminated with coliform, ranging
from 630,000 to 1,500,000 MPN/100 mL. This will be a source of microbial pollution for the
surface water samples collected in the area.




Figure 33: Wastewater sampling sites in the project area in November 2023

2.2.3.      Soil/Sediment Quality

2.2.3.1. Soil quality

199. There were totally 16 sampling points selected for surveying soil quality in the project
area. The taken samples were coded from D1 to D16, relatively (Figure 34). The analytical
parameters for soil samples were pHH2O, pHKCl, TOC, TN, TP, Pdigestable, EC, As, Cd, Pb, Cu,
Cr, Zn. The analysis results were compared with QCVN 03:2023/BTNMT 8.
200. The analysis results show that all 16 soil samples from the project area have heavy metal
concentrations within the permissible limits set by QCVN 03:2023/BTNMT. This indicates that
the heavy metal content in all soil samples is suitable for various land use purposes such as
agriculture, residential, industrial, commercial, and service activities. Additionally, the organic
matter content in the soil indicates that the soil samples are on average, ranging from 1.72 to
2.88%. The pH values of the soil samples range from 3.20 to 4.79, classified as moderately to
heavily acidic soil.


8
    QCVN 03:2023/BTNMT: National technical regulation on soil quality.

2.2.3.2. Sediment quality

201. There were totally 16 sampling points selected for surveying sediment quality in the
project area. The taken samples were coded from TT1 to TT16, relatively. The analytical
parameters for sediment samples were pHH2O, pHKCl, TOC, TN, TP, EC, As, Cd, Pb, Cu, Cr,
Zn. The analysis results were compared with QCVN 47:2017/BTNMT.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                        117   Page
                                                                                     Confidential
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT




Figure 35: Sediment sampling sites in the project area in November 2023
202. The sediment sample analysis results indicated that all analyzed parameters in all
sediment samples collected in the project area were within the permissible standards as outlined
in QCVN 47:2017/BTNMT. The concentration of heavy metals (As, Cd, Pb, Cu, Cr, Zn) meet
the QCVN. This implies that the sediment generated during the excavation of the sluices will
be reused for the sluice embankments.

2.3. SOCIO-ECONOMIC CONDITIONS

2.3.1.     Economic Conditions

2.3.1.1. Agriculture - forestry - fishery

203. 2-rice-crop production: is concentrated in the north Don Dong canal. In 2022, the
cultivated rice land area was 51,216 ha, and the average yield was 6.23 tons/ha, resulting in a
total output of 319,170 tons (see Table 36). Project communes continue to carry out plans to
restructure the agricultural sector, and improve the use of scientific and technological advances
linked with production and consumption links. This contributes to the improvement of rice
product quality and value.

205. Farming model of industrial whiteleg shrimp: This model has the advantage of causing
minimal harm to the shrimp while being profitable. After approximately 3 months, each 500
m2 pond raising white leg shrimp will yield 1 ton of shrimp, with the selling price ranging from
120,000 to 130,000 VND/kg. It has high economic efficiency but demands a large investment
capital, making it difficult for people to invest in. Currently, Binh Giang commune has roughly
49 hectares of extensive whiteleg shrimp farming, while Binh Son commune has 50 households
cultivating extensive whiteleg shrimp on a small scale.
206. Livestock: in 2022, the buffalo herd was 61 heads, the pig herd was 800 heads, and the
poultry herd was 5,000 heads, with a total output value of VND 6.71 billion.
207. Vegetables: In 2022, the total crop area of the project area was 510 ha, the average output
reached 7,650 tons, and the average yield was 15 tons/ha. The total value of vegetable output
was 38.25 billion VND.
208. Pineapple: About 247 ha of land is available for pineapple growing in the project area,
with most of it being in Binh Giang commune. Each hectare produces about 9.8 tons of
pineapples, for a total output of 180 tons/year (Table 38). This crop generates substantial profits
for the farmers. Because of this, this area needs to be taken into account when investing in
saltwater supply works.



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                      119     Page
                                                                                                     Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
209. Collective economy: The project communes kept up the support for the growth of the
collective economy through propaganda, advocacy, and good policy implementation. As a
result, 5 agricultural cooperatives and 5 farmer groups were formed. In addition, they regularly
review to consolidate the Steering Committee for collective economic growth in the communes.
Besides, they hold conferences on evaluating and classifying agricultural cooperatives and
farmer groups. They also helped cooperatives hold their yearly meetings in line with the Law
on Cooperatives. Furthermore, they developed and implemented a plan to link the production
and consumption of agricultural products between agricultural cooperatives and enterprises.

2.3.1.2. Industry

212. In 2022, the value of industrial production was 985.8 billion VND. Furthermore, total
retail sales of goods were 5,214 billion VND, while revenue from accommodation and catering
services was VND 579 billion.

2.3.1.3. Cottage industry, trade, and services

213. In general, the cottage industry and service development in the project area lagged behind
expectations. There are 59 companies and business households, including 04 rice milling plants,
05 minor repair establishments, 07 service providers, 01 ice-making factory, and 02 lime
processing facilities, and the remainder are small trading households.

2.3.2.     Social Conditions

2.3.2.1. Population, distribution of population, and labor

a) Population

214. According to 2022 figures, the project communes has a population of 29,035 people in
7,810 households (HHs, with a male population of 15,000 people (51.67%) and a female
population of 14,035 people (48.34%). About 25.80% of the people live in urban areas, while
74.20% live in rural places. It has an average population density of 84 people per km 2, but most
of the people live in the center of communes and along the main canals (Table 39 and Figure
37). It is very dangerous for the dike route because some households are building homes and
growing crops on the dike surface and safety corridor. Within the project area (approximately
5,500 ha), covering Ranh Hat, Giong Ke, No. 4, and Binh Hoa hamlets in Binh Giang commune,
as well as Vam Ray and Thuan An hamlets in Binh Sơn commune, the total population is 10,579
people, residing in approximately 2,650 households.

b) Labor

215. The human resource situation in the project area and the former Hon Dat district as a
whole has improved significantly between 2010 and 2020. An increase of 3.6 times occurred
from 14.8% in 2010 to 54.09% in 2020 in the proportion of trained laborers. Despite the fact
that the rural labor force in the agricultural sector is substantial (approximately 51.5%), its
quality is substandard and its application of advanced science and technology to production
remains limited. Rural laborers in particular continue to be deprived of a material and spiritually
prosperous existence due to the persistently low income system.
216. When people in rural places get vocational training, they mostly make their jobs because
there isn’t a strong link between training companies and hiring companies. A significant
proportion of the total number of trained workers (49.7%) are technical workers without
qualifications, although the proportion of trained workers has increased.

c) Incomes

217. The project area is part of the flooded Long Xuyen Quadrangle area, and the economy is
purely agricultural, so the technical infrastructure and people's lives in the area are still difficult,
with 3.52% of households being poor and 3.68% being near-poor (Table 40).
218. Between 2010 and 2015, annual per-person income averaged 42,5 million Vietnamese
Dong (VND). In recent years, the economies of the two project communes have had many
positive changes and achieved substantial successes in many respects, thus people's lives have
gradually improved. Infrastructure is being improved in various areas such as transportation,

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                     122   Page
                                                                                                  Confidential
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
irrigation, education, healthcare, and cultural facilities. This has led to an overall improvement
in the quality of life for the people, both in terms of material and spiritual aspects. Consequently,
the per capita income raised to 55 million VND per person each year by 2016-2020.

2.3.2.2. Ethnic composition

219. Kinh people accounts 87.92% of the people in the project area, Khmer people accounts
11.03%, and other ethnic groups, including Chinese, accounts 1.05%, see details in Table 41.
In recent years, the economic and socio-cultural life situation of ethnic minorities (EMs) in the
area has remained steady and developed. Many policies have been put in place to make it easier
for them to plan holidays and traditional festivities. Also, with a total budget of VND 514.8
million, the locality has collaborated with the Center for Water and Sanitation to hand over 750-
liter plastic tanks to 396 EM houses and poor households in extremely difficult hamlets.
Furthermore, 31,144 health insurance cards have been distributed to EMs and poor people in
coastal districts.
220. According to the project’s RP, construction of the sluices and the road will acquire land
of 16 Khmer households and will impacts on Khmer households living near the construction
sites. Details are in Chapter 4. The Khmer people are economically integrated with the Kinh;
however, the Khmer community still maintains its own cultural–traditional institutions,
customs, social structure, and language, which are legally recognized as part of their distinct
ethnic identity. At the same time, they are also households affected by land acquisition and
livelihood impacts. Therefore, FPIC will be applied to ensure meaningful consultation and
secure the support of the Khmer community for the project.

2.3.2.3. Education and training

221. In the project area, there are currently 14 schools of all levels, including 04 kindergartens,
04 primary schools, 01 secondary school, 03 secondary + high schools, and 02 high schools.


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                                      123    Page
                                                                                                                    Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
There are a total of 213 classrooms and function rooms. The total number of teachers is 324
and the total number of pupils is 6,620 [Former Hon Dat District Statistics Office, 2023].
222. According to the result of social and economic surveys of affected households in the
project area show that the affected people in the project area have a relatively low level of
education, as indicated by the results of the household survey. Of the 260 members of the
surveyed families, 249 have attended school, accounting for 95.8%, and 11 are illiterate,
accounting for 4.2%. As a result, the proportion of persons who do not attend school in the
project area exceeds the national average (2.15%, according to the Ministry of Education and
Training in 2019). The top three reasons for not attending school are school expenses, the need
to work from home, and a distaste for school. More than that, most of the people who went to
school only finished secondary school (33.5%) or elementary school (31.9%), and only 3.8%
went to college or higher. Thus, it is important to have the correct strategy in place when
consulting with stakeholders about the project's compensation policies, entitlement, and
suggested technical options, and when disseminating information about the project, so that the
communities can grasp its importance and contribute to its success.

2.3.2.4. Health

223. In 2022, there were 2 health stations with a total of 12 beds in the project area. The 2
stations met the Ministry of Health criteria. The stations employ a total of 21 people, including
2 doctors, 2 pharmacists, 7 physicians, and 3 midwives. In 2022, 273 children under the age of
one received vaccines [Former Hon Dat District Statistics Office, 2023].
224. As such, the medical facilities and staff in the project area are limited. Consequently, the
labor influx of the project may strain the local healthcare system.

2.3.2.5. Socio-economic conditions of affected households of the project

During the resettlement plan (RP) preparation, the socio-economic survey (SES) of AHs was
conducted for 61 affected households in the project area. The details are in the project’s RP.
The results are summarised below:
225. Family size: According to the survey results, there are 260 people in 61 AHs, with an
average of 4.26 people in each HH, in which: households with 3-4 members account for the
highest percentage (46,7%), followed by households with 5-6 members (26,7%), and
households with 1-2 members account for 26.7%. There are 16 EM households with 65 people
in the project area.
226. Age profile: The survey results show that of 260 surveyed people, people of working age
(18-60 years old) accounted for 58.1%, followed by 27.3% of those of school age (<18 years
old), and 14.6% of those over 60 years old.
227. Ocupation: The occupational structure of working-age respondents in the household
survey shows that 46,4% of the respondents are involved in agriculture (cultivation, livestock
raising, and aquaculture), followed by workers in companies.
228. Income: The survey results show that the average monthly income per capita of 61
affected households is VND 2,360,000. In which, the average monthly income per capita of the
poor group in the survey area is 1,140,000 VND, while that of the middle group is 2,170,000
VND and the better income group is 3,850,000VND. Thus, compared with the income
classification standards of Decree No. 07/ND-CP dated January 27, 2021 applied to rural areas,


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               124   Page
                                                                                            Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
there are 10 affected households as poor, accounting for 16.39%, and 23 households with
average income, accounting for 54.09%, and 18 households with a well-off income, accounting
for 29.5%.
229. Water supply and quality: The results of the socio-economic survey show that the
percentage of affected households having access to clean water sources for drinking and daily
life is quite high. The main water sources of water used for drinking are groundwater (48
households) and bottled water (9 households). The survey also shows that up to 78.7% of
surveyed households said that the water source for their drinking and household activities was
clear and clean, whereas 14.8% reported that the water was clear.
230. Sanitation conditions: The survey results indicate that 53 households (86.9%) have
septic toilets, and 8 households (13.17%) have simple flush toilets. However, there is currently
no wastewater drainage infrastructure in this area; hence, all domestic wastewater is discharged
directly into the environment.
231. Power supply: The survey results show that all 61 surveyed households (100%) use
electricity for daily life. Furthermore, 100% of the interviewed households use gas for cooking,
while only 23% use wood.
232. Access loans: Taking out a loan is a common occurrence in rural communities. The
survey in the project area illustrates that the majority of interviewed households (71.5%) are
currently in debt. On the loan scale, 85.6% of households borrowed less than VND 60 million.
The loan was taken out primarily to allow children and grandchildren to attend school.
Generally, households do not take out larger loans due to unstable production conditions and
low-profit returns from agricultural production, as well as a low ability to repay the loan capital
and interest. The Bank for Social Policies is the most important source of funds for local
households, accounting for 21,5% of all loans. The Bank for Agriculture and Rural
Development comes next, providing about 20% of the borrowed funds. The remaining 5% of
household borrowing comes primarily from credit funds managed by the local Women's Union
and Farmer's Union. The Bank for Social Policies has the lowest interest rates, ranging from
0.65 to 0.8% over one month (depending on the borrower's socio-economic situation).
Households typically borrow money over three years to invest in livestock (such as a cow or
pig) or to start a business.
233. Social connection: According to the survey results, PAHs receive only 1-2% of their
support from friends and neighbors, while they rely heavily on the support of siblings, parents,
children, and local governments and organizations, such as Farmers’ Union, Women’s Union,
Youth Union, and Veterans Association, in which:
    Farmers’ Union has a function that directs and coordinates the implementation of a variety
     of programs and projects on economic, cultural, and social development in rural areas, as
     well as guiding members in the development of collective economies in agriculture and
     rural areas. Members of the union receive technical assistance in cultivation/livestock
     production, as well as participate in vocational training and science and technology transfer
     activities to aid in the development of production, the improvement of community life, and
     the protection of the environment. Furthermore, the Union is entrusted by the Bank for
     Social Policies with assisting its members in borrowing loans for family economic
     development or building houses/clean water/toilet facilities.
    Women's Union has taken an active role in empowering women economically by helping
     women start their own businesses. This helps achieve the goals of reducing poverty,

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 125   Page
                                                                                              Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
     promoting sustainable development, and improving social welfare. Members of the Union
     can be able to participate in short training courses/sessions that teach them about economic
     development, agricultural and rural restructuring, the promotion of intensive farming,
     diversifying products, and promoting the development of high-quality agriculture. In
     addition, the Women's Union of Hon Dat district helps its members who need to launch a
     business to strengthen the family economy. The Union is also authorized by the Bank for
     Social Policies to provide its members with loans for family economic growth or the
     construction of houses/clean water/toilet facilities.
    Youth Union: tasks a mass organization of young people in the area to create an
     environment for young people to participate in activities that will help them train and
     develop the personality and capacity to meet the requirements of society. Its members
     receive support and encouragement to use scientific and technological advancements in
     production, business, and economic growth. In addition, members receive loans for
     economic development through the Bank for Social Policies, loans from the Central Union,
     support for seedlings and seeds, and assistance with the development of scientific and
     technological models.
    Veterans Association is a well-known and well-respected organization in the community.
     Members participating in the association will be supported with credit if needed for
     economic development/medical treatment, in addition to meaningful activities for
     members in difficult circumstances (building houses of gratitude).
234. The survey results show that the most affected people are members of the Farmer's Union
(11 people, accounting for 50%), followed by the Women's Union (10 people, accounting for
45.5%). In other associations, like the Association of the Elderly, the Veterans' Union, and the
Youth Union, fewer people join. At least one member of each family belongs to at least one
organization, and some people belong to more than one.
235. Decision-making in the households. When asked who makes the major decisions in the
household, i.e. who is the householder, the responses were 65% for husbands and 16% for
wives. Other family members were reported by 19% of respondents (Figure 38). This
percentage reflects the common family scenario in Vietnam, where the husband in the family
is thought to have a more vital role in decision-making than his wife and is considered the
householder in most circumstances.




Figure 38: Percentage of husbands, wives, others as householders
236. Land rights of men and women. In terms of landownership among interviewees, the
majority of husbands own land-use rights certificates (41%), whereas 13% of females own land.
Other family members are landowners in 20% of the cases among the interviewees. Only 12%
of the interviewees answered that the names of both spouses were registered in the land rights

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               126 P a g e
                                                                                             Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
certificates. In general, despite the new land regulation promulgated in 2004, which allows for
having the names of both husband and wife in their land-use rights certificates, there are still
much fewer female landowners than male landowners. The reason for this conservation of male
landownership is that the transfer of land ownership to the woman is only being made in case
of the death of the husband or divorce. With the land-use rights certificate as mortgage property,
the owner can loan money from the bank for their business. Being the landowner can result in
significant legal and financial assets and result in increased financial independence. This figure
shows that men have three times more often access to land and related loans than women.
237. Gender-relevant participation in shrimp farming. Men, usually the husbands of the
families, do most of the “hard work” in shrimp farming. This is because that's what people in
the area think is proper for men and women in terms of work. The percentages range from 59%
to 62% of all those asked. In some cases, both men and women worked together. 70% of the
work that is done by both men and women is harvesting shrimp. When raising shrimp, this is
the most important step. Not only does it require the work of both parents, but the whole family
needs to be involved. The process of deciding about the price for selling the shrimp (Figure 39)
is being done with equal participation of both spouses as 50% of the respondents answered. The
participation of women who stated to be responsible for this task is slightly higher than the
male, with 27% for females and 23% for men. This indicates that the role of the wife/ female
regarding the pricing of the shrimp is mostly equal to that of men. However, it needs to be taken
into account that most men are making the decisions regarding expenses of higher amounts.




Figure 39: Participation of men and women in various steps of extensive shrimp farming
238. Gender-relevant participation in rice farming. The results of the interviews with 30
rice farming households revealed that the man charged for the majority of the job kinds,
including soil preparation, rice sowing, rice curing, rice harvest, rice carrying, and decision-
making on rice selling price, with 46% to 75% of each type of work. As a result, such tasks
require the labor, skills, and experience of both male and female farmers. Male
farmers/husbands answered that they not only worked in the field but also participated in
decisions on rice selling prices (59%), see details in Figure 40.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                127 P a g e
                                                                                              Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT




Figure 40: Participation of men and women in rice farming
239. Gender differences in wages. In terms of incomes for men and women, the survey
showed that males consistently earn more than females. While men earn an average of 125,000-
200,000 VND per day, women earn an average of 70,000-100,000 VND per day in the two
communes, resulting in a salary gap of 31.2% lower for women.
240. Gendered caretaking of children. Concerning men's and women's engagement in
childcare, including supplying food, drinking water, sanitation, and health care (taking care of
sick children), about 87% of all respondents claimed that these duties are performed by women.
Men participated in these home tasks at a relatively low rate (only 3%). See details in Figure
41.




Figure 41: Participation of women and men in taking care of children
241. Participation of women and men in training. Figure 42 demonstrates that more female
than male respondents reported having attended seminars, training, or meetings about
livelihood, occupation, or everyday labor. Out of 114 respondents, 47% were women and 32%
were men who participated in such activities. 10% of respondents participated with their spouse.
11% of respondents’ other relatives (sons, moms, and fathers) engaged in such training.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               128 P a g e
                                                                                             Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT




Figure 42: Participation of women and men in seminars, trainings, meetings on livelihood,
occupation, daily labor
242. Women’s clubs and social activities. The higher participation of women can be
explained by the existence of various women’s clubs in most of the surveyed communes where
women can meet for social activities. These clubs are usually well accepted and play an
important place for local women to meet, socialize, exchange information, learn, and develop
their capacities in many fields. Especially, the clubs for capital pooling and family planning
shall be highlighted as significant hubs for development. Reports from the Family Planning
Club show that the activities carried out contributed to reducing the number of childbirths in
the locality and to improving the economic situation of the participating households. These
clubs help improve the general knowledge and technology of farming for women.

2.4. TECHNICAL INFRASTRUCTURES IN THE PROJECT AREA

2.4.1.   Transportation Infrastructure

2.4.1.1. External transport system

243. Road traffic: The road infrastructure in the project area is underdeveloped. Previously,
the region was served by a single national highway, NH80, which spanned 46.5km. It has a 15-
meter-wide asphalt concrete road surface and a 22.5-meter-wide roadbed. Beginning in Rach
Gia city, it passes through the towns of Soc Son and Hon Dat (formerly), as well as the
communes of My Lam, Son Kien, Binh Son, and Binh Giang before ending at the border of
Kien Luong district. It’s the main road in the project area that links it to Rach Gia and Ha Tien,
two big cities.
244. Waterway: The canal system in the project region is well-developed and convenient in
general. It serves most commodities and travelers traveling inside the area as well as across
adjacent provinces, including the RG-HT canal - an inland waterway route of class III,
accommodating boats of 500 tons or tugboats of 900 tons. But there isn’t much boat movement
in the system and mostly small boats. Below are summarised main waterway routes in the
project area:
  Main canals: There are five main canals (Tuan Thong, Binh Giang 1, No.6, No.9, and Vam
   Ray) that connect to the RG-HT canal and ultimately run into the West Sea. The canals have
   varying widths between 30 and 60 meters (as indicated in (Table 44 and Figure 44), which
   is adequate for the navigation of boats weighing between 300 and 500 tons. Presently, as a
   result of the absence of coordinated road infrastructure in the region, these waterways play

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                129   Page
                                                                                             Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
   a vital role in transporting raw materials and products for local agricultural and aquacultural
   activities in the project area.
  Horizontal canal 200: serves as the northern boundary of the project, which is situated
   approximately 800 meters from NH80. This canal is relatively small and sees minimal boat
   traffic. It is primarily responsible for the water supply and drainage of the region (Figure
   44).
  Don Dong canal, which intersects the project area, is situated around 3.5 kilometers away
   from NH80 and the coastline (Figure 44). It efficiently partitions the project into northern
   and southern sections and functions as a crucial canal for local inhabitants. In addition to its
   role in providing water supply and drainage, the canal also functions as a means of transit
   for residents to convey agricultural and aquacultural machinery, supplies, and products.

2.4.1.2. Internal roads

245. In the 2 project communes, a grade-V-delta asphalt road is being built on the sea dyke
route from Linh Huynh bridge to Hon Queo sluice. Besides, a number of other provincial and
district roads are paved with asphalt or concrete to make driving in the area easier. Furthermore,
rural roads leading to commune towns have been constructed. However, the majority of these
roads only accommodate small cars and primitive vehicles, as follows [Former Hon Dat DPC,
2022]:
  Binh Giang commune: 13 routes with 106.5km long, including 17.8km of cement concrete
   road (accounting for 16.7%) and 88.7km of dirt road (accounting for 83.3%).
  Binh Son commune: 5 routes with 30.4 km long, of which 100% are dirt roads.

246. Furthermore, road traffic within the project area remains fairly limited, including (Figure
43):
  The road along the east bank of the Tuan Thong canal connecting NH80 to the sea has been
   paved with concrete. However, the road width is only about 2.5-3 meters, which can only
   accommodate motor vehicles under 4 tons.
  The road along the Binh Giang 1 canal: Both the east and west banks of Binh Giang 1 canal
   are dirt paths, with widths of 1 - 2 meters. Therefore, it is difficult for non-motorized and
   motorized vehicles can travel during the rainy season.
  The road along Canal No. 6: The west bank remains a dirt path, while the east bank has been
   concreted. However, the road is quite narrow, with a width of 2 - 2.5 meters. It is appropriate
   for rural traffic, as it can accommodate motorcycles and vehicles that weigh less than 2.5
   tons.
  The road along canal 9: Like the road alongside canal 6, the western bank remains unpaved
   while the eastern bank has been paved with concrete. Nevertheless, the road remains narrow,
   with a width ranging from 2 to 2.5 meters, making it suited for rural traffic and capable of
   fitting motorbikes and cars weighing less than 2.5 tons.
  The road along Vam Ray Canal: The west bank, which is within the project area, has been
   concreted. However, the road is narrow, ranging from 3 to 3.5 meters, and only
   accommodates two-wheeled vehicles and small cars under 2.5 tons.
  The road along Canal 200: This road is low of quality, as it is approximately 200 meters
   from NH80. The south bank of the project area is primarily composed of field paths that are

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 130   Page
                                                                                              Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
   defined by the borders of rice fields. These pathways are used by farmers to transport and
   care their crops. Along the north border of this canal, there is no road.
  Don Giong Canal: The south bank has been concreted with a width of 2 to 2.5 meters, but
   this route still lacks many bridges over the canals, causing incomplete traffic flow, mainly
   suitable for two-wheeled motorcycles.
  The road over the sea dike: Previously, it was only appropriate for motorcycles and
   pedestrians. It is currently a coastal corridor road connecting Kien Luong to Rach Gia.
247. In general, the road network fails to adequately meet the travel needs of local people,
both currently and in the future, while waterway transportation proves to be highly convenient
for them. As a result, the project's waste will be conveyed via roads, while its construction
materials will be transported by waterway. However, the number of trips needed to transfer
waste is low. Therefore, impacts of the transporation of raw materials and waste of the project
on local movement will be small.




Figure 43: Existing roads in the project area

2.4.2.    Water Supply

248. The water utilized in the daily lives of people in the project area is taken from 3 main
sources: surface water, groundwater (via groundwater plants), and rainwater. The groundwater


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                              131 P a g e
                                                                                            Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
in the project area is extracted via local people wells from depths ranging from 80 to 100m, and
its quality is suitable for human consumption. There are 4,540 private and state wells and 2
clean water supply stations with a capacity of 80m 3/day/station managed by local authorities in
the project area. The 2 water supply stations take surface water and only provide water for
houses in and around the commune center (Table 42). Approximately 87% of households have
access to clean and sanitary water.

Source: General report on Kien Giang (now An Giang) provincial planning in the period of 2021 -
2030 and vision to 2050 (2023)

2.4.3.    Solid Wastes Management

249. Currently, only domestic solid waste from the Cau So Hai market to the commune center
along NH 80 is collected and transported to Tam Sinh Nghia’s waste treatment facility (capacity
500 tons/day) in Rach Gia City for treatment. The remaining waste is either sent to 2 landfills
(Table 43) or people dispose of it through burning and landfilling in their gardens. Also, some
households who live near canals dispose of their waste straight into the canals, while others
burn it. Both, however, don’t meet technical and environmental sanitation standards, which
hurts the ecosystem around them.
250. The project owner/contractor will enter into a contract with the legal entity of Binh Giang
commune landfill and Binh Son commune landfill to collect and treat the waste in compliance
with current regulations at a minimum of 3 times per week. For construction sites where current
collection vehicles cannot reach, the project owner/contractor will arrange for their workers to
transport the waste to the nearest collection transfer point for proper collection and treatment.

2.4.4.    Electrical Supply

251. The proportion of households with access to electricity in the project area reached
99.05% from 2016 to 2020, an increase of 0.91% from 2010 to 2025 [Former Hon Dat District
Statistics Office, 2023].
252. The expansion of the electrical grid has significantly altered the face of rural areas,
contributing to the improvement of socio-cultural life, the strengthening of national defense and

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 132     Page
                                                                                                Confidential
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
security, and the formation of residential clusters. Presently, the administration of the public
power system is inadequate due to insufficient fixing equipment and the extensive array of
devices that require supervision, as well as the degradation of the system. Consequently, certain
regions frequently experience power outages. Therefore, the installation of the power lines will
help to improve the electrical supply in the project area.

2.4.5.   Irrigation

253. In the project area, there are 4 primary canals totaling 36.65 km in length that are
responsible for water supply and drainage for secondary canals (Table 44 and Figure 44). There
are also 9 secondary canals totaling 67.7 km in length (Table 45), as well as 46.84 km of tertiary
and infield canals. Secondary, tertiary, and infield canals are responsible for providing water
and drainage for about 5,500 hectares of cultivated land in the region.
254. In the livelihood model demonstration area of former Hon Dat district, the West Coast
sluices for salinity intrusion prevention and flood drainage have been completed for the
conversion of rice cultivation to sustainable aquaculture. There is also the Vam Ray sluice,
which was built in 1999 as part of the West Sea flood drainage project. Furthermore, Kien
Giang (now An Giang) province has invested in a closed sluice system, which includes T5,
Kenh Hai, Binh Giang 1, and Binh Giang 2, to support the area's production (Table 46 and
Figure 45).
255. Embankments have been invested along the majority of primary and secondary canals for
production purposes. Certain embankments, including Canal 200, Vam Ray Canal, and Tuan
Thong canal, have been reinforced for rural traffic. By operating the existing network of canals,
sluices, and embankments and the project sluices, water can be managed to accommodate a
variety of production patterns in the region.

256. In addition, in the project area, there is the Hon Dat - Kien Luong sea dike, including:
  The section from sluice No. 2 to Hon Soc is 30,600m long and has the main dyke and
   branches that connect to sluices on the dyke:
    The main dyke route is 25,683m long, going along the coastal protection forest invested in
      the period 2000-2005. It is covered by on-site soil. It has a width of 6m, a top elevation of
      +2.0m, a slope of 2, and a greenfield width of 10m. There are 10 sluices along the route,
      each with an aperture ranging from 3 to 22.5 m and a total aperture width of 88.5 m. The
      sluices are utilized for salinity regulation, freshwater retention, and floodwater drainage.

     m, a top elevation of +1.5m, and a slope of 1.5
  Section from Vam Ray sluice to Binh Giang II sluice: It is 1475m long and 6m wide, with a
   greenfield width of 15 to 18m and a canal width of 8 to 10m along the sea dike. Few
   individuals live on the river bank of the sea dike. The portion has a 605m branch that
   connects to the Binh Giang II sluice.




Figure 46: Current status of the sea dike from Vam Ray sluice to Binh Giang II sluice
  Section from Binh Giang II sluice to T6 sluice: is 2,090 m long, 6 m wide. The width of the
   greenfield is 6-8 m. This section is around 220 m from the sea. The fieldward canal is 12 to
   15m wide, while the seaward canal is 15 to 17 meters wide. Aquaculture is practiced by
   locals on both sides of the section and near the sea. Both canals are used for water supply
   for shrimp farming. On the landward side of the section, there is a protection forest ranging
   in width from 30 to 50 m; however, certain areas lack protection forests. Two soil sub-
   sections that connect to T6 are 390 meters in length, and 4 meters in width, have a crest
   elevation of +1.8 meters, and a 1.5-meter slope.
  Section from T6 sluice to Tuan Thong sluice (T5): has a length of 4,885m, width of 6m,
   crest elevation of +2.0m, slope of 2. The seaward canal is used mostly for taking soil for the
   dike section, but not for the Giong Ke sub-section. The fieldward canal is used for water
   drainage. 400m of the section has eroded but no coastal protection works are planned for it.
   The sub-section linking to the Tuan Thong sluice on the left side is 300m long and made of
   soil, with a width of 5m, a crest elevation of +1.8m, and a slope of 1.
  Section from T6 sluice to Binh Giang I sluice: is 2,450m long, dike surface of about 6m,
   dike crest elevation of +2.0m, slope of 2. In this section, the canal for taking soil is on the
   seaside, while the drainage canal is on the field side. The greenfield is 15-17m wide. The
   canal inside the dyke has an inconsistent width, ranging from 7-18m, there is an outer canal
   10-12m wide about 1000m from the T6 canal. T6 sluice is about 200m from the sea dike.
   The protection forest in this section is very thin, from 0-100m. This section is sparsely
   populated.
  Section from Binh Giang I sluice to Tuan Thong sluice (T5): is 2,435m long with a dike
   surface of about 6m, crest elevation of +2.0m, slope of 2. The canal for taking soil is located
   on the sea side and the drainage canal is on the field side. The greenfield is 15 to 20m wide,
   and the air space on the seaside is 12-15m wide. The canal inside the dyke is 8-10m wide,
   and the land canal on the sea side is 10-12m wide. This portion is in good condition, with
   the road on the dyke surface covered with concrete b=1.5m and not used for aquaculture.
   The majority of the locals live at the head of the Tuan Thong sluice. The dike sub-section

2.5. ENVIRONMENTAL                   AND       SOCIAL   CONDITIONS         AT     SPECIFIC
     PROJECT SITES

2.5.1.   The 23 sluices

258. Locations: the 23 are situated on canals of Don Dong, K3- Tuan Thong, No. 200 and the
canal along the National defense dike within Binh Giang and Binh Son communes (Table 10).
259. Social status: The majority of the sluices are situated in sparsely inhabited regions,
encompassed by vacant land and a few trees (Figure 48). Table 47 provides the features of the
sluices. There are 2 water supply plants in the project area, namely Binh Giang commune plant
and Binh Son commune plant. The 2 plants with 80m3 per day each take surface water to supply
for 534 households (see Table 42). The water intake point of the Binh Giang plant is 200m from
the T6-1 sluice and 320m from the VR-1 sluice for the Binh Son plant (Figure 49 and Figure
50).




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                              137   Page
                                                                                           Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT




Figure 48: Surrounding area of the sluice construction area
260. Environmental status: Air quality in this area is quite good. Water quality on the canals
where the sluices will be buit is not contaminated with acid sulfate. This indicates that water
exchange was relatively good during the sampling time, and floodwater from the LXQ was very
effective in flushing acid sulfate from the area. Water sources heavily contaminated with
bacteria often only meet Class C water quality standards. This also reflects the current
distribution of the population in the project area mainly along canals and the low sanitation
conditions. Most domestic wastewater of local people is directly discharged into the canals,
which is the main cause of bacterial contamination in the water sources in the area. Soil in this
area is classified as moderately to heavily acidic soil. Sediments in the canal have
concentrations of heavy metals meet the current standards.

2.5.2.    The upgraded sluices

261. Locations: the upgraded sluices are located on Don Dong canal (13 sluices) and along the
sea dike (5 sluices).




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                            145 P a g e
                                                                                          Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
262. Social status: Around these sluices, there are no residents living nearby. The sluice gates
are operated automatically based on the tidal differences, which affects water source regulation
for production. Additionally, some of the wooden sluice gates are currently decayed, causing
saltwater leakage into freshwater production areas (Figure 51).




Figure 51: Existing conditions of some sluices to be improved in the project
263. Environmental status: Air quality in this area is quite good. Water quality on the canals
where the sluices will be buit is not contaminated with acid sulfate.

2.5.3.    The connecting road

264. Location: The road is situated on the eastern side of Binh Giang 1 canal, commencing at
NH80 and terminating at the seadike. It spans a total length of 7.03 kilometers (Figure 9).
Currently, it is unpaved and of poor quality, making it challenging for people to travel,
especially during the rainy season (Figure 52). The east side of the road is Binh Giang 1 canal
and the west side of the road is rice fields and fruit gardens of local people (Figure 54 and
Figure 55).

2.6. ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL SENSITIVE RECEPTORS IN THE
     PROJECT AREAS

2.6.1.   Cultural Resources

2.6.1.1. Physical cultural heritage

270. Hon Dat district (formerly) not only includes many historical sites but also magnificent
landscapes, making it a popular tourist destination. Among them is the Ba Hon historical relic,
particularly the tomb of Hero of the People's Armed Forces Phan Thi Rang (also known as Ms.
Su), the faithful daughter of Ba Hon land, and the Military Hospital cave, Cave No.5, Tam Bao
Pagoda, Ky Vien Pagoda on Hon Queo mountain, etc. However, all these monuments and
works are far (up to 5km) from the project’s construction sites. Thus, there will be no direct
impacts during preparation and construction phases.

2.6.1.2. Intangible cultural resources

271. Former Hon Dat district hosts several significant festivals, such as the memorial
ceremony for Nguyen Trung Truc, held at various temples dedicated to him across the district's
communes. The Ky Yen Festival at village temples attracts a large number of worshippers.
Several pagodas, temples, and shrines located in the Hon Dat, Hon Me, and Hon Queo
mountains also serve as spiritual destinations for people from within and outside the province.
Notably, the memorial ceremony for Phan Thi Rang, on January 9th each year, draws thousands
of participants. However, these festival sites are far from the project area, taken place outsite
the project area.

2.6.2.   Other Sensitive Receptors in the Project Areas

2.6.2.1. In the construction area of the sluices

272. The majority of sluices are strategically chosen for placement in areas with low
population density. Therefore, there are relatively few sensitive receptors within a 300m radius
of the project items, see Table 48 and Figure 56 to Figure 62.

3.2. “WITH PROJECT” TECHNICAL DESIGN ALTERNATIVES

3.2.1.   Options for the construction of sluices

3.2.1.1. Options for sluice arrangement

277. With the aim of shifting from the model of two crops of rice/vegetables to the shrimp-
rice model according to the planning of MD and Kien Giang (now An Giang) province for the
period 2021-2030 and the vision to 2050 approved by the Prime Minister, it is necessary to

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                         156   Page
                                                                                                      Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
invest in an irrigation system for water source regulation and saline and freshwater separation
in the project area. Also, these works don't interfere with the LXQ irrigation system's
operational procedure, whose main task is to drain floodwaters into the West Sea. The
procedure was approved by the MARD (now the Ministry of Agriculture and Environment)
under Decision No. 3829/ QD-BNN-TCTL dated September 22, 2021. Based on the area's
existing irrigation and canal systems, 3 infrastructure development options were proposed and
evaluated, as indicated in Table 51, Figure 63, and Figure 65.

278. The results of analyzing the advantages and disadvantages of the options presented in
Table 37 show that Option 2 has a larger budget than Option 1 and is smaller than Option 3.
However, the construction system under Option 2 will be consistent with the gradual
transformation of production in the direction of converting production in small areas according
to the adaptation of the people in the area. On the other hand, the production area following the
shrimp-rice model is sandwiched between canals that are both responsible for supplying fresh
water and draining floods, so it does not affect the flood drainage of the TGLX irrigation
system, and can conveniently collect water. sweet during the aquaculture season to dilute and
reduce salinity in shrimp ponds during the dry season when salinity in the area is higher than
25g/L. Furthermore, Option 2 is the option with less impact on environmental and social issues
compared to the remaining options. Therefore, option 2 was selected.

3.2.1.2. Options of sluice structure

279. The hydraulic calculation results suggest that for the scale of the sluices, which includes
one gate with a width of 5.5-8m, the proposed structural forms for consideration are as follows:
  Opt 1: The sluice structure is composed of single-piece pillars and bottom beams that are
   rigidly connected via a pile foundation system made of reinforced concrete.
  Opt 2: The sluice structure consists of distinct pillars and bottom beams that are flexibly
   connected.
280. Based on a comparison of the pros and cons of each option, the scope of application,
natural conditions, and construction tasks of the sluices, Option 1 is selected. Details can be
found in Table 53 and Table 54.

3.2.2.    Options for the connecting road

281. The following are 2 options for the connection point from the Binh Giang 1 road to NH80:
  Opt 1 (at the end of the approach ramp of Binh Giang 1 bridge) at Km169+236 NH 80, about
   78m from the Binh Giang 1 bridge abutment towards Rach Gia city (Figure 22).
  Opt 2 (about 140 far from the approach ramp of Binh Giang 1 bridge): at Km169+133 of
   NH80, about 181m from the Binh Giang 1 bridge abutment towards Rach Gia City (Figure
   23).

CHAPTER 4. ASSESSMENT OF SOCIAL AND
           ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACTS AND RISKS OF
           THE PROJECT
4.1. POSITIVE IMPACTS

283. The project's benefits are long-term rather than short-term. If successful, the project
should not be limited to increasing the number of aquaculture farmers and service providers for
shrimp farming activities, but should also contribute to the national sustainable fishery
development, specifically:
  The  project items assist An Giang province in resolving its urgent problems, such as
   preventing saline intrusion in coastal areas and regulating water for stable cultivation, etc.
   This contributes to adapting to climate change, protecting and improving people's lives, and
   creating a sustainable development environment for the locality.
  The 23 sluices of the project, together with existing sluices, will improve the ability to
   control salinity, and tides, adapt to climate change, and sea level rise, and separate salty and
   freshwater boundaries. The project will also ensure flood drainage, acid sulfate washing for
   the LXQ, provide salt/fresh water, delineate the freshwater-saline water interface in the
   restructuring area, and regulate water sources for agriculture, fisheries, daily life, and rural
   environmental sanitation during the dry season. Furthermore, the project promotes rural
   development and socioeconomic integration by regulating water use demand for shrimp
   farming and contributing to disease control for an area of 5,500 ha of aquaculture, effectively
   changing the structure of agricultural land to the south of NH 80.
  The project roads that connect NH 80 to the West Sea dike will make it easier for farmers to
   transport breeds, food, equipment, shrimp, and other seafood outputs. The production costs
   for the residents will decrease as they no longer need to rely on manual or semi-automatic
   vehicles. This will contribute to the enhancement of seafood quality by reducing
   transportation time.



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 165   Page
                                                                                              Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
  The project's medium      and low-voltage power system will contribute to addressing the
   longstanding issue of electricity shortages in the aquaculture area. Shrimp farmers will use
   electric-powered machines and equipment for shrimp farming, which is both efficient and
   economical, while also reducing environmental pollution from diesel use (a common
   practice when facing power shortages). The aquaculture production costs will decrease by
   not using diesel fuel, leading to increased income and improved livelihoods for the farmers.
  When the project is finished, the shrimp farming area is expected to shift to an industrial and
   semi-industrial farming model with better extended agricultural procedures. This change is
   projected to boost shrimp output, improving the livelihoods of the local community.
  In addition to the direct impacts on shrimp farming development, the project is expected to
   attract investments from private enterprises for upgrading and constructing new
   infrastructure for industrial shrimp farming facilities.
  Farmers   will be able to access aquaculture facilities with enhanced sanitary and
   environmental conditions, which reduces occupational health risks, improves the quality and
   value of shrimp production, and reduces shrimp losses due to shorter transportation.
  Improved waste management in shrimp farms will allow for less wastewater and sediment
   discharge into water bodies. As a result, in the long run, the project should help to improve
   the water quality in the area.
  By  implementing Integrated Pest Management (IPM) and Good Agriculture Practices
   (GAP), as well as introducing bio-security practices and disease-free seeds, ponds can be
   rendered less susceptible to pests and diseases. This would result in a decreased reliance on
   antibiotics and chemicals in aquaculture, safeguarding the environment, mitigating losses
   caused by disease outbreaks, improving food security, and enhancing occupational health
   and safety (OHS) for farming-based laborers.
  Improved quality of shrimp seed and increased productivity of shrimp farming areas will
   help farmers expand their shrimp farming by adapting to the challenges of rising sea levels.
  Enhanced productivity of shrimp farming areas and improved quality of shrimp seed will
   enable farmers to expand their shrimp farming operations in response to challenges of sea
   level rising (SLR).
  Bio-security, good nutrition, and a reasonable cost are the primary elements for exporting
   shrimps internationally with good international competition.
  The project would also generate environmental and social benefits through a variety of other
   mechanisms. These include (i) beneficiaries, local communities, and authorities becoming
   more aware of and concerned about environmental and social issues; and (ii) more PPMU
   staff and aquaculture extension staff of the Department of Agriculture and Environment
   being trained in environmental and social screening. This helps with bringing up
   environmental and social problems during the planning and execution of the project.

of activities undertaken and the duration of these activities. Construction camps are semi-
permanent and must be removed after construction phase. Types and scales of the project's

4.3. NEGATIVE IMPACTS AND RISKS DURING PRE-CONSTRUCTION
     PHASE

4.3.1.    Impacts by Land Acquisition

286. The area of land permanently used by the project is approximately 15.46 hectares.
However, the land managed by the 2CPCs accounts for up to 12.6 hectares (water surface,
current roads, canal banks, etc.), accounting for 82%, while the land managed and used by the
local people is only 2.77 hectares (0.33ha of argicultural land, and 0.65ha of land for planting
trees), making up 17.9% (Table 57). There is no forest land to be acquired for the project.
287. The project will impacts on 22 houses with a total area of 1,363.48m 2 (mostly temporary,
with one category 4 house), various auxiliary structures (such as fences, yards, mooring areas,
and drilled wells), and approximately 93,097 trees of various types, including fruit and
landscape trees. Details are in Table 58, Table 59 and Table 83.
Table 57: Permanent land acquisition of the project
   No.                                Descriptions                            Area (m2)
