288. The project will have an impact on 22 houses with a total area of 1,363.48m 2. Most houses
are temporary houses (21) and only one house is catagory 4 houses (see Table 58)
289. Detailed information related to permanent and temporary land acquisition, including
areas, types of land, houses, structures, trees, etc., are presented in the Project’s RP.
Furthermore, the final PAH figures will be determined by the Detailed Measurement Survey
(DMS) during the RP's implementation.
290. The following are the impacts of land acquisition:

4.3.1.1. Impact on relocated households

291. Within the land acquisition area for the project, 12 households need to be relocated,
including 8HHs for the new sluices and 4HHs for the road. The DHs will experience
psychological repercussions as well as changes in community interactions. The displaced

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                              170     Page
                                                                                             Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
households require time to adapt to their new surroundings, including access to new services
such as health care, markets, and schools. When individuals move to a new neighborhood, they
will gradually form new social connections. However, the 8HHs in the construction area of the
new sluices had constructed the affected houses on the canal banks or the state land. These
houses are temporary. Furthermore, there are no infrastructures or residents in the vicinity. It is
challenging for the HHs to access the health care system, schools, and markets. Additionally,
the houses displaced by the road construction are located in the Binh Giang 1 residential area,
designated as the project's resettlement area. Consequently, if these DHs choose the
resettlement option in the project's designated resettlement site, the impacts of relocation will
be negligible.
292. The findings of field surveys and community consultations in the project area reveal that
households desire the project to be implemented. They believe that once the project is
completed, they will benefit from its infrastructure, particularly the project road. Furthermore,
interviews with AHs by land acquisition for the project show that they are completely
supportive of the project's implementation and are willing to hand over their land for the project
when they are relocated to a new place. Because they know that the road will provide enormous
benefits.
293. According to the project’s RP, environmental and social due diligence has been
conducted for the Binh Giang 1 residential line served for the DHs of the project. The result of
the due diligence shows that the line which was built in 2002 by the province budget. It is
currently located along Binh Giang 1 road, which will be upgraded as part of the project. It
consists of 395 land lots, of which 43 lots have been allocated to individuals and organization
for settlement, leaving 352 vacant lots that can be allocated to displaced households of the
project. It has access complete infrastructure including electricity, water, and drainage, as well
as health stations, markets, and schools within a short distance of 1-2 km. It means that their
living conditions will improve significantly, particularly for the DHs of the new sluices, because
the majority of their affected houses are temporary structures built on canal banks and other
state-managed lands, with poor living conditions and limited access to health care, markets, and
schools.
294. As a result, the impact is rated as moderate, short-term, and can be mitigated due to the
following: (i) the small number of DHs (12 HHs) and their generally poor living conditions,
particularly for the DHs of the sluices; (ii) the relocated houses are temporary and constructed
on state land or the bank of canals; (iii) the DHs will receive a variety of support, including
transportation, rental, and self-relocation assistance; (iv) they will be arranged for relocation
at the existing resettlement site with adequate infrastructure (if they desire); and (v) the
resettlement plan (RP) has been prepared for the project.

4.3.1.2. Impact on livelihood

295. There are 57 project affected household (PAH)s with 229 people by land acquisition of
the project. They are affected types of loss such as agricultural land, structures, trees and crops.
Only 1 vulnerable household (those with a physical or mental disability), 7 ethnic minority HHs
and 12 displaced household (DP)s area found among the 57 AHs.
296. The loss of agricultural land has a direct impact on the income and indirectly reduces the
living conditions of the affected households. However, the argicultural land will be acquired
very small, including 0.33ha of agricultural land for the 23 sluices and 0.65ha of land for


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                  171   Page
                                                                                               Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
planting trees the road for the road. Also, the project has no severe impact on any household,
meaning that no households will lose 20% or more and vulnerable losing 10% or more of their
agricultural production land.
297. Futhermore, because the area where the 23 sluices and the road are to be built is rural
areas, no business is available, no business activities will be affected by land acquisition.
298. As a result, the impact on livelihood is determined to be “small”, short-term and can be
mitigated by cash compensation and livelihood restoration program outlined in the project’s
RP.

4.3.1.3. Changes in land use

299. The project’s footprint of the 23 sluices and the road will change the local land use
structure, affecting income sources and livelihoods of the affected households. However, the
impact of the project is assessed to be “LOW” due to the following reasons: (i) the amount of
agricultural and residential land acquired for the project is low; (ii) the completion of the road
will increase the land value; (iii) improved accessibility will facilitate local economic growth;
and (iv) the project’s land use are planned. The project's positve impacts outweigh the negative
ones and futhermore, these negative impacts can be mitigated through the RP of the project.

4.3.1.4. Impacts on public assets and cultural heritages

300. At the time of the preparation of resettlement plan (RP), no cultural-historical assets have
been identified in the project area where land may be required. However, some public
infrastructure, such as electric poles, telecommunications, and television cable lines may be
potentially affected by the project. Once the detailed engineering design has been approved, a
Detailed Measurement Survey (DMS) will be carried out to determine the potential impacts on
the items in further detail. However, the project owner shall notify the affected households of
the relocation time 2 weeks in advance and shall request contractors set up new infrastructures
before moving the currents ones so the impacts will be small and short-term and mitigable.
301. Comments for impacts due to land acquisition: the impact of land acquisition is assesed
as small and can be mitigated due to the following reasons: (i) The area of land to be acquired
from the people is 2.77 hectares and scattered across two communes, so the number of affected
households is small (57 households, including 12 displaced households), and the area affected
per household is small; (ii) The affected land is mainly adjacent to canal banks, with only 0.33
hectares of rice cultivation land affected; (iii) For the 12 displaced households, the affected
houses are temporary structures built on canals or on land managed by the state, with relatively
low living conditions. These households will be resettled at the Binh Giang 1 residential area,
where the infrastructure is good, and access to healthcare, education, and markets is easy,
within a radius of 1-2 kilometers. Consultation results with the displaced households show that
all of them agreed to resettle in this area; and (iv) all affected households support the
implementation of the project and will benefit from the project's components, especially the
road. Additionally, the project's RP has been developed to ensure that the affected households
will be compensated and supported to restore their livelihoods to at least the same or better
level than before the project.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                172   Page
                                                                                             Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
4.3.2.   Safety Risk Related to Unexploded Ordnances (UXOs)

302. Vietnam in general and the project area in particular experienced civil wars that
concluded in 1975. Despite the land having been utilized for the past four decades following
the conclusion of the war, there is still a possibility that certain unexploded ordnances (UXOs)
remain underground. The UXOs could potentially cause harm to workers, and damage to
project and nearby households’ structures if it is disturbed by construction activities such as site
clearance, ground leveling, and excavation. Most of project works are required to implement
the clearance of UXOs by licensed units, except for the 18 upgraded sluices which already was
cleared in the previous projects. The new sluices will have more risks of UXO than the rest
given that the excavation activities may be executed to the depth of 5m on agricultural fields
and canal beds.
303. If UXOs are not cleared, they might pose a risk during the construction process, affecting
workers, locals, and adjacent infrastructure. According to a consultation with the military chief
of An Giang province, there have been no cases of UXOs discovered or causalities due to UXOs
in the entire commmunes in the last ten years. Therefore the risk is moderate and long-term if
it happens in the construction areas of the project works, with the exception of improving the
18 sluices. However, it can be mitigated because clearing work will be completed thoroughly
before construction begins, and construction activities should be limited to UXO-free zones.

4.4. NEGATIVE IMPACTS AND RISKS DURING CONSTRUCTION PHASE
304. Based on the project scope, scale, and construction methods mentioned in Chapter 1 and
current natural and social circumstances of the project area mentioned in Chapter 2, the negative
impacts during project construction are evaluated and separated into generic and specific
impacts in the following sections:

4.4.1.   Generic Impacts

4.4.1.1. Impacts on ambient air quality

305. During the construction of the project, the sources of air pollution include:
  Dust and air emission from demolitions.

  Dust and air emissions generated during transportation of construction materials.

  Dust and air emission from excavation.

  Dust from concrete mixing.

  Noise and vibration.


a) Dust and air emmision generation from demolition

306. Dust and emmision generation to air can occur during the site clearance (e.g. the
demolition of houses, other structures on land, trees, etc.), which will be conducted in scattered
areas of two communes over a period of one to three months, contingent upon the area and
quantity of site clearance. Only 2 structural components require the demolition, including the
construction of the 23 sluices and the road.

adjacent to the project area.

affected, while the remaining houses will be affected by their auxilaries, such as fences, yards,
mooring areas, and drilled wells (Table 58 and Table 59). In addition, about 93,097 trees of all
kinds will be demolited by the project, including fruit trees and landscape trees. Demolition
work mainly targets walls, auxiliary structures, and solidified constructions.

310. The WHO's 2003 rapid assessment guideline states that dust emissions during land
clearance and demolition range from 0.1 to 1 g per m³. The project entails a calculated
demolition volume of 77.3m³ (see Table 61) with an estimated duration of 30 days and a daily
work schedule of 8 hours. The dust generated from demolition activities will be very small,
ranging from 0.01-0.11 g/h (Table 61). In addition, cutting trees will generate no dust. However,
the demolition will be carried out in 2 project communes throughout a month. It will not be
done all at once, but rather in intervals scattered across the three years of the project's
construction for various components. Dust mainly affects the workers who are presence at the
site during demolition and local people around the sites. However, the impact is small, short-
term, and localized within the construction sites of the traffic routes due to the following
reasons: (i) Only temporary structures need to be demolished, and each structure requires 1-2
workers and 1-2 types of demolition equipment for 1-2 days; (ii) the demolition is carried out
in sparsely populated places; (iii) the demolition takes place throughout 2 communes. In
addition, the impact can be minimized by applying appropriate mitigation measures outlined in
Chapter 5.
Table 61: Dust emissions generated during the demolition of structures on the project sites
        Activity               Demolition            Dust emission       Total generated          Dust load
                               volume (m3)           factor (g/m3)          dust (g)                (g/h)
 Demolition         of             77.3                 0.1 - 1             7.72-77.2              0.01-0.11
 structures on land
311. The affected crops are mainly fruit trees and perennials. Therefore, chainsaws powered
by gasoline engines will be used. According to the project's RP, a total of 93,097 trees of all
kinds will be cut down. About 5 workers will be needed to run 5 chainsaws, working over a
period of 30 days. The estimated fuel consumption rate is 60 trees per liter of gasoline. The
amount of fuel required for tree cutting activities is presented in Table 62.

313. From the calculated results, the concentration of air emission caused by the tree-cutting
is within the allowable limits according to QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT. In addition, the demolition
will occur in sparsely populated areas. Therefore, the impact is negligible, short-term (only and
localized within the land acquisition area.
314. Comments: The impacts of dust and air emissions resulting from demolition are assessed
as minor, as the quantity of structures and trees to be demolished is limited. This activity
disseminated across the sparse regions of the two communes during a brief period of
approximately 30 days. The project owner shall request the contractors to implement the
mitigating measures outlined in Chapter 5.

b) Dust and air emissions from construction material transportation

315. As stated above, boats will be used to transfer the construction materials for the project.
According to Table 64, the total volume of building materials needed to build a sluice is about
215,625 tons. To transport all of the materials, 1,078 trips are necessary (estimated for a boat
with a load capacity of 200 tons). The building process is estimated to take 6-24 months,
resulting in a daily need for 2.36 trips, with the highest demand for road construction (1.47
trips/day) and the lowest for power lines (0.04 trips/day), see Table 64.

c) Dust and air emissions from excavation

319. During construction, excavation activities will be carried out to remove organic matter
from the topsoil layer and dig soil for the foundation pits of the sluices and holes for the power
lines. This will involve the use of construction equipment and tools such as excavators, drilling
machines, and hammers, etc. These operations have the potential to generate dust and air

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                           177   Page
                                                                                                        Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
pollutants, which can have a negative impact on the health of the community and workers, as
well as the air quality in and around the construction sites.
320. According to the project’s FS report, the total soil excavation and filling volume of the
project is 309,726 m3, mainly from the sluices and the connecting road (Table 68).
Table 68: Volume of soil excavation and filling of the project
 No. Project items                            Total volume of soil excavation and filling (m3)
  1     23 new sluices                                                  282.762

  4     Power lines                                                     1.266,37
        Total                                                       309,726.47

321. According to the Environmental Assessment Sourcebook of the World Bank (Volume II,
Sectoral Guidelines, Environment, World Bank, Washington D.C., 8/1991), the level of dust
dispersion from soil excavation activities is determined based on by the emission coefficient
(E) as follows:
                                E = k x 0,0016 x (U/2,2)1,4/(M/2)1,3, kg/ton.
Among them:
E: Pollution factor (kg/ton)
k: Average grain structure value (0.35)
U: Average wind speed in construction area (3 m/sec)
M: Average soil moisture (20%).
                                          .    .        .
          Thus 𝐸 = 0.35𝑥 0.0016𝑥                   /( ) . = 𝟎. 𝟎𝟏𝟖𝟖 kg of dust/ton of soil
                                          .

322. The volume of dust generated from soil excavation and filling is calculated by the
following formula:
                                              W=ExQxd
In which:
      W: Amount of dust (kg);
      E: Pollution factor (kg of dust/ton);
      Q: Volume of soil excavation and filling (m3);
      d: soil density (d = 1.5 ton/m3).

   impact volume.
323. The construction of 23 sluices: The total volume of soil excavation and filling for 23
sluices is 282,762 m3 and the time for soil excavation and filling activities is estimated to be
about 18 months. As such, the total and daily dust loads are shown in Table 69. However,
excavation and filling activities take place within the canals, where the moisture content is very
high (about 30-40%). As a result, the real amount of dust generated is around half of the
projected value. Furthermore, the sluices will be positioned at 23 various locations within both
Binh Giang and Binh Son communes, which are rural areas with very few residents. Only, 3
sluices have the closest house within 10m (see Figure 68 and Figure 69): T5-1 (2 house), T6-
1 (20 houses), and T6-6 (2 houses); 3 sluices have the nearest house within 20m: K9-6 (1house),
K3-7 (2 houses), and T6-4 (1 house); and 3 sluices have the nearest house within 30m: K9-1 (1
house), K3-5 (1house), and T6-3 (1house). Furthermore, the air quality in this region remains
good. Affected objects are residents and water, air, and soil within a radius of 50m from the
construction sites and the scale of impact is small, short term, and ended when the sluice
construction is completed. In addition, the project owner will request the contractors to
implement mitigation measures for the impacts as described in Chapter 4.

Figure 69: The residential area close to the construction area of T6-1 sluice
324. The road construction: The amount of excavation required for the road is 25,698.5m 3,
and the excavation duration is 3 months. The amount of dust generated per day is 10.1 kg/day
(see Table 69). 74 households are living along the road. The majority of the road is thinly
populated, except for the segment from NH80 to Canal 200, which has approximately 20 homes
(Figure 70). As a result, the dust from the road building will affect the health and daily lives of
people who live in that area (74 households in the Binh Giang 1 residential area) and work on
the site (about 30 workers). The amount of dust generated is minimal, the air quality in the area
remains good, and the road will be built in a successive construction method, with each segment
measuring 500 meters in length. In addition, the project owner will require that the contractor
implement mitigation measures, particularly in the area between NH80 and Canal 200. As a
result, this impact is considered minor, localised, occur in the excavation time of 3 months and
will cease upon completion of excavation work and can be mitigated by measures in Chapter
5.
325. The installation of power lines: The soil excavation and filling required for the power
lines is on a relatively minor scale, amounting to 1,266.37m3 for bout 550 poles. This mainly
serves the placement of electricity poles and digging each pole take 1-2 days with amount of

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 180   Page
                                                                                              Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
soil excavation of 2-3m3. The dust emissions resulting from the electrical lines are minimal,
totaling approximately 0.001 kg per day for each pole. Affected objects of the impact are
construction workers and the impact is neglible, localised and can be mitigated because (i) the
lines are installed along canals, which are sparsely populated; (ii) happen near the electric
pole installation sites, and last about 1-2 days.

area with low population; (ii) the excavation will be short (about couple of days to 6 months)
and spread over 2 communes of Binh Giang and Binh Son; (iii) all impacts will be mitigated
by measures in Chapter 5.

d) Dust from concrete mixing

328. New sluice construction and sluice improvement: The total concrete volume for newly
built sluices and upgraded sluices is 98,027m 3. According to the rapid assessment guideline of
WHO, the dust emission from concrete mixing ranges from 0.5 to 5g/m 3. Therefore, the total
amount of dust generated is approximately 4,9 to 49kg. However, 500L mixers will be utilized
for concrete mixing, which takes place on the construction site, hence, dust will only disperse
within a radius of 5-10m from the mixing station. Affected objects are the plant operators and
households within 10m from the construction (1 house in sluice T5-1, 20 houses in sluice T6-
1, and 2 houses in sluice T6-6) because they are exposed to dust regularly. The impact is small,
short-term, and mitigatable due to the following: (i) the number of constructors working on a
station is approximately 1-2 peoples, and the construction sites are located in sparsely populated
areas; (ii) the stations are situated at the end of the wind direction; (iii) workers will be equiped
with personal protective equipment (PPE); and (iv) contractors are required to implement
mitigation measures to minize the impact, particularly when constructing the T6-1 sluice.
329. The road construction: The total volume of concrete used for the road construction is
quite high, about 34,522m3. According to the rapid assessment guideline of WHO in , the dust
emission from concrete mixing ranges from 0.5 to 5g/m3. Therefore, the total amount of dust
generated is approximately 1.72 to 17.2 kg. However, the impact of this action is concentrated
and immediate at the concrete mixing site. Affected objects of the impact are concrete mixing
workers and residents living within 10m from the mixing plants. The impact on residents is
small, localised, mitigable because (i) it lasts only on 1-2 construction days before moving to a
new location; (ii) localised at the mixing plant of a road section, especially from NH80 to Canal
200; and (iii) can be mitigated by measures in Chapter 5. The impact on concrete mixing
workers is medium, short-term and mitigatble, because (i) only 1-2 workers at the mixing plant,
(ii) mixing concrete happens about 18 months of construction; and (iii) workers are provided
with PPE and trained Occupational health and safety (OHS) before commencing.
330. The installation of power lines: The concrete volume used for the electrical system is
approximately 2,070m3. According to the rapid assessment guideline of WHO, the dust
emission from concrete mixing ranges from 0.5 to 5g/m3. Therefore, the amount of dust

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                     182   Page
                                                                                                  Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
generated from concrete mixing ranges from 1.03-10.3kg and only appears at the concrete
column erection site for 1 house before moving to a new pole. Affected objects are only workers
because the poles are located along canal banks. The impact is small, long-term, and mitigatable
due to the following: (i) the number of constructors working on a station is approximately 1-2
peoples, and the construction sites are located in sparsely populated areas; (ii) the stations are
situated at the end of the wind direction; and (iii) workers will be equiped with PPE.
331. Comments: The impact of concrete mixing is small, short-term, and mitigatable due to
the following: (i) the number of constructors working on a station is approximately 1-2 peoples,
and the construction sites are located in sparsely populated areas; (ii) the stations are situated
at the end of the wind direction; (iii) workers will be equiped with personal protective
equipment (PPE); and (iv) contractors are required to implement mitigation measures to minize
the impact in Chapter 5.

e) Noise and vibration

e.1). Noise
332. Noise urging construction is usually generated by (i) transportation vehicles; and (ii)
construction equipment and tools. However, the noise level gradually decreases with distance
and can be determined by the following formula:
                                      ΔL = 20lg (r2/r1)1 + a (dB)
Where:
      ∆L: reduction of noise at distance r2 compared with source r1;
      distance for noise source normally considered as r 1= 8 m;
      a: noise absorption of area
333. Transportation of construction materials: As mentioned above, boats of 200 tons will
be used to deliver the construction materials. Noise from the boat is 80 to 100 dBA. The noise
attenuation from the boat is 60.5 dBA at distance of 50m from the boat and 52.5dBA at the
distance of 20m from the boat. The primary axis canals for transportation include the Rach Gia-
Ha Tien (RG-HT) canal, the Rach Soi-Long Xuyen canal, and flood drainage canals. So, the
residents along the canals are prone to the impact from noise. The noise from transport boats is
relatively significant, but their impact has a directional pattern (in the direction of movement),
and the length of the impact at a point on the transport route is very short, only around 1-2
minutes. Also, there are few people living along the transportation routes, except some
households living near the transportation route to the construction site of sluice T6-1.
334. Construction of the 23 sluices: As mentioned in Table 16, at least 13 construction
equipment an tools will be used for the 23 sluices. The maximum noise level at different
distances due to their operation is presented in Table 71. Noise levels at 20m from noise sources
are lower than Vietnamese requirements during daytime but higher than that during night-time,
while the IFC 2007 guideline value will only be achieved at 50m.

at the same time will be limited, the calculated noise levels during the construction phase are
shown in Figure 71 and Figure 72. The largest noise source is pile driving. Noise levels at a
distance of 100 m from this source are lower than the national standard.

IFC Guideline (2007) applying to the daytime level in residential, institutional, and educational facilities is
55dBA
Ministry of Health (MOH) standard: Noise at the production area is 85 dBA for 8 hours of noise exposure

  338. Noise from the construction of the road is expected to exceed existing noise levels and
  exceed the maximum noise levels allowed in particular areas such as residential areas which
  are found along the alignment as well as sensitive receptors that are located less than 100 m
  from the row of way of the road, including the residential cluster from NH 80 to Canal 200, the
  users of the Cultural house of Ranh Hat hamlet and Siviranba Chanpa Pagoda. However, the
  impacts of noise are considered low negative impacts because (i) residents only live at the
  section from NH80 to Canal 200 and the amount of construction workers is low (about 10
  workers a site); (ii) the impacts happen in very short time, intermittent and localised; (iii)
  contractors shall be strict compliance with mitigation measures during the construction phase
  in Chapter 5.
  339. Improvement of the 18 sluices: the number of equipment and tools for the improvement
  of the sluices is small, including bulldozers, boats, movable cranes, power generators and pile
  drivers. However, there are no houses and sensitive receptors within 100m from the sluices and
  the construction time is very short (6 months). So, the impact is negligible and short-term and
  can be mitigated.
  340. Installation of the power lines: like the sluice upgradation, the number of equipment
  and tools for the installation of power lines is small, such as bulldozers, diggers, concrete
  mixers, concrete compactors and hammers and the equipment and tools will mainly be used in
  the pole erection. Therefore, the impacts is negligible because (i) the construction time is very
  short (about 1-2 days for a pole); (ii) the number of tools and equipment is very limited; and
  (iii) the construction areas are along canals with small number of people living nearby.
  341. Comments: Impacts from noise is small due to (i) it happens only in the construction time
  (about 1-2 days to 6-18 months depending on the project item); (ii) the number of construction
  workers and residents nearby the site are small; (iii) PPE will be provided to workers; and (iv)
  contractors shall be strict compliance with mitigation measures during the construction phase
  in Chapter 5.
  e.2). Vibration
  342. Most vibrations on construction sites come from heavy machinery like bulldozers,
  excavators, rollers, etc., used for tasks like leveling, compacting, etc. Building damage and
  disruption potential are usually the two main considerations when evaluating ground-borne
  vibration.
  343. According to the Transit Noise and Vibration Impact Assessment study (US, Federal
  Transit Administration, 2006), which details the vibration levels of vehicles, machinery, and
  equipment, the following formula is used to predict the amount of vibration decreasing with
  distance:
                                    L = L0– 10lg (r/r0) – 8.7a (r – r0) (dB)
  Where:


  10
       QCVN 26:2010/BTNMT – National technical regulation on noise.

  Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                        186   Page
                                                                                                       Confidential
                       ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
     L: vibration in dB at a distance “r” meters away from the source;
     Lo: vibration in dB measured at a distance “r0” meters from the source. Vibration at a
      distance of r0=10 m is generally acknowledged as source vibration.
     a: the intrinsic vibration reduction coefficient on clay ground of about 0.5.

345. Construction of the road: The main noise-generating equipment due to the road
construction, includes excavators, pull dozers, air compressors, and rollers. Based on Table 1,
at a distance of 12 meters from the vibration source, the vibration level meets the permissible
standard. Within a 12-meter radius of the vibration source, about 74 houses are affected, mostly
concentrated in the section from NH80 to Canal 200. Survey results show that those houses are
temporary structures with corrugated iron roofs, so they may be significantly affected by
vibrations. However, the vibration impact is considered moderate for the following reasons: (i)
the road construction will use the rolling method, which takes 7 days for each 100-meter
segment; (ii) the machinery and equipment will operate intermittently and for short periods of
time; (iii) the road constructors will implement the vibration mitigation measures outlined in
Chapter 5; (iv) the quality of houses will be assessed and recorded in their current condition
before construction begins, and any damages will be compensated.
346. Construction of the 23 sluices: The pile drivers are the source of the most vibration in
the sluice construction. The safe distance for withstanding significant vibrations from the pile
drivers is around 16 meters. Nevertheless, some local residences and structures are situated
within 16 meters of the sluice building sites, including T5-1 (two houses and Kenh T5 bridge),
T6-6 (two houses), K9-6 (one house), K3-7 (two houses), and T6-4 (one house). Some of them
are temporary structures with corrugated iron roofs (Figure 73), so they will be significantly
affected by vibrations. This is a concern that the project owner needs to address to ensure the
safety of the residents' houses. The impact is moderate but intermittent, short-term, and can be
mitigated by implementing effective engineering solutions and construction management

                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
practices, as well as in close consultation with the local community, inspection, and supervision
and the quality of houses and structures will be assessed and recorded in their current condition
before construction begins, and any damages will be compensated.




Figure 73: Typical houses near the project sluices
347. Upgradation of the 18 sluices: The new gates will be craned into their existing positions.
Furthermore, there are no houses, constructions, or people living with 100m these sluices.
Therefore, there is a negligible impact due to the upgrade.
348. Power line installation: mainly light equipment and small concrete mixers are used to
install the poles. It takes about only 2-3 days for installing a pole. The installation takes place
along the canal banks. As a result, the vibration from the pole installation is negligible.
349. Comments: There will be minimal to medium vibration impacts due to the following
reasons: (i) the construction time is short; (ii) vibration only occurs during construction and
within 20m of the source; (iii) the houses and structures that could be affected are only there
for a short period of time; and (iv) these impacts can be reduced through the use of effective
engineering solutions and construction management practices, in close consultation with the
local community, inspection, and supervision. Before construction starts, the houses and
structures will be evaluated and recorded in their current condition, and any damages will be
compensated for.

4.4.1.2. Impacts on water quality

a) Impacts of wastewater generation

350. The project construction generates wastewater from multiple sources, including (i)
workers’ wastewater; (ii) rainwater runoff; (iii) construction wastewater from boats used for
transporting construction materials and from cleaning machinery.


a.1). Workers’ wastewater
351. According to TCXDVN 33:2006, a worker uses 60 lits of water each day, and 80% of
that amount is discharged into the environment. As mentioned in Table 18, during the peak
period, there are a total of 210 individuals present at the sites. However, only 10 people visit
the sites briefly for supervisory purposes, while the remaining 200 workers are divided into 24
groups and actively working at the sites. Therefore, wastewater generation only account for 200
workers.
352. At a site, the maximum number of workers is 10 (for sluice construction, and road
construction) and 5 (for power lines, and sluice gate upgrades). As a result, the total amount of
wastewater discharged into the environment from a site is 0.24-0.69m 3/day, while the total
amount of wastewater discharged by all sites is 9.36m3/day (Table 74). The per capita load of
pollutants in domestic wastewater according to the World Health Organization (WHO) is
presented in Table 75.

354. Domestic wastewater consists of suspended particles, oil and grease, organic matter
(BOD5 and COD), nutrients (nitrogen, phosphorus), and microorganisms. The average
concentration of pollutants in domestic wastewater before passing through septic tanks or
similar structures is significantly higher than the standards allowed for discharge into the
environment (QCVN 14:2008/BTNMT column B), see details in Table 77. The high
concentrations of nitrogen and phosphorus in wastewater can lead to eutrophication, promoting
the growth of certain algae in water. This, in turn, reduces the concentration of dissolved oxygen
in water, affecting water quality, soil quality, and aquatic organisms in water of canals No.200,
No.2, Don Dong, Binh Giang 1, Tuan Thong, etc. and soil near the construction sites in hamlets
of Giong Ke, Ranh Hat, Ranh Bon (Binh Giang commune) and Vam Ray and Thuan An (Binh
Son commune), especially at the discharge point of the wastewater. Furthermore, wastewater
has a high concentration of germs and microorganisms that can induce gastrointestinal disorders
like diarrhea, dysentery, food poisoning, and jaundice in construction workers.
355. If not properly treated, this issue can be a major cause of pollution, directly impacting the
living environment of workers and nearby residents. It can also contribute to disease outbreaks
and have a direct impact on the quality of groundwater and surface water.
Table 77: Concentration of pollutants in domestic wastewater
                                                          Untreated             QCVN 14:2008/BTNMT

                                                        concentration             column B (k=1.2)
     1    BOD5                           mg/L                563 - 675                      60
     2    COD                            mg/L                900 - 1,275                     -
     3    Total suspended solids         mg/L                875 - 1,813                    120
     4    Oil and grease                 mg/L                125 - 375                      24
     5    Total N                        mg/L                 75 - 150                       -
     6    Ammonia                        mg/L                  30 - 60                      12
     7    Total                          mg/L                  10 - 50                      11
   8    Coliform                     MPN/100mL         106,000 – 109,000                   6,000
Source: Hoang Hue, 2002
356. 23 sluices construction: The number of workers on each camp is small, with a maximum
of 10-15people (10 people for sluices and 5 people for nearby power lines or sluice upgrades),


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                             190   Page
                                                                                                          Confidential
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
equivalent to 4-6 households. Surface water areas prone to this risk are: Don Dong canal, canal
200, K3-Tuan Thong canal, the canal along the sea dike. However, the direct impact due to
domestic wastewater is considered to be LOW because: (i) the volume of domestic wastewater
generated at each camp is small; (ii) the project will prioritize the use of local non-skilled
workers, as well as arrange for 3-compartment septic tanks at camps; (iii) the impact is
localized, only in camp area and cease after construction; construction time is short (24
months); and (iv) the impact can be minimized by applying appropriate management and
technical measures.
357. Road construction: Many rice fields and fruit orchards line the road’s construction area
(Figure 54 and Figure 55). Although there are only approximately 10 workers at each
construction site for the road, discharging domestic wastewater directly into fruit orchards and
rice fields would limit their productivity or death. The project owner should pay a lot of
attention to this concern during the road construction. However, the impact of workers’
wastewater is small, localized, short-term, and ends after construction because (i) local non-
skilled labor will be hired for the project; (ii) 3-compartment septic tanks at will be arranged at
camps; (iii) the rolling method with a section of 100m will be used for the road; and (iv) the
impact can be minimized by applying appropriate management and technical measures.
358. Comments: Workers’ wastewater has a small, localized, short-term impact that ends after
construction. This is due to several factors, including: (i) a small workforce (210 people at peak
and 5-10 people at a site) and the use of local, unskilled labor (up to 50% of total workers); (ii)
3-compartment septic tanks will be installed at camps, and only houses with septic tanks in the
area will be utilized for the project; (iii) the road and power lines will be constructed using the
rolling method; and (iv) the impact can be minimized by implementing the appropriate
management and technical measures outlined in Chapter 5.
a.2). Rainwater runoff
359. The nature of rainwater is considered clean. According to WHO, the concentrations of
pollutants in rainwater are N: 0.5–1.5 mg/L; P: 0.004–0.03 mg/L; COD: 10–20 mg/L; and TSS:
10–20 mg/L. Rainwater passing through the construction sites could introduce pollutants to the
surface water environment. Runoff of materials, sand, and rock into water is expected to be the
main source of pollution and would increase water turbidity, and inundation, affecting water
supply for domestic use, houses, and gardens of residents. Additionally, acid-sulfate soil areas
can pose a risk of acid-sulfate contamination during excavation. When it rains, acid sulfate
significantly affects the water environment.
360. According to the analysis results of soil samples taken in the project area, the soil is
slightly to severely contaminated with acid sulfate (see Appendix 3). Since there is a lot of
digging going on at the sluice building site, there is a chance that the soil will change from
potential acid sulfate soil to active acid sulfate soil, which could affect the water sources in the
canals at the start of the rainy season.
361. The amount of runoff from a construction site is calculated via the following formula:
                                          W0 = S x M/1000
Where:     - W0: runoff water volume (m3)
              S: surface area (m2). Most of these are material warehouses and construction yards
               and only about 20% are vacant land.



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                  191   Page
                                                                                               Confidential
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
              M: Maximum rainfall amount (mm), for the sluices construction area M=50mm
362. Constructing 23 sluices: The surface area of the construction site of the sluices ranges
from approximately 460-3,327m2, with an average of about 2,000m2. Accordingly, with the
highest daily rainfall of 50mm/day, the volume of rainwater overflowing at the fluctuating
sluices is between 23-202 m3/day and averaging at 100 m3/day/sluice (Table 78). Rainwater
runoff will carry along dust, dirt, and grease on the ground of the construction site, flowing into
the surrounding canals, increasing the turbidity of surface water sources in the area. Due to the
high permeability of the soil in the early rains, only 40–60% of the water flows into the canals;
the remainder is absorbed by the soil, resulting in a volume of water flowing into the canals of
approximately 40-60 m3 per rain event.

363. In reality, the impact of rainwater runoff on the receiving water at the construction site is
often negligible. Experience with the construction of similar sluices in the area has shown that
with good construction site management, including preventing the spread of waste oil on the

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 192   Page
                                                                                              Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
construction surface and forming sediment traps and settling ponds, the impact of turbidity
caused by rainwater runoff is limited to a range of about 100-200 meters. However, the
contractors shall implement mitigation measures for acid sulfate exposure.
364. Road construction: The road width is 9m and each segment is 100m long. Therefore,
the amount of rainwater overflowing for each 100m section is W0 = 9 x 100 x 50/1,000 = 45m3.
If there is sand and oil on the construction site surface, rainwater will wash these materials into
fields, gardens, and houses, affecting (i) people's access to their houses and fields, causing
inconvenience, particularly for those living at the start of NHH80; (ii) plant productivity or
death; (iii) water quality of the receiving body. However, the current road is a poor-quality dirt
road that is frequently muddy during the rainy season (Figure 74), making it difficult for people
to travel, and the population in this area is small. As a result, this impact is rated as low,
localized, and short-term, and can be mitigated through the mitigation measures in Chapter 5.
365. Installation of power lines: The construction area of the power line is only for placing
poles, accounting for approximately 5,000 m 2. The amount of rainwater overflowing at one
point of power line construction is 1.2m3 (Figure 75). The power line routes are built along
canals, so rainwater can bring sand, and garbage infiltrating into these canals, affecting their
water quality and aquatic ecosystems. However, the construction area is quite small, and this
impact can be controlled through the measures presented in Chapter 5.




Figure 74: The current situation of Binh Giang 1 road
366. Comments: Aside from direct impacts on water quality, the project’s rainwater runoff has
indirect impacts on (i) freshwater ecosystems, such as fish kill, invertebrates, benthic fauna,
flora, habitat degradation, and loss of ecosystem services; and (ii) water treatment and health-
related costs: polluted water is the most serious health risk, and it continues to threaten both


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 193   Page
                                                                                              Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
quality of life and public health. Water quality degradation can also increase the cost of water
treatment; (iii) economic activities such as agriculture, fishery, and breeding; and (iv)
aesthetic, cultural, and spiritual qualities, resulting in losses in tourism activities. The indirect
impacts are rated as LOW, short-term (during 2 years of project construction), localized and
can be mitigated due to the following: (i) the affected canals have been subjected to human
activities such as domestic wastewater discharge, improper solid waste management,
agricultural and aquacultural activities, etc. for long period of time, resulting in a low
biodiversity in their ecosystems; (ii) there is only one water taking point for the Binh Giang 1
water supply plant on Canal 200, and the distance to the T6-1 sluice is 200m; (iii) the direct
impact on the water quality is minimal; and the project area has not yet become a well-known
destination for tourists; and (iv) mitigations measures for the impact are proposed in Chapter
5.




Figure 75: Typical power pole of the project
a.3). Construction wastewater
367. Construction wastewater includes wastewater from shipping construction materials,
washing materials, and operating and maintaining construction equipment and machinery.
368. Wastewater from construction material shipping: 200-ton barges are used to ship
materials for the construction of project components. As such, there are roughly 2.36 trips per
day to supply materials to building sites, resulting in an average wastewater volume of 2m3 per
day. The wastewater contains oil, grease, suspended solids, organic matter, nutrients (N, P),
and microorganisms. If not treated and directly discharged into the water source of the
transportation canals (Rach Gia-Ha Tien, canal 200, etc.), it can cause water pollution and
impact aquatic life of the affected canals. However, the project owner will require material



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                   194   Page
                                                                                                Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
suppliers to collect and treat this wastewater. Therefore, the impacts are low, happen during the
transportation period and can be mitigated.
369. Concrete washing wastewater: The concrete mixer should be cleaned after each work
shift. This water is primarily composed of cement particles and sand that remain in the rotating
drum. The amount of water required to clean the mixer is small, around 30-50 liters/mixer/time
and there is a shift per day at each construction site, equivalent to 30-50L/day/site. If this
wastewater is discharged directly into surrounding canals, it may have a minor impact on the
canals' aquatic ecosystem. Because the amount of wastewater is minimal and the composition
is free of pollutants, this waste source can be poured directly into the bare ground of the
construction site, particularly into acid sulfate soil, which will neutralize alum in the soil and
reduce the negative impact of acid sulfate soil on the water environment. Therefore, the direct
impacts caused by construction wastewater are assessed as low and can be mitigated by
measures in Chapter 5.
370. Wastewater from maintenance and cleaning construction equipment: This form of
wastewater contains organic compounds, oil, grease, and suspended particles. The amount of
water needed for various tasks on the construction site is heavily influenced by how the
construction equipment operates. Experiences in previous projects show that each sluice
construction area and road section generated about 0.5m3 for maintenance and 0.75m3 for
cleaning. Therefore, the wastewater volume was showed in Table 79. To avoid direct impacts
on neighboring water bodies, wastewater from maintenance locations will be collected and
treated. For wastewater from routine machinery cleaning that is not oil-contaminated, it will be
treated by passing it through sedimentation tanks to remove all sediments, such as soil and sand,
before being discharged into the environment. For wastewater from equipment maintenance
that is oil-contaminated, it will be treated by passing it through an oil and grease separator
before being discharged. The oil and grease will be collected into containers and treated as
hazardous waste.
371. Comments: the direct impact of construction wastewater is low because (i) the amount
of this wastewater is small, generated scatterly; (ii) the impact occurs on the construction time
(6-24 months); and (iii) the contractors shall collect and sign contracts with relevant unit to
treat the wastewater.

b) Impacts on groundwater

372. Digging holes for power poles and soil excavation for Binh Giang 1 road and sluices may
have an influence on groundwater aquifers. As stated in Section 2.1.2.6.b, the depth of
groundwater in the project area extends from 80 to 100 meters, however digging activities only
take place at 1.5 meters for the power poles and maximum 15m for sluices. Therefore, the
impact on groundwater is negligible.

4.4.1.3. Impacts of solid waste generation

373. Solid waste is generated during construction, which includes (i) workers’ domestic waste,
(ii) construction waste, and (iii) hazardous waste.

a) Workers’ waste

374. Workers’ garbage is primarily made up of styrofoam boxes, plastic bags, vegetables,
plastic tubers, fruits, and leftovers, etc. from their everyday activities on the building site and
in camps. According to the MONRE (2019), the amount of solid waste generated by each
worker is 0.4kg/day. Therefore, given that there are around 5-10 workers assigned to each site,
the daily solid waste of construction workers per site is approximately from 2 to 4 kg. The total
amount of garbage from construction groups in one day is around 70 kg (Table 80).
375. With 2-4kg of domestic waste at a building site (equal to the waste of 2-3 houses) and a
construction time of around 2-3 days to 15 months. Approximately 60% of domestic waste is
organic and easily biodegradable. Soil, water, and air quality can be negatively impacted by the
breakdown of organic waste if appropriate collection and disposal methods are not put in place
throughout the construction phase. Water turbidity, water flow, and sedimentation can all be
affected by this. Additionally, it creates conditions for the development of disease-causing
microorganisms, posing a risk of disease transmission and affecting the health of workers and
residents.
376. Comments: As mentioned earlier, there are 2 landfills in the project area. Therefore, the
project owner/contractor will enter into a contract with the legal entity of Binh Giang commune
landfill and Binh Son commune landfill to collect and treat the waste in compliance with current
regulations at a minimum of 3 times per week. For construction sites where current collection
vehicles cannot reach, the project owner/contractor will arrange for their workers to transport
the waste to the nearest collection transfer point for proper collection and treatment. So, the
impacts are neglible and do not cause any significant impacts on community and worker’s
health.

b) Construction waste

377. Construction solid waste generated mainly consists of demolition waste, other waste
types such as cement bags and discarded formworks.
378. Demolition of trees and structures on land: the biomass generated from the vegetation
release due to the demolition activity includes various crops in the project area, mainly shrubs,
and some timber species. The calculated biomass is based on the experimental biomass
coefficient of 1 hectare of Ogawa and Kato's ground cover type, as shown in Table 81.

379. Thus, the biomass generated from the vegetation clearing for annual crops is 7.5 tons/ha.
However, the vegetation in the project's land acquisition area is mainly shrubs and a few timber
trees. Therefore, the estimated biomass accounts for about 30% of the annual trees, equivalent
to 2.25 tons/ha. According to the project’s resettlement plan, the total permanent land
acquisition area for the project is 15.46ha (mostly for the sluices and connecting roads), which
includes vacant land and public land within the transportation and hydraulic works corridors.
Hence, the biomass is primarily generated from land acquisition for other lands (residential,
and agricultural land). The total area of the remaining land use types (excluding transportation
and hydraulic works) is 2.5 ha. Therefore, the estimated biomass is: 2.25 tons/ha × 2.5 ha ≈ 5.6
tons. Consultation with local people showed that most of the demolition waste materials are
suitable as fill materials and the affected households can produce wood fuel from the trees and
compost from the rest of the waste vegetation materials and remaining wastes of demolition
will be sold and no waste materials left in the construction sites.


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                       197   Page
                                                                                                    Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
380. Other types of construction waste: In addition to demolition waste, construction solid
waste generated consists of scattered soil, sand, cement bags and discarded formworks, as
follows:
                                                         3
  Cement bags: On average, each sluice uses 2,800 m       of concrete, which equates to the use
   of 28,000 cement bags. As a result, 20,000–26,000 cement bags are released into the
   environment. Decomposition of these bags can be a multi-decade process. When discharged
   into the environment, particularly into canals or production areas, they can negatively impact
   on the canals and nearby gardens.
  Discarded formworks: At the moment, most builders are using iron or plastic formworks and
   very few are using wooden ones. All iron or plastic formworks are collected because they
   can be sold. Wooden workforms can also be reused as wood; however, if they are discarded
   in the environment (thrown into canals or buried in the ground), they have a negative impact
   on the canals and nearby gardens.
381. Comments: The impact level of construction waste is evaluated as low, localized, and
manageable, as the majority of construction waste is reused and the remaining waste is
collected, transported, and treated by appropriate entities.

c) Hazardous waste

382. During the construction phase, hazardous waste is generated from the operation of
construction machines and equipment. Hazardous waste includes wasted oil, oily rags, chemical
containers (diesel oil, gasoline, etc.), and welding rods. Furthermore, on-site repair of
construction machinery and equipment might result in waste oil, empty oil containers, oil-
contaminated rags, etc. These are examples of hazardous waste that must be collected, stored,
and disposed of in compliance with regulations. The volume of hazardous waste is contingent
upon the number of equipment and machinery, as well as the volume of materials, including:
  The amount of wasted oil and grease generated in the project area depends on the number of
   vehicles and construction equipment and tools on the construction site; the frequency of
   lubricant changes and machinery maintenance; the amount of lubricating oil discharged
   during each lubricant change/maintenance. The waste is not generated daily; it only occurs
   when construction machinery breaks down unexpectedly. According to the findings of the
   research on recycling waste lubricants into liquid fuel conducted by the Military Science and
   Technology Research Center - Ministry of National Defense, the amount of wasted oil
   discharged from a change of oil is 7 liters per item. Therefore, the amount of waste oil and
   grease of each project components is estimated as follows (Table 82):
      Construction of 23 sluices: At each building site, certain oil-powered equipment and
       tools such as barges, tugboats, excavators, bulldozers, cranes, etc. will be used, totaling
       roughly 5 items. Depending on the operating intensity of the equipment, the average oil
       change and machinery maintenance cycle lasts about 6 months. Thus, each sluice
       construction site produces 55 kg of hazardous waste per quarter.
      Upgradation of 18 sluices: the construction time is short, only about 3 months, so most
       of the equipment does not need maintenance or engine oil changes.
      Road construction: In fact, the main equipment and tools include bulldozers and road
       rollers that need maintenance and periodic oil changes with about 3 items of equipment



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                198   Page
                                                                                             Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
        at each construction site. Thus, each quarter the amount of hazardous waste on a road
        construction site is 31kg.
  Waste from welding rods: A construction site generates waste from welding rods that are
   utilized to construct steel structures. It is estimated that the percentage of welding rods with
   an excessive portion is equivalent to 5-7% of the weight of the welding rods, which is
   approximately 3-5 kg for a sluice. Despite the fact that the weld rods are rarely used and are
   used discontinuously, the waste will be collected and transported to the regulated hazardous
   waste site in accordance with the current regulations.


Table 82: Estimated volume of hazardous waste at each project construction site
                                                            At a sluice            At a road

   3                                         solid              10                      5

                               Total                            55                     31

  Other hazardous waste types are generated during the construction process, including used
   light bulbs, batteries, etc. It is estimated that the amount of this waste at each construction
   site is around 2 kg/month. Without appropriate management measures, these types of waste
   can have negative effects on the soil, water, and air environment. Moreover, residual grease
   in containers can seep into the soil, causing soil pollution.
383. If hazardous waste is not properly collected, it will have two main impacts. Firstly, the
oil and grease will seep into the water, directly affecting aquatic species in canals within the
project area such as canal 200, Don Dong canal, T3-Tuan Thong canal, and the canal along the
sea dike. This will hinder the growth of aquatic plants and animals, and may even cause their
death. Secondly, the heavy metal elements present in the waste will contaminate the food chain,
posing a serious threat to human health.
384. Comments: The volume of hazardous waste generated on each site is minimal, localised
and only occurs during the construction phase. Hazardous material will be collected, managed,
and disposed of in line with applicable legislation. This direct impact is estimated to be LOW
in the shortterm and can be minimized with suitable management and treatment measures in
Chapter 5.

4.4.1.4. Impacts on ecological environment

385. Site clearance, dredging, and construction activities are likely to have negative effects on
both local terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems, as follows:

a) Impacts on terrestrial ecosystems

386. The effects are most likely to occur during site clearance, when existing trees and
vegetation covers within construction sites must be removed. According to the RP of the
project, there are 93,097 trees of all kinds of households affected by the project, including fruit


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 199    Page
                                                                                               Confidential
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
trees and landscape trees, see Table 83. Also, it is necessary to remove the current vegetation
covers. Besides, the clearance of the site for the 23 sluices and the road will certainly result in
the removal of certain trees that have been wildly planted along the canal banks, including
bamboo, eucalyptus, banana, and guava, etc.
387. In addition to the effects on terrestrial flora, the removal of trees and vegetation in
conjunction with construction activities, such as excavation and the operation of construction
equipment and machinary, will affect the local terrestrial fauna in the project area, including
the loss of habitat and feeding areas. The local terrestrial fauna, which is impacted by the
project's implementation, includes mammals (mice, cow), reptiles (snake, lizard), birds
(sparrow, stork) in agricultural or livestock areas (pig, poultry, duck), and pets (dog, cat) in
residential areas.

388. Site surveys indicate that due to its extensive use for agricultural and residential purposes,
the terrestrial flora of the project area is limited to shrubs, grasses, and common trees and the
fauna also is quite poor, consisting primarily of domesticated livestock and poultry that are
raised by households in their gardens, such as chicken, ducks, pigs, and cows, etc. In addition,
consultation with the Department of Agriculture and Environment and the local community has
determined that there are no endangered species or species that require protection within the
project's area. For the aforementioned factors, the impact on the terrestrial ecology is
considered low and can be mitigated by measures in Chapter 5.

b) Impacts on aquatic ecosystems

389. Sluice construction activities mostly happen within the canal along the sea dike, Don
Dong canal, K3-Tuan Thong canal canal 200. According to design, in order to construct the
sluices, the use of a floating platform on which a crane is placed for reinforced concrete pile
driving and sluice gate foundation treatment at the piers in flow of the canals are unavoidable
causing increased turbidity. Phytoplankton production is directly dependent on light penetration
into the water column. Increased water turbidity results in a decrease in light penetration, which
is likely to affect phytoplankton adversely. Unlike zooplankton and phytoplankton, which live
in watery environments and drift with water movements and each water resource will have its
zooplankton and phytoplankton, zoobenthos moves very low, especially Polychaete and
Mollusca. So they will die-off when the environment changes dramatically exceeding their
tolerance and new zoobenthos will be formed to adapt to new living conditions.
390. However, construction of the sluices gates into two to three batches in steel coffer dams.
When the construction activities are carried out in the next batch, water is diverted to the area
where the construction is completed in the previous batch to ensure drainage and supply flow
and will be localised in space and time and therefore aquatic life, even zoobenthos can
recolonize from 3 months12 to 2 years13 after the construction phase and impact on the aquatic
life will be low.

4.4.1.5. Impacts on local traffic

a) Impact on waterway transportation

391. As mentioned in Chapter 2, one of the project area’s assets is its dense network of rivers
and canals, which facilitates water transportation. The major canal system, which includes
primary and secondary canals, makes water transportation easier. As a result, delivering
building materials for the project via canals is feasible given the current conditions of the project
area.
392. As of now, the road system in the project area is capable of accommodating two-wheeled
and four-wheeled vehicles with a load capacity of less than 2.5 tons. As a result, the residents'

12
   Bolam,S.G., Schratzberger, M. and Whomersley, P. (2006). Macro- and meiofaunal recolonization of dredged

1746-1755
13
   Stronkhorst, J., Ariese, F., van Hattum, B., Postma, J.F., de Kluijver, M., Den Besten, P.J., Bergman, M.J.N.,
Daan, R., Murk, A.J. and Vethaak, A.D. (2003). Environmental impact and recovery at two dumping sites for
dredged material in the North Sea. Environ. Pollut. 124: 17-31.

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                              201   Page
                                                                                                           Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
daily travel is primarily conducted via roads, with minimal use of waterways. Almost no
external vessels are present in the secondary canals where the project sluices are all constructed.
Futhermore, the transportation of materials for production and construction of local people in
the region is conducted via waterways, as the road system is not appropriate for vehicles with
a load capacity exceeding 2.5 tons. Through the secondary canals, traders will navigate their
boats during the harvest season to buy agricultural outputs while locals utilize boats to transfer
machinery and equipment to the fields for production. Hence, the construction of the sluice will
affect the navigation of boats within these canals.
393. According to site surveys and community consultations, the number of boats moving on
the canals in the project area is low on off-season days, with only a few boats (about 2-3 boats)
traveling on the canal each day (Figure 76), particularly at the beginning of the Vam Ray canal,
which is currently silted up and thus has no boat traffic. However, during the rice or shrimp
harvesting days, the boat density will rise to approximately 10 boats each day. The daily
increase of 2.36 trips and the cumulative increase of 1,078 trips over the construction period
will pose a small challenge to the environment and waterway traffic operations. Additionally,
the project owner will require the construction material suppliers to implement measures to
ensure water transportation safety, especially during the period of residents’ agricultural
harvests.
394. Besides, there is a bridge (Kenh T5 bridge) located approximately 50 meters from the T5-
1 sluice construction site (Figure 77). This is a concrete bridge, with a width of 3 meters and a
length of about 20 meters. Carelessness during the sluice construction activities could lead to
damage to this bridge. If such an incident occurs, it will affect the travel and health of local
residents.
395. However, the impact is moderate and can be controlled because (i) there are densely
canal network in the project area and in case of bridge failure, there are numerous alternatives
for local movement; and (ii) contractors will apply measures to manage the impact as described
in Chapter 5.
