Figure 77: Current status of Kenh T5 bridge

b) Impacts on local road traffic

396. As stated in Section 2.4.1.2, the internal road system in the area is limited. Impacts of the
project construction on the local road traffic are assessed below:
397. Sluices: The project's sluices, which include 23 new sluices and 18 upgraded sluices, are
not situated on road transportation routes. Consequently, the construction and enhancement of
these sluices will not have an impact on the road traffic of the project area.
398. The road: the impact is presented in Section 4.4.2.3.
399. The power lines: installing power lines, particularly the erection of poles, will have an
impact on the movement of residents. However, most of the power lines will be located in
sparse area with very few people. Only there are some households living in the power lines
along the Binh Giang 1 road and along Don Dong canal, including:
  For the power lines running along the project road (Binh Giang 1 road): This road,
   which is quite wide and has shoulders, will be constructed as part of the project.
   Consequently, the installation of poles along this road will have a negligible effect on the
   flow of traffic along the road.
  For the power lines running along the road along the Don Dong canal: the road width
   is only approximately 2.5 to 3.5 meters. Consequently, the road will be temporarily closed
   to guarantee the safety of residents during the installation of poles. This will have an impact
   on the travel of residents.
400. Comments: Nevertheless, this effect is assess to be minor, happens in a short-term (about
1hour for erecting electric poles), and manageable due to the followings: (i) Erecting poles
usually takes about 15-60 minutes; (ii) The traffic density on this road is low (about 2-5


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                203   Page
                                                                                             Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
vehicles/hour); (ii) Residents can travel when the pole is being placed into the hole under the
guidance of the constructors; and (iv) The construction schedule and alternative routes will be
announced to residents 15 days before construction begins.

4.4.1.6. Risks of local flooding

401. The excavation, earthwork, and leveling activities have the potential to impede water
drainage. Simultaneously, precipitation within the construction site has the potential to result
in local inundation of the site. If proper arrangements are not made for temporary drainage on
the construction sites of the road, it will result in local flooding. The road construction locations
are prone to local inundation. This will have adverse effects on the health and working
performance of workers, as well as on the surrounding population. Additionally, it will cause
damage to the construction works. The prolonged local flooding will (i) have direct and indirect
impacts on living conditions, production activities, and travel for people and workers; (ii) result
in the loss of beautiful landscapes, poor environmental sanitation, and potential sources of
communicable diseases; and (iii) cause damage to ongoing construction items.
402. However, the impact mainly occurs during the rainy season (May to November). The
impact is rated to be low because (i) the majority of the road will be built during the dry season;
(ii) the road is located in an area with no history of flooding; (iii) rainwater can drain quickly
because the road is close to the Binh Giang 1 canal; and (iv) the impact can be reduced by
popular technical mitigation measures such as pumping and temporary drainage, as proposed
in Chapter 5.

4.4.1.7. Risks of land subsidence and bank erosion during heavy rains

403. There is a risk of land subsidence and erosion at sluice abutments, bridge piers, and along
access roads during the sluice construction. Such land subsidence and erosion could damage
the property of contractors and local residents of Binh Giang and Binh Son communes, who
live near the construction sites of 23 sluices. More seriously, they could pose safety risks to
workers and local residents. However, the risk is assessed to be low because (i) the access roads
to the sluices are suitable for the existing transportation system and align with the current
plannings; (ii) the maximum longitudinal and transverse slopes of the bridge are 5-6% and 2%,
respectively. The design elevations of the access roads through residential areas are optimally
chosen based on the elevations of existing structures and comply with the hydrological control
elevations of drainage works, approximately 30-50 cm; (iii) the foundation where the bridges
are placed is reinforced with concrete columns; and (iv) the road surface is paved with asphalt
concrete, crushed stone base, with Eyc ≥140Mpa, design axle load P=10T, meeting the
standards for allowable elastic deflection, surface shear standards, tensile strength, and flexural
testing standards for monolithic material layers.
404. However, the risk can be controlled and mitigated by adhering to strict construction
measures, which will be proposed in Chapter 5.

4.4.1.8. Risks from raw material supply

405. The building of the sluices and the road will require a large amount of materials (soil and
sand) for backfilling. The project material sources are detailed in section 1.3.6. It is specified
that materials including soil, sand and stone for construction must be obtained from the licensed
mines; cement, iron and steel from An Giang or other provinces. However, some contractors


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                   204   Page
                                                                                                Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
may not comply and choose the cheaper material supplies from the illegal sources, resulting in
the following risks and impacts: (i) Material supply conditions and properties are not meet the
standards, resulting in lower construction quality; (ii) The supply quantity is not assured, which
has an impact on the project items' implementation progress; (iii) Illegal exploitation of
construction materials does not guarantee environmental conditions; and (iv) Potential risks
include labor accidents due to inadequate PPEs and traffic accidents during raw material
delivery if the chosen route is not suited for the project.
406. The indirect risks are rated as LOW, short-term and can be mitigated by the following:
(i) All raw materials used for the project must be sourced from licensed mines; (ii) Experimental
conditions on construction geology are conducted prior to construction; (iii) The risks are short-
term, only occur during the construction period; and (iv) mitigation measures are proposed in
Chapter 5.

4.4.1.9. Impacts on social aspects

a) Impacts and risks due to labor influx

407. During the peak period, it is expected that 24 construction teams will be formed, each
with 5-10 workers per team but 16 camps will be set up. Workers who will work on the road
and the power lines will rent local people’s houses or move to nearby camps. The main social
problems are listed as follows:
a.1). Risks of social conflicts
408. Risks of social conflict related to labor influx include:
  Potential conflict between workers and local communities due to differences in habits,
   customs, culture, lifestyle, employment and income. In densely populated construction
   areas, such as those for the construction of the sluices and road, this impact is expected to
   be higher than that of power lines,
  The influx of labor can    lead to social disorders and give rise to social issues such as
   gambling, drug, and prostitution. In addition, negative effects of untreated and uncollected
   wastewater from worker camps building the sluices and road include bad smell and poor
   local residents' health. This can cause workers to clash with residents of the area. Besides,
   labor influx could increase the risk of Gender Based Violence (GBV) and sexual exploitation
   and abuse (SEA) Sexual Harassment (SH).
  Conflicts arise between local residents and contractors when waste and emissions, including
   dust, solid waste, hazardous waste, and wasted rock and soil, are discharged directly into the
   residents' rice fields and gardens, or when rainwater runoff sweeps away waste into their
   gardens during road construction.
409. Comments: The project will be implemented in a rural area that lacks public services and
health systems. As a result, the potential social conflict risks will be medium, localised because:
(i) there are 16 workers camps and the camps will be located in agricultural areas; (ii) An
Giang has experience managing workers from different provinces; and (iii) local people are
familiar with the movement of the influx of workers, and local authorities have experience
managing immigrant labor. The risks are short-term, happen during construction and can be
mitigated by measures in Chapter 5.



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 205   Page
                                                                                              Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
a.2). Influx of additional population (“followers”)
410. Workers from neighboring provinces or commues of An Giang province who come to
work on the project may bring their families, adding to the load on the project communes' social
and service systems and promoting migration. However, the risks will be low because workers
will be participating in the construction process for 6 months to 18 months, and contractors
will be encouraged to hire local labor.
a.3). Local inflation of prices
411. For the project to be carried out, a certain number of workers and experts will need to be
sent to the sites. The mobilized workers could raise various personal needs (food, clothing,
housing, etc.), resulting in the risk of local price inflation. However, the risks will be low for
the following reasons: i) the construction sites are dispersed across two communes in the Hon
Dat district; ii) only a small number of workers (approximately 210 workers during the period
of highest demand) will be mobilized to the sites; iii) workers will only spend a brief period on
the sites (6 to 18 months); and iv) An Giang province is the most active province in the Mekong
Delta, and supply may significantly exceed worker demands.
a.4). Increase of illicit behavior and crime
412. The risk of illegal behavior and crime will be a result of labor influx. Workers’
mobilization may cause social disturbance, illicit behaviors, and crimes due to disparities in
income, working, customs, etc. Furthermore, there is a risk that the workers will engage in
social evils such as gambling or prostitution. However, the risks are rated as low because the
number of migrant workers is small and many workers from neighboring provinces have
already arrived to work in the fishery sector in the province. Locals are familiar with the flow
of migrant workers, and local authorities have experience managing immigrant labor.
e.4). Increase in burden and competition for public service provision
413. The project activities will increase demand for water, power, and solid waste disposal, all
of which are managed by the public sector. However, the project's electricity consumption will
not be concentrated in a single site, and it will be met by connecting to the national grid via the
220/110 kV station, as well as investing in the installation of power lines. Regarding solid waste
treatment for the project, the current service of the solid waste collection does not cover all the
project area, so contractors will transport it to the nearest transfer points for the URENCO to
transport for final disposal to mitigate impacts due to the generation of solid waste of the
project. Therefore, the competition for public service provision of the project will be low.
a.5). Increased pressure on accommodations and rents
414. Previous project experiences have demonstrated that project communes can only
accommodate a certain number of migrant workers without putting pressure on housing and
rent. Project contractors will establish worker camps, rent out local houses to workers in the
project communes, or prioritize mobilizing local workers to reduce demand for worker housing.
As a result, the impact is low and end when the construction is completed.
a.6). Sexual exploitation and abuse/sexual harassment
415. Since all activities of the project will take place in rural areas, where contractors and local
authorities have easy control over sexual exploitation and abuse (SEA) and harassment (SH),
the risk assessment indicates that this is not a significant concern. Besides, mitigation measures
for the risks are included in the ESMP and code of conduct (CoC) for contractors.


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                  206   Page
                                                                                               Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
a.7). Increased risk of communicable diseases and burden on local health services
416. Because of the poor living conditions, the disease transmission will have a negative
impact on both workers and local inhabitants. It will cause eye and skin diseases, and respiration
diseases. Local healthcare systems get overcrowded as a result of this. This risk is assessed as
moderate because (i) local workers will be prioritized for the project; (ii) PPE will be provided
to all project workers; and (iii) training on OHS will be provided to project workers before they
work for the project. This risk will be end when the construction is finished.

b) Impacts due to improper access

417. The construction of the new road will require the use of a portion of the existing road as
construction sites, as it will be conducted on the current road surface. The sites require fencing
for safety reasons because they are in front of people's houses. This fencing system will impede
access of local people. The impact will cease after the road construction is finished, and an
alternate route will be established throughout the construction period.
418. The impact is considered low because: (i) the successive construction method will be used
for the road; (ii) the impact occurs over a short period of time (about 2-3 months for each
household); and (iii) the road is strongly supported by locals, and when it is operational, their
movement will improve.

c) Impacts on fishing activities

419. The results of on-site surveys and community consultations indicate that there is almost
no aquaculture or fishing activity on the canals where the sluice will be built. If there are any,
they are merely recreational activities rather than the residents’ livelihoods. Therefore, the
construction activities of the sluices will not impact fishing activities in the area.

4.4.1.10. Labor violence and hard-working conditions

a) Labor violence and hard-working conditions

420. Types of Project Workers
  Direct workers are those who are directly employed by the project management unit. They
   are staff of the An Giang PPMU and consultants hired by the PPMU to work on the project.
   In the framework of the project, the PPMU is responsible for the management and
   supervision of the overall implementation of the project, including contractors’ construction
   work. Direct workers are staff (permanent or term) who are directly employed by the PPMU.
   Given that the PPMU is unlikely to have sufficient expertise to manage the entire project, it
   will require assistance from experts in certain technical areas (project management and
   coordination, financial management, procurement, construction, assessment and
   management of E&S risks and impacts, independent E&S monitoring, among others). Labor
   requirements are calculated from the start of the project through its end.
  Contracted workers are those who are employed or engaged by third parties to perform
   work in relation to core functions of the project, regardless of location. An Giang PPMU
   will hire numerous contractors to carry out various types of project work, including technical
   design consultants, construction supervision consultant (CSC), and civil works construction.
   The amount of manpower required for these activities varies according to the size of each


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                207   Page
                                                                                             Confidential
                     ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
      activity. The timing of labor requirements will fluctuate depending on the project stages. In
      general, the construction will last about 6 months to 2 years.
      Primary Supply Workers: The construction work will require primary supplies essential for
      the functions of the works, such as sand, soil, etc.. Where the contractors will source such
      materials directly from primary suppliers on an ongoing basis, the workers engaged by such
      primary suppliers are deemed “primary supply workers”, as defined in ESS2.
      Community Workers: The project will not engage community workers as defined under the
      ESS2 as a community contribution to the project. Community members who may be engaged
      by the project contractors will be categorized and managed as “contracted workers”.
421. Direct government workers. Other than the above-mentioned project workers,
stakeholders working on the project will include government civil servants from relevant
departments and agencies of An Giang province, such as Department of Finance (DOF),
Department of Home Affairs (DOHA), Department of Construction (DOC), Department of
Agriculture and Environment, District Land Fund Development Centers (DLFDC), district Site
Clearance and Compensation Councils (DSCCCs), and provincial, district, commune People’s
Committees (PC). They will be subjected to the terms and conditions of their current public
sector employment, which are governed by the Labor Code, the Public Employees Law, and
the Civil Servants Law. There will be no legal transfer of their employment to the project. The
ESS2 will not apply to government civil servants. However, if they visit the project sites, they
must adhere to E&S risk management regulations14.
422. Totaling, the project will mobilize approximately 326 people within its timeline.
423. Labor disputes are common in the infrastructure construction field in Viet Nam. Demand
for limited employment opportunities; labor wage rates and payment delays; disagreement over
working conditions; and health and safety concerns in the workplace are all potential causes of
labor disputes. Additionally, employers such as contractors/subcontractors may retaliate against
employees who demand better working conditions, raise concerns about unsafe or unhealthy
work environments, or voice any grievances. These kinds of circumstances may result in labor
unrest.
424. This risk is assessed to be medium bbecause (i) the impacts are localized within the
construction site and scattered in 2 communes; (ii) short construction period from 6- 24 months;
(iii) workers are provided with PPE and reasonable construction time; (iv) mitigation measures
for these risks have been provided in the ESCOPs and workers will be trained on labor safety,
traffic safety, sanitation before starting any civil works. However, to implement the project's
policy on fair labor treatment in accordance with ESS2, the project has prepared the LMP that
also outlines a labor GRM. Besides, project contractors/subcontractors will be required to
provide information about employment to their laborers while negotiating to reach an
agreement on labor terms and conditions prior to signing labor contracts. Monitoring
compliance with the labor contract terms and conditions and strictly enforcing the GRM for
laborers will be effective mitigation measures for resolving labor conflicts during the
implementation of the project.




14
  ESS2 applies in part to government workers, through the provision of paragraphs 17 to 20 (Protecting the Work Force) and
paragraphs 24 to 30 (Occupational Health and Safety).


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                                     208    Page
                                                                                                                   Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
b) Use of child labor, school dropout and forced labor

425. Based on the project characteristics, some unskilled labor will be mobilized. In this case,
the child laborers can be used for tasks that do not require high qualifications (according to
Vietnam’s regulations, a child is under 15 years old). Due to limited knowledge, children can
suffer from labor abuse that affects their psychology, health, and ability to learn. Child labor
may cause school dropouts for working and earn money. However, the impact is assessed to be
minor as (i) The experiences with the WB-financed and state budget projects show no cases of
child labor or forced labor have been recorded in Kien Giang (now An Giang) province; (ii) the
WB and the Government of Viet Nam have strong and comprehensive policies/requirements to
protect children from child labor and other abuse; (iii) According to the socio-economic survey
results, no case of child labor use or forced labor was recorded in the project area; (vi) The
contractors have to commit not to hiring child labor for the project-related jobs and this
commitment shall be one of the required conditions in bidding and contractual documents; and
(v) The project owner will coordinate with local authorities and related units to strictly control
the contractor's labor use.

c) Gender based violence (GBV)

426. The concentration of 5-10 workers at a construction site, with approximately 75% men
and 25% women, has the potential to cause social disturbance. This may affect women who are
directly employed on the construction site, as well as those who reside in the adjacent area and
their families during construction.
427. According to previous similar projects, unmarried women may face a disadvantage in
getting married or becoming single mothers. Meanwhile, married women can be influenced by
the happiness of their families. Furthermore, they may be infected with communicable diseases,
such as HIV/AIDS and syphilis, which can have an impact on the next generation.
428. Within this project, the impact occurs only during the construction phase, which lasts
from 6 to 18 months. The impact is considered minor for the following reasons: (i) The socio-
economic survey results indicate that no negative impacts on women have been observed in the
project area; (ii) The number of women workers is small because local workers are given
priority by contractors; (iii) Training programs and information disclosure will provide workers
with information about social diseases and prevention methods; and (iv) Regulations and
penalties will be established to address any violations by workers at the construction site; and
(v) Contractors will closely collaborate with local authorities to manage the number of workers
at the construction sites.

4.4.1.11. Community and worker health and safety

a) Waterway incidents

429. Because almost all the materials will be transported by water, accidents can occur due to
boats colliding during the travel to the work sites. These incidents can cause serious impacts on
the environment, especially on the water quality, such as increasing turbidity by stirring the bed
on contact or through oil spills from engine damage. The emergencies have moderate negative
impact because there likely is a low frequency of occurrence. However, if an accident happens
during the construction phase it could create a significant impact on habitat, people, and



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                209   Page
                                                                                             Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
properties and this impact are long term. Therefore, an appropriate rapid-response plan is
needed to ensure the management of emergencies.

b) Labor accidents

430. The construction of the project's structural items will generate occupational heath and
safety (OHS) risks for project workers, including:
     Health risks. Health risks due to working in a pollution environment with high dust
      concentration are caused by the construction activities of site clearance and demolishing of
      old structures, soil excavations, leveling, machine operation, and transportation.
     Accidents due to structure collapse. Excavation and demolishing activities can cause
      damage of nearby existing structure foundations leading to the structure collapse and
      causing serious injuries to workers.
     Accidents due to falling in water. Workers may fall in water and cause serious injuries
      during dredging, pilling, or constructing sluices and bridges due to dizzying or/and strong
      wind, waves, or/and careless working without compliance with working safety.
     Accidents due to falling from high levels. Workers may fall from a high-level due to
      incorrectly installed scaffolding, uninsured ladders, and unprotected steel bars during
      construction of operational houses, sluices, bridges, electric systems, etc. This leads to
      labor accidents.
     Falling objects: Workers working below a house/power tower being constructed may be
      hit by hard objects falling from a high-level causing injury.
     Electrical shock: Workers may be exposed to live electricity during testing transmission
      lines, and substations and using power for soldering. Electrical shock can cause serious
      injuries or fatalities.
     Accidence due to iron cut/ soldering. Workers cutting irons and soldering without using
      proper PPE (e.g. protective glasses) may suffer from eye accidents with serious injuries
      even blinding.
     Accidents due to fire and explosion. Welding workers can generate fire-catching
      flammable materials nearby, especially fuels and gases leading to a fire or explosion
      causing serious effects on workers and communities.
     Accidents due to heavy equipment. Several working sites may gather many workers and
      heavy machines (e. g cranes for sluice construction and upgradation) working in a narrow
      space for different works. Moving heavy equipment may hit workers working nearby
      causing injuries. In addition, the construction activities involving heavy types of machinery
      will be quite noisy. Loud, repetitive, and excessive noise causes long-term hearing
      problems, such as deafness. Noise can also be a dangerous distraction and may distract the
      worker from the task at hand, which can cause accidents.
     Health risks on disease infection. Contamination can occur during disease outbreaks if
      proper hygiene practices are not followed regularly (frequent and proper hand hygiene and
      wearing masks to serve as barriers to human-to-human transmission of the virus).
     Occupational disease. Workers working under conditions with noise generated from
      heavy machines (e.g. Power operators) can suffer from occupational diseases such as
      deafness.

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 210   Page
                                                                                              Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
     Inadequate facilities: Lavatory facilities (toilets and washing areas) are sometimes
      inadequate for personal hygiene when a number of people work on construction sites.
      Adequate supplies of clean drinking water, clean eating areas, and a safe working
      environment need to be provided to ensure the occupational health and safety of the
      workers.
     Lack of awareness of occupational health and safety requirements: In conjunction with
      other facility controls and safety systems, personal protective equipment (PPE) protects
      workers against workplace hazards. However, there is often a lack of the use of PPE and
      safe work practices on construction sites in Viet Nam. Furthermore, materials for
      handwashing and hygiene, as well as masks, are frequently unavailable at work sites. As a
      result, in some cases, workers' eyes, heads, necks, ears, hands, arms, feet, legs, ears, lungs,
      and even the entire body may be injured due to a lack of appropriate PPE.
431. The above-mentioned OHS risks will mainly have been rated low to moderate because
(i) the risks are localized within construction sites; (ii) the risks will primarily stem from the
construction activities that will occur in a short time; (iii) workers will be provided with PPE
and first aid stations; and (iv) mitigation measures for these risks will be in place, as specified
in the ESMP and C-ESMPs. Standard Operation Procedures (SOPs) will be developed for
workers to follow during the construction period and the SOPs include an evacuation plan in
case of hazards (e.g., earthquake, fire, extreme weather events, and accidents). Workers will
be trained in labor safety, traffic safety, and sanitation practices prior to the start of construction.
Furthermore, during the operation phase, all operation units will develop and implement
operational and maintenance (O&E) procedures for sluices, connecting roads, and low and
medium-voltage lines.

c) Risk of fire, explosion and leakage of fuel

432. Emergencies of fire and explosion could be incurred through fuel storage and unsafe use
of electrical equipment. The consequences are extremely adverse and could cause injuries,
disabilities, and loss of life. The reasons for fire and explosion are (i) unsafe or inappropriate
firefighting systems and management at fuel storage areas on construction sites; (ii) electric
generators supplying energy for machinery, the equipment could cause electrical incidents
resulting in fires; (iii) use of heating equipment could cause fire or occupational accidents such
as burns. The emergencies are a moderate to substaintial negative impact and when the the risk
happens, it cause long term affects. Because these emergencies could occur at any time thus it
requires a specific emergency preparedness and response plan to be in place at the construction
site as well as appropriate equipment to minimize the probability of these emergencies as
proposed in Chapter 5.

d) Risk of security forces

433. It is expected that the project contractors will either retain or contract workers to provide
security to protect their staff and properties during the construction process. In Bank-financed
projects, it is common practice for the contractor to employ one or two individuals to provide
security for the construction materials and equipment, particularly during nighttime. So far,
there have been no reported incidents. Therefore, the risk posed by this security arrangement
to those within and outside the project site is assessed as small and only occurs during the
construction phase. To mitigate the risk, the contractor will be required to implement proper
procedures for recruiting and maintaining the security force at the site.

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                     211   Page
                                                                                                  Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
e) Risks of community’s health and safety

434. Numerous construction activities may cause risks of health and safety for communities in
the project area, including:
     Demolition of houses and structures on land cause accident risks for local people as well
      as for project workers
     Labor accident, fire, explosion, short circuit and electric shock posed by the construction
      activities will also affect the safety of the community.
     Inadequate lighting and fencing of construction sites inside of construction sites can be
      dangerous for pedestrians and vehicles especially during the night time.
     Increasing of traffic due to boats and vehicles movements to construction sites may cause
      inconvenience for local population as well.
     Road construction activities limit household access.
     Untimely and inefficient disposal of solid waste and improper sanitary conditions
      generated by the construction workers at construction sites and workers’ camps may cause
      pollution of the surrounding environment. This creates conditions for development of flies
      and mosquitoes and possible outbreaks of diseases such as diarrhea, dengue fever, etc.
      affecting the health of the local community.
     The transportation of raw materials and wastes will contribute to increase traffic flow on
      canals and roads. Also, noise from construction activities will also pose health and safety
      risks to local people.
     Risks of outsiders entering the construction sites: Illegal entry of local people into the
      construction area to play or pick up construction materials will cause safety risks, possibly
      endangering lives (especially children) and loss of project materials. Therefore,
      construction sites will be covered and posted with warning signs to prevent unauthorized
      access.
435. The impact level is assessed small to moderate as: (i) the impacts are localized in each
construction area; (ii) construction period is short from 6-18 months; (iii) the number of workers
is from 5-10 workers each site; moreover, local workers will be prioritized by the Contractor;
(iv) workers will be train on labor safety, traffic safety, sanitation; (v) warning signs and fences
will be installed in construction sites; and (iv) these impacts can be minimized through
ESCOPs.

4.4.1.12. Impacts on physical cultural resources (PCR)s

436. Construction activities may affect religious and cultural structures such as pagodas or
temples by dust, noise, and vibration, disturbing traffic or access to these works, or cause visual
impacts or nuisance if construction materials and wastes are loaded improperly. Loud noise
may also affect religious procedures.
437. There are no important historical and cultural sites identified in the project construction
sites. There are two sites, Siviranba Chanpa Pagoda located about 240m to sluice K3-7 and
Temple of Vam Ray Lady located about 200m to sluice VR1. The sites are used regularly,
particularly when offerings are made every 15th day, on the first day of the lunar month, and



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                  212   Page
                                                                                               Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
during festival days. No direct impacts on the temples are expected, but there may be indirect
impacts (noise) on people visiting them.
438. In addition, the project involves considerable excavation. Therefore, there are chances
that some artifacts may be exposed during construction.
439. Comments: There is likely small negative impact on Siviranba Chanpa Pagoda and
Temple of Vam Ray Lady. To mitigate potential noise impacts, equipment with low noise
signatures will be used, work activities will avoid days of worship, and if necessary, noise
barriers will be installed. No adverse impacts on other historical and cultural heritage features
are expected during the construction phase of the project. However, during execution of the
works, the construction there might be chances that some historical and cultural objects or
remains might be found. The process for addressing these cases is presented under the Chance
Find Procedures presented in Chapter 5.

4.4.2.   Specific Impacts

4.4.2.1. Impact on domestic water supply

440. Currently, within the project area, there are 2 water supply stations, including one in Binh
Giang commune and one in Binh Son commune that use surface water for domestic purposes
(Table 48). Furthermore, the Binh Giang water intake point of this station is located on Canal
200 and is approximately 200 meters away from the construction site of sluice T6-1 (Figure
49) while the intake point of Binh Son plant is 310m from sluice VR-1 (Figure 50). The 2 plants
supply water for a total of 534 households. However, the water flow in this area is only one
way, from large canals (where there are two water intake points) into smaller canals (where the
two sluices are being built) therefore the possibility of turbidity or pollutants moving from the
sluice building sites to the two water intake sites is extremely low (see Figure 78 and Figure
79). Futhermore, all activities of on-water construction activities will be implemented in coffer
dams (Figure 81). The likelihood of increasing TSS and turbidity only happens where the coffer
dam is installed (only 1 days) and it will occur durng the low tide. Therefore, the project will
insignificantly affect the domestic water supply and the impact will be shorterm (during the
construction time). The impact will be mitigated because the project owner will require the
constructors to implement mitigation measures in Chapter 5.

                                                                                            Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

4.4.2.2. Impacts on livelihoods

a) Impacts on rice cultivation

441. Sluice construction: The canals where the project's sluices are primarily located provide
freshwater for two rice crops. Therefore, construction activities that narrow the flow and
increase turbidity and other pollutants will affect the water supply for rice production. For rice
cultivation, the most significant water requirement is during field preparation and the first week
after sowing. Thus, contractors need to carry out sluice construction activities between the two
cropping seasons as well as during the period from the 25th day after sowing to the end of the
rice season.
442. Road construction: There are numerous rice fields and fruit orchards with total area of
195ha along the Binh Giang 1 road (105 ha only using water from Binh Giang 1 canal and 90ha
using both water from Binh Giang 1 canal and Canal 500, see Figure 80) and 36 culverts to
take water from the Binh Giang 1 canal. The construction of the road will affect these culverts
and the impact occurs during maximum 2 dry seasons (from April to November) in when the
residents in Giong Ke hamlet, Binh Giang commune need to take water for their rice fields.
However, the Project Owner will have a plan to rebuild these culverts before starting the
construction of the road and constractors need to prepare specific construction plans to ensure
that residents can still access water to meet their production needs during the dry season.
Therefore, the impact is assessed as minor and end when the construction of the 36 new culverts
are completed.




Figure 80: Area of paddy fields and fruit trees along the Binh Giang 1 road

b) Impacts on shirmp farming

443. Sluice construction: During the dry season, residents in the region from Don Dong canal
to the sea, particularly between T5-K3 canal, engage in shrimp farming on rice land. The
construction of the K3-4, K3-5, and T6-3 sluices will occur in that region. However, these
sluices are positioned at the end of the water supply sources for shrimp aquaculture. As a result,
the sluice building is predicted to have a minor impact on 30 ha of shrimp farming in Binh
Giang commune within 6 months. The impact is limited to the sluice construction area and has
no effect on water intake, drainage, or the shrimp farming environment of the whole region.
Besides, Contractors shall apply mitigation measures for the impact as in mentioned in Chapter
5.
444. Sluice improvement: The current sluice gates will be replaced with new ones to
guarantee adequate water supply and drainage for the region. The duration of the replacement
period is relatively short, typically lasting around 1-2 months. After the installation of the new


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                215 P a g e
                                                                                              Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
sluice gates, the construction unit will remove the existing sluice gates and begin utilizing the
new ones. Currently, only 2 out of 18 upraded sluices (Kenh Hai, and Kenh Nam sluices) are
operated for shrimp farming. In a shrimp crops, the sluices are opened 3 times, including: 30
days in April, 15 days in June and 15 days in July. For the rest of the time, the sluices will be
closed to prevent saltwater intrusion or to drain water from the fields to the sea. As such, the
contractor can proactively choose the timing of sluice gate replacement to avoid impacting the
shrimp farming activities of the local residents. Therefore, the impact on shrimp farming is
minimal (only happen during the upgradation of the Kenh Hai sluice and Kenh Nam sluice) and
30ha in Binh Giang commune, and short-term (30 days).

4.4.2.3. Impacts on water traffic

a) Impacts due on-water construction

445. Construction activities will take place within the steel coffer dams on the beds of the
canals of Don Dong, No.200, No. 2, and the horizontal canal (Figure 81). This operation will
partially narrow the width of these canals. Additionally, concentrating a large number of
vehicles and equipment on the canals of to support construction will significantly impact
waterway traffic. This leads to the limitation of the speed of vehicles passing through the canals
during the construction period and these vehicles having to travel by other rivers and canals at
certain times. Furthermore, it causes to collision risks between local people's boats and project
construction boats and completed parts of the sluices, especially during nighttime.
446. The investigation results show that: (i) the project area is not a waterway route; (ii) on the
canals Don Dong, No.200, No. 2, and the horizontal canal where sluices will be built, the
majority of vessels are small boats weighing around 50 tons (Figure 76), primarily used for
transporting materials and agricultural products; and (iii) the density of boat traffic is low,
averaging only 5-10 boats per day, but increases during periods when materials and outputs
must be transported for production or harvest seasons. The canals’ current width ranges from
10 to 22 meters (Figure 82), and this width allows for boat traffic on the other half. As a result,
the impact on the waterway in the canals will occur is small and short-term (during sluice
foundation building, which generally takes about a month). Also, the project owner shall
request that contractors not plan sluice foundation treatment during peak periods.




Figure 81: Coffer dams for sluice construction




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 216   Page
                                                                                              Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
447. Furthermore, the construction timeline and alternative canal routes will be consulted with
communities and released 15 days before construction begins, allowing people to plan their
journey accordingly. In addition, the contractors will install signage and deploy traffic
management workers to the construction sites. As a result, the impacts are assessed to be
moderate.

b) Impacts on the movement and house access of local people due to the road construction

448. The project road is now a dirt road with around 74 houses residing along it, as previously
stated. However, the majority of these residents are primarily clustered within a radius of
around 800 meters from NH80. They so usually travel from the direction of NH 80 inward
rather than across the whole length of this road. During construction, the current road will be
excavated for the new road. Consequently, the absence of alternate routes for people currently
living along the road and road users in Binh Giang commune will have a substantial effect on
their travel due to road construction. Currently, the non-built-up area of the land strip on the
eastern side of the Binh Giang 1 canal (in Binh Giang commune) is approximately 50 meters
in width, whereas the reqquired width for road construction, which includes its protection
corridor, is only 12 meters. As a result, the contractor has the option to utilize the remaining
width to establish a alternative route for residents during road construction. As a result, the
impact of road construction on the mobility of residents is negligible, temporary, and
manageable due to the following: (i) The current road is narrow and of poor quality (Figure
43), which makes it inconvenient for residents in Binh Giang commune to travel; (ii) A
temporary road will be established during construction to ensure continuous travel along the
entire route; (iii) The construction period is short, lasting approximately 18 months; and (iv)
Once the new road is completed, residents' travel will be significantly improved.



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                   217 P a g e
                                                                                                 Confidential
                      ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
4.4.2.4. Impacts by the relocation of infrastructure

449. Some public infrastructure, such as electric poles, telecommunications, and television
cable lines may be potentially relocated by construction of the road. The owners of the affected
infrastructures, such as the Hon Dat power company for electricity lines and the former Hon
Dat branches of FPT, Viettel, and VNPT companies for internet cable lines, will implement the
infrastructure relocation. The quality of living conditions, the performance of work and study,
and the level of annoyance experienced by the residents will be adversely affected by the
temporary disruption of the infrastructure.
450. However, the effects of these temporary disruptions are minimal due to their short
duration, such as a maximum of 2-3 hours for power supply and one hour for internet access.
The owners of the affected infrastructure have extensive experience in relocating similar
projects within Kien Giang (now An Giang) Province. This issue is a common occurrence in
infrastructure projects in Viet Nam, and the Kien Giang (now An Giang) PPMU has
successfully collaborated with infrastructure owners in previous WB-financed projects (ICRSL,
CRSD, WB6, etc.) in recent years.

4.4.2.5. Site-specific impacts and risks

a) Impacts on ethnic minorities (EM)

451. The survey results obtained during the project preparation phase indicated the presence
of about 126 EM households in the project area. EMs will have long-term benefits through
investment in the components of the project. However, they can also be negatively affected by
environmental impacts and risks, as follows:
         Common construction-related impacts and risks: These include increased levels of dust,
          gas emissions, noise, vibration, loss of vegetation cover and trees, waste and wastewater
          generation, water quality reduction affecting aquatic lives, soil erosion sedimentation, and
          localized flooding risks.
         Presence of migrant workers: During construction time, many workers will come and stay
          in the project area. This may cause social difficulties, affecting local security and health,
          especially young EM people.
452. Scale and magnitude of the impacts on EM is higher than that of Kinh people. Therefore,
specific policies and action plans to minimize the potential impact of land acquisition and
resettlement will be addressed through the project RP. In addition, an Ethnic Minority
Development Plan (EMDP) has been prepared for the project. The EMDP will help to assist the
devlopment of the EMs in the project area.

b) Sluice construction and upgradation

453. Impacts on sensitive receptors due to sluice construction are presented in Table 84.

      1      Sluice T5-1

4.5. IMPACTS AND RISKS IN THE OPERATION PHASE

4.5.1.    Generic impacts

4.5.1.1. Dust and Exhausted Gases

455. Once the connecting road and bridges are operational, the traffic flow rate will experience
a significant increase. As a result, the ambient air in the residential areas near these items will
be impacted by the dust and gas emissions from the traffic on the road and bridges.
456. Therefore, it is assed to be “small” to the air quality and people living along the road and
near the bridges because (i) the air quality in the area is quite good and has no sign of pollution;
(ii) tree lines along the road planted in the project will help reduce the air pollution; (iii) people’s
awareness on limiting use of fossil fuels (petrol, oil, etc.) is raising, biogasoline (E5) and electric
bikes are used more popular recently; (iv) the road and bridges will be cleaned periodically by
a professional and experienced unit under the management of the province; (v) traffic and
warning signs are established suitably for characteristics of each area; and (vi) there are few
people living along the road and bridges. However, the impact is long term (during the life span
of the road and bridges).

4.5.1.2. Noise and Vibration

a) Noise and vibration from vehicles

457. The flow of vehicles will cause noise and vibrations on the road and bridges (Table 84),
affecting the living conditions of the adjacent residents. According to Table 87, noise levels at
20m from the source are within the acceptable limits of QCVN 26:2010/BTNMT (between 6
a.m. and 9 p.m.). As a result of the low population density in the project area, the impact of
noise on residents is assessed as low but it happen in a long term (during the life span of the
road and bridges).
Table 86: Noise levels of some vehicles
 No.     Type of vehicles              Noise level (dBA)
  1      4-seat cars                              77
  1      4-stroke motorbikes                      70
  2      2-stroke motorbikes                      73
Table 87: Maximum noise level from the distance to the source
 No.     Type of vehicles                         Noise from the distance to the source (dBA)
                                              10m      20m         50m          100m         200m
  1      4-seat cars                          60.5      54.5       46.5         40.5           34.5
  2      4-stroke motorbikes                  53.5      47.5       39.5         33.5           27.5
  3      2-stroke motorbikes                  56.5      50.5       42.5         36.5           30.5
   QCVN 26:2010/BTNMT (6h-21h)                                         70




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                     226    Page
                                                                                                   Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
b) Noise of the transformers

458. Operating transformers of power lines generate mainly noise, about 75dB. However, the
noise level is small and has a negligible, intermittent and long term impact on people and
surrounding areas because few peole living near the tranformers and the transformer station
operates intermittently based on water supply needs of the locals.

4.5.1.3. Domestic Wastewater and Solid Wastes

459. Experiences from similar projects in Kien Giang (now An Giang) province, the operation
and maintenance of each sluice generates 1kg/month of oily rags. Therefore, the operation and
maintenance of the 41 sluices (23 newly built sluices and 18 improved sluices) may generate
oily rags with a maximum volume of approximately 41 kg/month and almost no domestic waste
of any type.
460. The hazardous waste will have two primary effects if it is not collected correctly. First
and foremost, the oil and grease will make their way into the water, thereby directly affecting
aquatic species in the canals where the maintenance is conducted, including Canal 200, Don
Dong canal, Tuan Thong canal, the canal along the sea dike. This has the potential to cause the
death of aquatic animals and vegetation, as well as impede their growth. Secondly, the existence
of heavy metal elements in the waste will result in the contamination of the food chain, which
poses a significant threat to human health.
461. However, the waste will be collected, transported and treated by relevant units. Therefore,
the impacts is negligible and only occurring during the maintenance time and can be managed
easily.

4.5.1.4. Increase in traffic accidents

462. When the bridges and the connecting road are in use, road safety is an essential concern
due to an increase in the number of vehicles on the road. Accidents, injuries, or deaths that
happen because of actions on the road, at the project sites. The environment, living organisms
and human health are at risk in the event of an accidental leak involving oil, fuel, or any number
of other dangerous compounds. However, this risk is minimal since the number of vehicles will
only increase by approximately 20% compared to the pre-project levels. n addition, before the
operation, the Division of Water Resources and the Transportation Agency will develop an
Emergency Contingency Plan that will address situations involving people, probable spills of
oil, diesel, and hazardous materials and their cleanup, medical evacuation, etc. This helps to
reduce risk and accidents while also allowing for quick and professional response in the event
of an accident. Efforts have been made throughout the design of the project to incorporate all
road safety infrastructure (lights, signs, etc.) to reduce road safety risks.

4.5.1.5. Damage to traffic roads on the bridges over the sluices

463. The causes of these incidents can be the following: (i) Quality of construction work does
not meet technical requirements; (ii) Maintenance and protection of the road and bridges and
are not well carried out; (iii) Due to direct impacts of extreme hydrometeorological such as
large rainstorm, heavy flooding combined with storm, etc.
464. When the incident occurs, it will have a significant impact on the lives of those who use
the road and bridges, as well as the locals who live nearby. In addition, the road and bridges


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                227   Page
                                                                                             Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
will be damaged. However, the impact is small because (i) the CSC and PPMU will carefully
inspect the road and bridges during construction; (ii) the operation units will maintain the road
and bridges on a regular basis; (iii) local authorities will raise awareness of the protection of
the bridges and roads; and (iv) repairs will be carried out immediately following the incident.

4.5.2.    Site-specific impacts

4.5.2.1. Site-specific impacts due to the operation of the road

a)       Impacts on water drainage

465. Water drainage: Currently, most of the households along the connecting road are at the
same level as the current road. Once the road is constructed, the road surface will be elevated
about 20-30cm higher than the ground level of the houses. This will affect access to the houses
as well as drainage and local flooding during heavy rains. However, drainage culverts will be
installed along the road, and since there are fields behind the houses, water can drain into the
fields or drainage culverts. Therefore, this impact is considered minor.

b)       Risk of the road damages

466. The causes of the risk can be the following: (i) Quality of construction work does not
meet technical requirements; (ii) Maintenance and protection of the road are not well carried
out; (iii) Due to direct impacts of extreme hydrometeorological such as large rainstorm, heavy
flooding combined with storm, etc.
467. When the incident occurs, it will have a significant impact on the lives of those who use
the road, as well as the locals who live nearby. In addition, the road will be damaged. However,
the impact is small because (i) the CSC and PPMU will carefully inspect the road during
construction; (ii) the operation units will maintain the road a regular basis; (iii) local authorities
will raise awareness of the protection of the road; and (iv) repairs will be carried out
immediately following the incident.

4.5.2.2. Site-specific impacts due to operation of the power lines

468. Impacts due to electromagnetic field: The power line's high-voltage conductors will
generate electromagnetic fields, resulting in power outages, air pollution, noise, and an impact
on electronic equipment and human health in the project area. Interference and signal disruption
can impact the functionality of transmission devices, wireless equipment, and radios.
Individuals residing within the electromagnetic field zone may encounter impacts on their
nervous and circulatory systems, which can potentially lead to the development of both chronic
and acute hazardous diseases. However, the impact is small and short term because (i) the
standard of electromagnetic field will strictly follow during the installation and the operation
of the power lines and (ii) the operation unit will be maintance the power lines on a regular
basis.

4.5.2.3. Site-specific impacts due to operation of the sluices

a) Degradation of Surface Water Quality

469. Impacts due to the transition from the rice to rice-shrimp model: In the rice-shrimp
farming system, in the dry season, when the canal water is saline, it is used for shrimp farming.

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                    228   Page
                                                                                                 Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
When the rainy season brings fresh water, it is used for rice cultivation. After one shrimp crop
is harvested, a rice crop is planted. The organic waste from the bottom of the field after the
shrimp harvest makes the field more fertile, allowing farmers to apply only a small amount of
fertilizer to meet the crop's needs. Moreover, to prevent negative impacts on the shrimp, farmers
apply integrated disease prevention measures, reducing the use of pesticides by 70-80%.
Conversely, when shrimp farming follows a rice crop, the pond bottom has undergone
mineralization, reducing harmful substances and preventing shrimp farming areas from
deteriorating due to long-term salinity in the soil. This process also helps eliminate pathogens
in the field, stabilizes the environment, reduces the need for chemicals and medicines in shrimp
farming, cuts production costs, and increases profits [Directorate of Fisheries, 2015] 15 . In
addition, farmers will have access to training and demonstration models that will help them
reduce their chemical usage and manage water quality during farming. So, instead of getting
worse, the water quality will get better.

b) Impacts of saline and/or brackish water on construction works

470. Given that the 23 sluices will be built at the canals which are impacted by salinity,
saline/brackish water will impact construction materials. This results in the structures'
corrosion, which in turn reduces their lifespan and impacts the water intake for production. The
impact is negligible since salt-tolerant construction materials are chosen in the design stage of
the project.

c) Damage to sluice gates

471. As analyzed above, risks and incidents that may arise are the concerns of the project.
Risks and incidents happening during operation are mainly derived from subjective causes;
sometimes there are objective reasons. The reasons that might lead to risks and incidents are:
(i) Technical quality of the works is not guaranteed from the beginning; (ii) Operation of these
sluice gates does not follow the regulations; (iii) Regular maintenance activities do not comply
with regulations; (iv) People lacking awareness of the protection of the works.
472. Damage incidents to sluice gates are among the most serious incidents in terms of the
environment and may cause adverse impacts on the ecosystem and socio-economic activities
in the area affected by the incident. If a sluicegate is damaged, freshwater will quickly enter
into shrimp ponds, leading to severe consequences, including shrimp death. Therefore, the
impacts is moderate and short term because (i) the CSC and PPMU will carefully inspect the
sluices during construction; (ii) the operation units will maintain the sluices a regular basis; (iii)
local authorities will raise awareness of the protection of the sluice; and (iv) repairs will be
carried out immediately following the incident.

d) Increase in water use conflict

473. Constructing sluices will promote aquaculture development. Aquaculture can use a huge
quantity of water for production while discharging its wastes causing water polluted, which
leads to a shortage of water for other users. This will become more serious under effects of the
climate change with an increased temperature and sea level rise. Impacts are assessed below:

15

d.1). Impacts on freshwater supply for agricultural production
475. The completion of the project will lead to a shift from 2-rice-crop to shrimp-rice. During
the dry season with high salinity, aquaculture is practiced, while rice cultivation takes place
during the rainy season. However, in the region, there is a period of flood drainage (September
and October), so farmers start rice cultivation in November, concluding it by February. In April,
when salinity increases, farmers commence shrimp farming, concluding the shrimp farming
season by the end of August. Therefore, from early November of the previous year to the end
of February of the following year, farmers need fresh water for rice cultivation. In March, fresh
water is drained and saltwater is taken for aquaculture.
476. According to the simulation results, the total flow through the freshwater supply canals
(Tuan Thong, Vam Ray, No. 3, No. 6, No. 9) is 3 to 4 times greater than the region’s freshwater
demand. The water level in the area steadily declines from 1.1m (November) to 0.2m (March)
throughout the period when freshwater is required for the Winter-Spring rice crop (November
of the previous year to early March of the following year). Farmers can readily deliver water to
their fields at this water level.
477. As a result, if finished, the project will ensure a fresh water supply for rice production
during the transition from the 2-rice-crop model to the shrimp-rice model, even in years of low
water availability, the impact will be negligible and end when the entire 5,500ha of rice land is
transformed to shrimp-rice model.

d.2). Impacts on saline/brackish water supply for shrimp season in the transitioned model
478. When the project is finished, the production model north of the Don Dong Canal will shift
to a shrimp-rice model. As previously indicated, from March 15 to August 31, irrigation works
in the project area will be run as follows to supply saline water for aquaculture in both the south
and north of Don Dong canal:
                    The sluices of Kenh Hai, Kenh Bon, Kenh Nam, Kenh Bay, Kenh Tam, and Kenh Muoi: are
                     open to supply saline/brackish water for the project area.


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                                      230 P a g e
                                                                                                                    Confidential
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
    Sluices for flood water drainage, including Kenh Nam (Tuan Thong), Kenh Ba, Kenh Sau,
     Kenh Chin, and Vam Ray: operated by the operational procedure of the LXQ irrigation
     system (closed when Chau Doc station’s water level lower than 2.5m and open when Chau
     Doc station’s water level higher than 2.5m).
    Existing sluices that separate saline and freshwater along Don Dong canal: open to supply
     saline/brackish water.
    Sluices that separate saline and freshwater along the canals of Tuan Thong (Vo Van Kiet
     canal), No.3, No.6, No.9, and Vam Ray: are closed to prevent salinity intrusion for the
     desalination area.
479. The following is the calculation results of the ability to supply saline/brackish water when
the project is finished up to 2030 using hydrological data from the low water year (year 2016):
    Salinity: The salt concentration in the project area is between 18 and 22 g/L (Figure 84) at
     the start of the shrimp crop, which is adequate for shrimp release. The salinity in April ranges
     between 20 and 30g/L, and with this salinity, water is required to dilute before providing
     shrimp. A network of alternating freshwater canals in the area, including the Vam Ray canal,
     Tuan Thong canal, Canal 3, and Canal 6, makes it simple to obtain fresh water for shirmp
     salinity reduction. The salinity of the canals reduces from June to August due to a lot of
     rainwater, ranging from 2 to 10 g/L (Figure 85). As a result, care should be taken to drain
     rainwater from fields to maintain salt in the pond for shrimp growth.
    Water level: The water level in the north of Don Dong canal is as high as the water level in
     the south of Don Dong canal and depends mainly on West Sea tides. In years of low water
     levels, such as 2016, the water level during low tides is very low, therefore the period for
     taking saline water is approximately 10 to 11 hours per day in both the north and south of
     the Don Dong canal (Figure 86).
    Saline water flow: A comparison between the inflow of saline/brackish water through sluices
     (such as Kenh Hai, Kenh Tu + Kenh Nam, Kenh Bay + Kenh Tam, and Kenh Muoi) and the
     demand for such water reveals that the inflow of saline/brackish water through the sluices is
     significantly greater than the demand for salty/brackish water of their serving (Figure 87 to
     Figure 90).

480. Thus, saline or brackish water supply for aquaculture in the project area during the dry
season will be ensured upon completion of the project. However, precipitation may fall during
shrimp crop; in such cases, the project operation unit shall direct farmers to drain rainwater
from the fields to maintain sufficient salinity in the pond for shrimp growth. Therefore, the
impact is small and occurs from March 15 to August 31.

e) Impacts on aquatic life

e.1). Impacts on biodiversity
481. For most projects of sluice gate construction, given that alteration of water quality and
water flow regime due to the closing of sluice in a long time would result in significant impacts
on aquatic creatures (changing from freshwater to saline/brackish water and from open to closed
ecological systems).
482. Currently, the project area is a freshwater ecology, and the predominant fish species in
the region are freshwater fish. When the sluices are constructed, the area becomes a brackish


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                                      234 P a g e
                                                                                                                    Confidential
                     ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
and freshwater ecology, creating favorable conditions for the development of both brackish and
saltwater species. Therefore, the impact on biodiversity is considered positive and long term.
e.2). Temporary blockage of fish passage
483. When closing the sluice gates, fish can not traverse from the main rivers or the West Sea
to the project area and vice versa. However, sluice gates will be closed in a short time so the
impact on fish passage is low.
484. Given that turbulence appears, and ebb flow velocities exceed the maximum sustainable
swimming capacities of aquatic species can obstruct the smooth passage of them through the
gate. These velocities can be easily calculated from empirical formulae using estimates of the
mean length and weight of sampled fish immigrating during the ebb flow period 16:




Where: Ums = maximum sustainable swimming speed; L = mean length of migrating fish;
       W = mean weight of migrating fish; n = constant = 3.
485. Using this formula, the optimistic velocity for aquatic species from the main rivers
(migrate into the project area should be: (i) from 0.2 to 0.30 m/s for small-sized shrimp and
fish; (ii) from 0.4 to 0.6 m/s for medium-sized fish (sizes 6 to 25 cm); (iv) from 0.6 to 1.2 m/s
for large-sized fish (size over 25 cm); and (v) above 1.2 m/s, fish can not migrate from the main
rivers into the project area.
486. Calculated velocities of the sluices with proposed dimensions show that velocities
fluctuate from 0.15 - 1.2m/s even though maximum velocities at the 23 sluice gates are always
lower than 1.2m/s. This means that the operation of 23 sluice gates will ensure the smooth
passage of fish from the main rivers into the project area. Therefore, operation of the sluices
will not impact on fish passage.

f) Impacts on waterway where the sluices are built

487. The canals where the sluices to be built are not designated as waterways, hence there are
no special waterway requirements in place. However, there are still a few local boats moving
agricultural and aquatic products along the canals, particularly during harvest season. As a
result, the sluices’ smaller size than those of the largest boats, as well as their closure, will have
an impact on local people’s movements.
488. As mentioned Section 4.4.3.2, on the canals Don Dong, No.200, No. 2, and the horizontal
canal where sluices will be built, the majority of vessels are small boats weighing around 50
tons (Figure 76), primarily used for transporting agricultural materials and products. However,
the density of boat traffic is low, averaging only 5-10 boats per day, but increases during periods
when materials and outputs must be transported for production or harvest seasons. Although
the amount is not large, these canals are also utilized for conveying shrimp farming materials
and products. In order to provide water for shrimp farming, the sluices will be closed once they
are operational. Closing the sluices will impact waterway of the local people.
489. However, the impact is small and short term (during the sluice closing) and can be
mitigated because (i) the selected sluice dimensions are the greatest in the area; (ii) the


16
     Fisheries Assessment and Data Collection Methodologies, MRAG Ltd, April 2003

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                    235   Page
                                                                                                 Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
operational procedures for the sluices and alternative canal routes will be consulted with
locals; (iii) local people and authorities will be informed at least two weeks before they are put
into action; (iv) the canals where the sluices are constructed are primarily used during the rice
season but the sluices only close during shrimp season; and (v) when the sluice gates are closed,
there are multiple options for boat transportation within the project area (Figure 91).




Figure 91: A drawbridge "carrying" boats and ships over sluices and dams

g) Impacts on flood drainage

490. The LXQ irrigation system, in addition to the task of directing water, storing fresh water,
and regulating saltwater intrusion, also has the task of draining floods for 498,141 hectares.
This project area belongs to the LXQ irrigation system, so it is also responsible for draining
rain/flood water. Therefore, operating 23 sluices of the project will likely impact the flood
drainage ability of the region. Mathematical modeling is used to evaluate this impact.
491. The rainy season in the project area starts in May and lasts until the end of November.
The flood season on the Hau River starts in June and ends at the end of November. Thus,
rain/flood drainage needs to be considered from May until the end of November.
492. According to measured data of the big flood year 2011 at Chau Doc station, from January
to July the water level at Chau Doc station is always less than 2m. Since the end of July, the
water level at the station exceeded 2m. By early August, the water level rose to 2.3m and
continued to rise rapidly to over 2.5m and peaked at 4.27m on October 12, 2011. Then the water
level tended to decrease and by the end of November, the water level dropped to 2.4m and
tended to decrease quickly (Figure 92). Hence, in the event of big floods (comparable to the
2011 flood, with a rainfall frequency of P10% at Rach Gia station and rainfall at other stations
matching the 2011 data), the LXQ irrigation system will operate as follows:
    From the beginning of May to the end of July:
       During this period, the water level at Chau Doc station is less than 2.5m, so the flood
       control sluices of the LXQ are closed to keep fresh water.
       The sluices that separate saline and fresh water in the project area are close to prevent
       salinity intrusion in the freshwater production area.
       The saltwater intake sluices in the project area are open to supply saltwater for
       aquaculture.
    In August:


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                236   Page
                                                                                             Confidential
                               ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
                    During this period, the water level at Chau Doc station is higher than 2.5m, so the flood
                    control sluices of the LXQ are sealed in one direction to drain flood water.
                    The sluices that separate saline and fresh water in the project area are close to prevent
                    salinity intrusion into the freshwater production area.
                    The saltwater intake sluices in the project area are open to supply saltwater for
                    aquaculture. If there is a risk of flooding due to heavy rain, these sluices will be sealed in
                    one direction to drain flood water. Once the water has been drained, the sluices will be
                    open freely.
         From September to the end of November:
                    During this period, the water level at Chau Doc station is higher than 2.5m, so the flood
                    control sluices of the LXQ are sealed in one direction to drain flood water.
                    The sluices that separate saline and fresh water in the project area are open freely to drain
                    flood water.
                    The saltwater intake sluices in the project area are sealed in one direction to drain flood
                    water.
                    If freshwater is required in a specific area during this time, the adjacent sluices will be
                    closed to facilitate the supply of freshwater. Once the freshwater requirements are
                    fulfilled, the sluices will be closed in one direction to facilitate the drainage of flood water.

flood corresponding to the big flood year of 2011 and precipitation at the Rach Gia station with
a 10% frequency; other areas with precipitation corresponding to the year of 2011 (see Figure
20 to Figure 26):
         From June to the end of July, as the flood flow on the Hau River increases, the water level
          at the Chau Doc station remains below 2.5 m, while precipitation in the LXQ also
          experiences an upward trend. To maintain freshwater for the LXQ's production, the flood
          control sluices remain closed during this period. Consequently, the water level in the LXQ

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                                   237 P a g e
                                                                                                                 Confidential
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
     region experienced a substantial increase, particularly in the drainage canals situated within
     the project area (including Tuan Thong, No. 3, No. 6, No. 9, and Vam Ray canals). The
     water level on these canals, in particular, reaches 1.30m by the end of July. The water level
     on the canals delivering brackish/saline water for aquaculture, on the other hand, is not high,
     with the highest water level reaching just +0.76m. Because sluices for brackish/saline water
     supply, such as those at Kenh Hai, Kenh Bon, Kenh Nam, Kenh Bay, Kenh Tam, and Kenh
     Muoi, operate freely in both directions, the water level in the aquaculture area is determined
     solely by the West Sea tide and local rainfall, rather than the upstream flood.
    From August to the end of November: Because the water level at Chau Doc station is more
     than 2.5m, the LXQ flood control sluices open one way to drain floodwater. The sluices for
     taking water for aquaculture remain open until the end of August, and they are closed in one
     direction to drain flood water from September. The water level in the project area at that
     time varies only between -0.2 and 0.9m. Thus, even in a big flood year like 2011, with a rain
     frequency of 10%, the LXQ's flood drainage capacity is still met after the project is
     completed.
494. Furthermore, the project area is arranged in the form of a semi-dike between flood
drainage canals, so they will not have an impact on the flood drainage canals and sluices of
the LXQ in general, or the project area in particular.
