h) Canalbank Erosion

495. The construction of a sluice, which reduces the width of the canal, may result in erosion
occurring both upstream and downstream. This phenomenon arises when the narrowing of a
canal or river surpasses 50% of its cross-section, according to construction experience with
comparable sluices in the MD. To mitigate this, it is advisable to design sluices with water
passage areas that fall within the range of 50% to 80%. For this project, the sluice passages
were narrowed within this range (Figure 82). As a result, the risk of erosion is assessed to be
low and is mitigated via upstream and downstream reinforcement measures in Chapter 5.

i) Risk due to the failure of livelihood models adversely impacting welfare of beneficiary
   households

496. As discussed above alien and artificial species may be released into the environment from
the model of raising engineered species provided by fishery research centers. The released alien
and engineered species can interact with wild aquatic species not only affecting aquatic
biodiversity but also potentially causing diseases to wild aquatic animals. Although the
measures are proposed to address such risk mentioned in Chapter 5, failure of the livelihood
models of applying such engineered species for farming may occur due to flooding, damage of
sluices, or discharge of wastewater from such models into open water sources during cultivation
and after harvesting. Once pathogens transmitting through water supply system, a disease
outbreak will be unavoidable in a wide area of cultivation in which cultivators have used the
same water sources for farming. Removal of pathogens out of a water source is impossible
within a short time, which can disrupt the livelihood activities of the communities where the
livelihood model is applied. To prevent such risk, the livelihood model of nursing or farming-
engineered shrimp should be planned in separate areas and using confined water sources. In
case an incident occurs, sterilization of the whole water source can be applied to clean the
pathogen to prevent it from a wide transmission.

j) Farmers’ resistance to adapting to livelihood models

497. It is noted that most shrimp farming activities are implemented by small-scale households
(about 2,650 households). The investment for infrastructures and farming technology for
shrimp farming is different depending on such households’ capacities and financial sources. As
a result, farmers who have raised or planned to farm shrimp in the project-planned area of
aquaculture may refuse to apply the livelihood models because probably their capacities will
not meet the requirements, or they have not found the confirmed benefits brought from the
livelihood models. This can also result in a risk of disease outbreak due to uncontrol of sources
of seed, water quality as well as farming technology and waste treatment during farming.
498. The experience of shifting from two rice crops per year to a rice-shrimp rotation in the
former districts of An Minh and An Bien in the province of Kien Giang (now An Giang) reveals
that farmers, especially those from lower socioeconomic backgrounds, were initially terrified
of shrimp farming due to the widespread belief that it posed significant economic risks.
However, within a short time, the shrimp-rice paradigm revealed its benefits. Along with
attempts to share the model's success, farming techniques, and local solutions to assist locate
markets, the people in these places bravely accepted the shift, which has now been nearly ten
years. As a result, these solutions will be implemented throughout the project area, and the shift
to this model will most likely be joyfully welcomed by the people, just as it was in An Minh


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                242   Page
                                                                                             Confidential
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
and An Bien. Therefore, the impacts will be small, short-term, and mitigable by measures in
Chapter 5.

k) Risk of soil salinization

499. The agricultural land adjacent to shrimp farms may face risks of salinization due to the
saline water for the shrimp farm can permeate through its banks to affect the freshwater sources
and land that have been used for crops and trees. The communities may also face to water
contamination and be exposed to water-borne diseases, insect-borne diseases and downing
possibly resulting from expanding aquaculture farming. However, shrimp farmers will be
requested to embank their shrimp ponds higher than their neighbor’s fields to (i) form a physical
division between agriculture and aquaculture; (ii) appropriately discharge wastewater into the
receiving waters. In addition, the project owner will conduct consultation meetings about the
transition plan of rice cultivation farmers to avoid conflicts of interest when agricultural land is
transferred to aquaculture production. Therefore, the risk is small and can be mitigated by
measures in Chapter 5.
500. The construction of sluices, power lines, and road promotes the transition of livelihood
models from rice cultivation to shrimp-rice integrated. The transition will not change land use
purposes. If the transition occurs, salinization of the sediment will occur. This will impact
productivity of rice of the next crop. However, shrimp farmers will be trained on how to leach
the salinity after the shrimp crop in accordance to TCVN 9167:2012 - Hydraulic structure -
Saline soil - Saline leaching process. Furthermore, the project area is located near the Hau River,
so there is the possibility of having an abundant source of fresh water to wash away the salinity.
Therefore, the impact is small and can be mitigated by measures in Chapter 5.

4.6. CUMULATIVE IMPACTS
501. This section discusses the cumulative impacts of the project. In this regard, the cumulative
impact under consideration is defined as two or more individual effects that, when considered
together, are considerable or which compound or increase other environmental impacts. The
cumulative impact from several projects results from the incremental impacts of the proposed
project when added to other closely related, and reasonably foreseeable, future projects. The
impacts that do not result in part from the project will not be discussed.
502. However, in the project area, there is no on-going project or planned project that cause
cumulative impacts with this project.

4.7. INDUCED IMPACTS
503. The induced impacts can be considered as project activities that occur as driving forces
for other activities to be developed or implemented, or that create environmental and social
change trends in the project area such as changing land use status, promoting climate change
mitigation, or lowering public healthcare costs, etc.
504. Economic and social development: The construction of sluices, power lines, and road
promotes the economic and social aspects in the project area, such as:
    The sluice system: In addition to improving control over salinity and tides, existing sluices,
     along with the 23 sluices in the project, will make it easier to deal with climate change, rising
     sea levels, and the division of freshwater from saltwater. Furthermore, they will be


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                    243   Page
                                                                                                 Confidential
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
     responsible for flushing acid sulfate, ensuring flood drainage in the LXQ region, and
     supplying fresh and salt water to the restructuring production zone, as well as dividing the
     area into freshwater and saltwater zones. In addition, during the dry season, they will control
     the flow of water so that it may be used for residential purposes, agricultural and aquaculture
     purposes, rural sanitation, and to encourage agricultural and aquaculture development as
     well as socioeconomic integration. Aside from that, they will govern the use of water for
     shrimp farming and aid in disease management across 5,500 hectares of aquaculture, which
     will significantly alter the agricultural land layout south of NH 80.
    The project road connecting NH80 with the West Sea dike will form a transportation route,
     facilitating the movement of seed stock, feed, equipment, as well as the exchange and trade
     of shrimp and other seafood products for aquaculture farmers in Binh Giang and Binh Son
     communes. Production costs for the farmers will decrease as they no longer need to rely on
     manual or semi-mechanical transportation (currently, most transportation is done by boats
     and canoes from irrigation canals or by basic vehicles such as tricycles, small trucks, and
     motorbikes on dirt roads along canal banks to collection points near NH 80 or the West Sea
     dike). The transportation time for shrimp and other seafood products will be shortened,
     contributing to improved seafood quality.
    The medium and low-voltage electricity systems will help solve the long-standing issue of
     electricity shortages in the aquaculture area in Binh Giang and Binh Son communes. Shrimp
     farmers will be able to use electric-powered machines and equipment, which are both
     efficient and economical, and will reduce pollution in farming environments caused by oil
     and grease when using diesel-powered equipment (due to the lack of electricity for
     production). Aquaculture production costs will decrease as diesel fuel is no longer needed,
     leading to increased income and improved livelihoods for aquaculture farmers.
    Changes land use:
      When the project is completed, 52 hectares of rice farming land will be converted to a
       shrimp-rice farming model, according to the FS report of the project. This model is
       deemed more effective commercially, socially, and environmentally, hence improving
       people's livelihoods. The transition will not change land use purposes.
      The construction of the road promotes economic and social development along its route,
       which also contributes to the conversion of land use purposes. However, the areas along
       the road have been planned for residential areas. So there is no significant impact but
       long term and can be mitigated by measures in Chapter 5.
505. Social impacts on vulnerable people. According to surveyed data, there are 2,650
households in the project area (only calculated for 5,500ha of the project area), including 553
EM households and 2,097 Kinh households. The project is projected to benefit 2,650HHs.
However, the poor (190 HHs) and EM (553HHs) may be left behind in terms of income
generation because raising shrimp under semi-intensive, intensive, and super-intensive models
requires large budgets, good experience, and capacity to capture the transferred high
technology, while the poor and EM often lack such resources. However, the EMDP has been
developed to address impacts of the project on EMs. Besides, the poor HHs will be supported
by non-strutural activities of the project and agricultural and fishery extension policies and
social policies of the province. Therefore, the impacts will be small, long term and can be
mitigated.



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                  244   Page
                                                                                               Confidential
                     ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
506. In addition, to mitigate the damage caused by climate change and sea level rise, the
province of Kien Giang (now An Giang) has reviewed and supplemented the provincial socio-
economic development plans with adaptation strategies for each sector and agency in the
province. The project components were developed per the plans.

4.8. GREENHOUSE GAS EMISSIONS

507. During the construction, the project will generate mostly greenhouse gas (GHG)
emissions by using fossil fuels. The most common fossil fuel used for construction equipment
in Vietnam is Diesel Oil (DO). The GHG emissions from the burning of diesel on construction
equipment are calculated in Table 88 using the estimated diesel fuel demand and the 2014
Emission Factors for Greenhouse Gas Inventories from the United States Environmental
Protection Agency (EPA)17. Furthermore, as stated in the Greenhouse Gas Inventories, it is
customary to express greenhouse gas emissions in terms of the carbon dioxide equivalent
(CO2e) unit. To convert gases into CO2e, their global warming potential (GWP) is multiplied
by the gases. The emission factors calculated for the project (Table 88) have not been converted
to carbon dioxide equivalents (CO2e). To do so, multiply the emissions by the corresponding
GWP listed in Table 89. The total GHG emission during 3 years of construction for the project
is estimated as minor equivalent to 87.03 COeq (tons).
Table 88: CO2, N2O and CH4 emission for diesel-used vehicles and equipment

508. During operation, GHGs will be generated as a result of the utilization of fossil fuels,
such as diesel fuel, gasoline, and biodiesel, by vehicles that travel on the 7.03 km project road.
The calculation of GHGs is technically challenging due to the diverse types of fuels used and
the number and varieties of vehicles on the road. Nevertheless, it is estimated to be small due
to the fact that the road is a rural one that is serviced for motorbikes, small cars, and light trucks,

which are small vehicles that utilize a significantly lower volume of fossil fuel than medium
and large vehicles. In addition, solar power will be used for lightening duringRe the operation
of the project road.

4.9. DETAILS AND RELIABILITY OF THE ABOVE ASSESSMENTS

4.9.1.     Assessment of Reliability of Methods Applied in the ESIA

    Statistical and comparative methods: In completing the ESIA, the environment team has
     taken many observations, and done data collection in the project corridor. Those data have
     been updated and include time series of years. Therefore, such methods could give reliable
     and accurate results.
    Field observation, measurement and sampling, sample analysis in the laboratory, and social
     survey are methods used in this ESIA. Personnel who are trained, experienced, and have
     worked with such techniques for years, conducted the evaluations. The data from such
     methods could ensure accuracy and reliability about environmental quality.
    Environmental rapid assessment method: the application follows the guideline of WHO in
     quantifying pollutants that depends on pollutant coefficients. This method could quickly give
     outputs that are used for other assessments.
    Integrated analysis and assessment methods: this method was used to synthesize
     environmental impacts caused by the project. Based on the projected impacts, mitigation
     measures were proposed. Although these methods are subjective assessments it is still
     reliable if it is done by environmental experts who are experienced with those impacts. The
     impacts will be assessed depending on the realities of local contexts as well as project
     designs before proposed mitigation measures that should be appropriate and feasible.
    The characteristics of impacts were identified according to the experiences of experts,
     secondary data as well as quantification of impacts that are caused by project activities. The
     impacts were quantified through secondary sources, including emission coefficients from
     reliable sources such as Assessment of Sources of Air, Water and Land Pollution by WHO
     (1993), and Environmental Impact Assessment by Larry W. Canter (1997).
    Determining the spatial scale and time scale of impacts that included expert consultation and
     analyzing the current characteristics of the system in terms of scale, scope, and construction
     plans in consideration of local climate conditions, infrastructures, as well as environmental
     management regulations.
    Impact assessments considered the spatial and temporal scale of impacts as well as the
     sensitivity of recipients. The actual experiences are very important in assessing the impacts
     and proposed mitigation measures. It requires an understanding of local social and
     environmental context as well as local environmental regulations.

4.9.2.     Reliability of the Assessment

    All impact assessments were conducted by experienced experts as well as useful tools that
     give reliable and accurate results. Impact type and impact scope were assessed for different
     phases of project implementation so that they reflect reality. Therefore, the project owner
     has committed to mitigate negative impacts as well as pollution control, which are detailed
     in later sections.


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 246   Page
                                                                                              Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
    The quantifying pollution is assessed based on the fuel consumption and emission norms
     documents. For example, the application of emission norms of vehicle WHO 1993; or
     calculation of noise level applied guideline of Pham Ngoc Dang 1997.
    The assessments were analyzed by experts who have experience in practices and scientific
     research as well as participate in many ESIAs therefore their opinions or analyses and
     solutions are determined.

CHAPTER 5. ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL MANAGEMENT
           PLAN
509. Based on the negative impacts and risks discussed in Chapter 4, this Chapter presents the
Environmental and social management plan (ESMP) for the project. This ESMP covers the
following sections:
     a. Mitigation measures proposed for FS and detailed design, pre-construction, construction,
        and operation phases along with implementation responsibilities for each investment
        item;
     b. Compliance monitoring and environmental quality monitoring program;
     c. E&S compliance framework;
     d. Capacity building and training programs;
     e. Cost estimates;
     f. Grievance redress mechanism (GRM);
     g. Subplans include the Labor management procedures (LMP) and stakeholder engagement
        plan (SEP), see details in the annexes.
510. In addition, the Resettlement Plan (RP) is also closely related to this ESMP and cross-
referenced in the ESIA/ESMP.

5.1. MITIGATION MEASURES FOR IMPACTS AND RISKS OF THE
     PROJECT

5.1.1.    General principles

511. This section identifies mitigation measures for the project impacts and risks during the
pre-construction, construction (including measures integrated into detailed technical design,
site clearance, ground leveling, construction, and restoration), and operation phases.
512. This section focuses on prosing appropriate mitigation measures to prevent, avoid, or
mitigate the negative impacts and risks on human and natural environment in the project area
of influence.
513. Based on the negative impacts and risks identified and analyzed in Chapter 4, the
mitigation measures have been proposed for three phases of project implementation i.e. pre-
construction, construction, and operation. A hierarchy approach has been adopted during the
development of mitigation measures:
    Anticipate and avoid risks and impacts;
    Where avoidance is not possible, minimize or reduce risks and impacts to acceptable levels;
    Once risks and impacts have been minimized or reduced, mitigated, where significant
     residual impacts remain, compensate for or offset them, where technically and financially
     feasible.
514. Given that most of the key impacts and risks will occur due to structural components and
transportation of construction materials and waste, labor influx, many of the potential negative
impacts and risks on the physical, biological, and social environment could be mitigated through
a set of general measures that are typically applied to most of the construction projects to


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               248   Page
                                                                                            Confidential
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
minimize impacts such as noise, dust, water, waste, etc. Since there are specific impacts, this
also addresses the measures both during the construction and operation phases.

5.1.2.     Measures to be integrated into the feasibility study and detailed design

515. Project design including detailed design could produce long-term opportunities to cause
negative impacts and risks on natural and socio-economic environments if environmental
factors are not adequately considered. Typically, adverse impacts relating to project design
include land acquisition and resettlement, encroachment on the ecosystem, visual impacts (i.e.
landscape disfiguration), localized flooding, and other risks. Some of these impacts have been
mitigated by careful surveys, calculations, and consultations with local governments in the
conduct of the preliminary design. Key measures implemented at the preliminary design stage
to minimize the project's social and environmental impacts and risks included the following:
516. For all works: During the detailed engineering and preparation of bid documents, the
PPMU and detailed design consultants shall update and include relevant generic measures
(specified in Tables 5.1 of the ESIA) and site-specific mitigation measures (as specified in
Table 5.2 of ESIA) to reflect the measures in the form of “Incidentals to work items” or
“specifications and bill of quantities” in case of works contracts.
517. For sluices:
    The aperture of the sluices has been calculated to maintain a specific flow velocity within
     the sluices to facilitate the movement of aquatic species from the main rivers into the
     project’s area. In particular, the ideal flow velocities vary according to the following criteria:
     0.2 to 0.30 m/s for small shrimps and prawns; 0.4 to 0.6 m/s for larger shrimps and small
     fish (sizes 2-6cm); 0.6 to 0.8 m/s for medium-sized fish (sizes 6-25cm); and 0.6 to 1.1 m/s
     for large fish (greater than 25cm). The fish are carried away by the water current when the
     velocity surpasses 1.1 m/s. When the velocity exceeds 1.1 m/s, the fish will be swept away
     by the water flow.
    The power source for illumination at the sluice’s traffic/work bridges and management
     houses has a minimum capacity of 100W and is used largely at night. Solar energy is thus
     proposed as a means of providing illumination at the sluices. This is a renewable energy
     source, emits no carbon emissions, and contributes to environmental protection.
    Salt-tolerant construction materials will be used for the construction of the 23 sluices and
     the improvement of the 18 sluices.
518. For the connecting road:
    The selected route is the one that minimally impacts houses and structures and requires the
     least relocation of residents.
    Similarly, the power source for illumination on the road has a minimum capacity of 100W
     and is used largely at night. Solar energy is thus proposed as a means of providing
     illumination on the road. This is a renewable energy source, emits no carbon emissions, and
     contributes to environmental protection.

5.1.4.    Measures for the construction phase

520. Below are the mitigation measures to be implemented during the construction phase of
the project and presented by category:
   Mitigation measures for generic impacts and risks are presented in the Environmental and
    Social Codes of Practices (ESCOPs).
   Site-specific mitigation measures to address site-specific potential impacts and risks.

521. Each bid package's bidding documents and construction contracts will include the
ESCOPs as well as site-specific mitigation measures that are compatible with the package. They
will be supervised by the CSC and the IMC.
522. The contractors will be required to prepare a site-specific environmental and social
management plan (C-ESMP) and submit it to the CSC, An Giang PPMU, and the Bank for
review and approval at least two weeks prior to construction commencement. The C-ESMP
will be prepared to meet the mitigation requirements described below.

5.1.4.1. Mitigation measures for generic impacts and risks

523. Generic impacts and risks defined in Chapter 4 are mitigated through measures identified
in the ESCOPs, including:
   1.    Impacts on ambient air quality including dust, air emission, noise and vibration
         generation;
   2.    Impacts on water quality;
   3.    Impacts of solid waste generation including construction, domestic and hazardous waste
   4.    Impacts on ecological environment;
   5.    Impacts on local traffic;
   6.    Risks of local flooding;
   7.    Risks of land subsidence and bank erosion during heavy rains
   8.    Risks from raw material supply;
   9.    Impacts on social aspects;
   10. Labor violence and hard-working conditions;
   11. Community and worker health and safety;
   12. Chance find procedures;
   13. Communication community.

                        leaked into the soil, drains or water sources. Use trays to hold rags and

                        persons.

                        contact with rainwater.

                        containers, store in safe places (on impermeable grounds, roofed, fenced and
                        with warning signs) for regular collection by licensed dealers.
                       On the construction site, containers of oil, lubricants, and hazardous waste
                        must be placed on sealed surfaces that are impermeable, preventing
                        stormwater from overflowing from the outside, with sun and rain shelters
                        covering the entire area for storing hazardous substances, ensuring no
                        spillage into the environment.
                       In areas with acid sulfate soil (pH < 3.5), lime powder will be used to to raise
                        the pH to above 5. Spread lime powder along the embankments surrounding
                        the soil storage of the sluice construction areas to reduce the impact of acid
                        sulfate runoff during rainfall (if any).
                       Collect and transport materials and wastes generated during excavation and
                        backfilling materials to as soon as possible.
                       Regularly inspect the excavation material storage areas, especially before the
                        rainy season, to assess and implement preventive measures against damages
                        caused by runoff, soil erosion, sedimentation, and acid sulfate leachage (if
                        any). Additionally, the construction sites will be restored upon project
                        completion.

                      Construction wastewater:
                       The Contractors are responsible for controlling surface water quality when
                        discharging it out of construction sites, in accordance with QCVN
                        08:2023/BTNMT and QCVN 14:2008/BTNMT;
                       For wastewater from routine machinery cleaning that is not oil-
                        contaminated, it will be treated by passing it through sedimentation tanks to
                        remove all sediments, such as soil and sand, before being discharged into the
                        environment. For wastewater from equipment maintenance that is oil-
                        contaminated, it will be treated by passing it through an oil and grease
                        separator before being discharged. The oil and grease will be collected into
                        containers and treated as hazardous waste
                       Provide sedimentation pits and ditches at big construction sites.

                       Wastewater and ballast water for construction material transportation boats
                        will be collected and treated by the boat owners to meet QCVN
                        17:2011/BGTVT and QCVN 10:2018/BGTVT. They are not allowed to be
                        discharged directly into the canals.
                       The contractors must be responsible for compliance with the relevant
                        Vietnamese regulations on wastewater discharges into surroundings.
                      Impacts on groundwater:
                          Only digging the electrical poles, sluice foundations and road bed to the
                           designated deep of the project

                       Recyclables (sellable waste), such as plastic bottles, glass, scrap paper,
                        plastic bags, etc. will be collected and sold to the informal sector on a regular
                        basis.
                       Sign a contract with the legal entity of the district to collect and treat the
                        residual waste (unsellable waste) in compliance with current regulations at a
                        minimum of 3 times per week. For construction sites where current
                        collection vehicles cannot reach, the project owner/contractor will arrange
                        for their workers to transport the waste to the nearest collection transfer point
                        for proper collection and treatment.
                      Construction solid waste:
                       Before construction, a solid waste control procedure (storage, provision of
                        bins, site clean-up schedule, bin clean-out schedule, etc.) must be prepared
                        by Contractors and it must be carefully followed during construction
                        activities.
                       Before construction, all necessary waste disposal permits or licenses must
                        be obtained.
                       Measures shall be taken to reduce the potential for litter and negligent
                        behavior with regard to the disposal of all refuse. At all places of work, the
                        Contractor shall provide litter bins, containers and refuse collection
                        facilities.
                       Trees and vegetation within the land acquisition area of the project will be
                        collected by local residents for sale and personal use. However, some
                        underbrush and tree branches may not be cleared by the residents; the
                        contractors will be responsible for cleaning up the remaining vegetation and
                        transporting it to the designated landfill in former Hon Dat District before
                        starting construction.
                       Excavated soil from the sluices is dried up and reused for backfilling the
                        construction ground, 2 banks of the sluice. Besides, excavated soil is reused
                        for the slopes of the road.
                       Solid waste may be temporarily stored on site in 240-L waste bins in a
                        designated area approved by the CSC and relevant local authorities prior to

                        collection and disposal through a licensed waste collector, for example, Hon
                        Dat URENCO.
                       Waste storage containers shall be covered, tip-proof, weatherproof and
                        scavenger proof.
                       No burning, on-site burying or dumping of solid waste shall occur.

                       Recyclable materials such as wooden plates for trench works, steel,
                        scaffolding material, site holding, packaging material, etc. shall be stored
                        on 240-L waste bins and collected and separated onsite from other waste
                        sources for reuse, for use as fill, or for sale.
                       If not removed off site, solid waste or construction debris shall be disposed
                        of only at sites identified and approved by the CSC and included in the solid
                        waste plan. Under no circumstances shall the contractor dispose of any
                        material in environmentally sensitive areas, such as in areas of natural habitat
                        or in watercourses.
                      Hazardous waste generation
                       Fuels and chemicals must be safely stored in areas with impermeable ground
                        with roofs and surrounding banks, equipped with safety warning signs
                        located at least 20m from the camps and at the end of prevailing winds;
                       Collect, store, and transported for treatment all hazardous wastes (road
                        asphalt, waste oil and grease, organic solvents, chemicals, oil paints, etc.) in
                        accordance with Circular No. 02/2022/TT-BTNMT on management of
                        hazardous waste.
                       Collect and temporarily store used oil and grease separately in specialized
                        containers and place in safe and fire-free areas with impermeable floors
                        roofs, at a safe distance from fire sources. Sign contracts with for oil and
                        grease to be delivered to suppliers/ manufacturers
                       Do not use unapproved toxic materials, including paint containing lead,
                        asbestos, etc.;
                       The removal of asbestos-containing materials or other toxic substances shall
                        be performed and disposed of by specially trained and certified workers.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                        260   Page
                                                                                                                                                         Confidential
                                                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
    E&S issues                                     Mitigation measures                                     Vietnam code/   Implementation Responsibility Supervision
                                                                                                            regulation          time
                       Used oil and grease shall be removed from site and sold to an approved used
                        oil recycling company.
                       Do not perform any maintenance (change of oil and filter) of construction
                        machinery and equipment outside the designated area.
                       Used oil, lubricants, cleaning materials, etc. from the maintenance of
                        vehicles and machinery shall be collected in holding tanks and removed from
                        site by n approved specialized oil recycling company for disposal.
                       Used oil or oil-contaminated materials that could potentially contain PCBs
                        shall be securely stored to avoid any leakage or affecting workers.
                       Unused or rejected tar or bituminous products shall be returned to the
                        supplier’s production plant.
                       Relevant agencies shall be promptly informed of any accidental spill or
                        incident
                       Store chemicals appropriately and with appropriate labelling

                       Appropriate communication and training programs should be put in place to
                        prepare workers to recognize and respond to workplace chemical hazards
                       Report all incidences of oil spills immediately and prevent any risks of oil
                        spills
                       Prepare and initiate a remedial action following any spill or incident. In this
                        case, the contractor shall provide a report explaining the reasons for the spill
                        or incident, remedial action taken, consequences/damage from the spill, and
                        proposed corrective actions.
                       In the event that accidental leakage or spillage of diesel/chemicals/chemical
                        wastes takes place, the following response procedures shall be followed
                        immediately by the Contractor(s):
                           The person who has identified the leakage/spillage shall immediately
                            check if anyone is injured and shall then inform the Contractor(s),
                            Supervision Engineer and PPMU;




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                        261   Page
                                                                                                                                                         Confidential
                                                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
    E&S issues                                     Mitigation measures                                      Vietnam code/   Implementation Responsibility Supervision
                                                                                                             regulation          time
                           The Contractor(s) shall ensure any injured persons are treated and assess
                            what has spilled/ leaked;
                           Should the accidents/incidents generate serious environmental pollution
                            (e.g. spillage/leakage of toxic or chemicals, large scale spillage / leakage,
                            or spillage/leakage into the nearby water bodies, the Contractor shall
                            immediately inform the PPMU;
                           In such cases, the Contractor(s) shall take immediate action to stop the
                            spillage/leakage and divert the spilled/leaked liquid to nearby non-
                            sensitive areas;
                           The Contractor(s) shall arrange maintenance staff with appropriate
                            protective clothing to clean up the chemicals/chemical waste. This may
                            be achieved through soaking with sawdust (if the quantity of
                            spillage/leakage is sand bags (if the quantity is large); and/or using a
                            shovel to remove the topsoil (if the spillage/leakage occurs on bare
                            ground); and
                           Depending on the nature and extent of the chemical spill, evacuation of
                            the activity site may be necessary;
                           Spilled chemicals must not be flushed to local surface drainage systems.
                            Instead, sawdust or sandbags used for clean-up and removed
                            contaminated soil shall be disposed of by following the procedures for
                            chemical waste handling and disposal already described;
                           The Contractor(s) shall prepare a report on the incident detailing the
                            accident, clean up actions taken, any pollution problems and suggested
                            measures to prevent similar accidents from happening again in future.
                            The incident report shall then be submitted to the Supervision Engineer
                            and PPMU for review and keep in the records. The incident report shall
                            also be submitted to Department of Agriculture and Environment, if
                            required.

 4. Impacts on        Terrestrial ecosystems:                                                           Law
                                                                                                       on Environment                                   
    ecological         Avoid disturbances and damage to the existing vegetation and green trees
                                                                                                 Protection       No.
    environment         outside of construction boundary.                                        72/2020/QH14
                       Do not load materials and wastes at places having vegetation cover. Load
                        them on barren land instead;
                       If trees can be replanted somewhere, move them instead of cutting the trees
                        down;
                       If any invasive species are found during construction phase, burn them
                        before disposed of to prevent them from regrowing at disposal site;
                       Chemicals must not be used to clear vegetation;

                       Minimize the areas disturbed, especially in locations having trees or
                        vegetation; Determine areas to be undisturbed for protection during
                        construction.
                       Do not remove or damage the vegetation without direct instructions.

                       Do not hurt, trap, feed or harm any animal - including birds, frogs, snakes,
                        etc.
                       Do not cut trees outside the approved construction area for any reason.

                       Do not buy any wildlife for food;

                       Birds and animals must not be kept in cages in camps.

                       No collection of firewood.

                       Do not burn litter that damages vegetation.

                       Limit disturbances to areas with construction operations, especially in
                        locations covered with green trees or vegetation.
                       Do not gather materials and wastes at places covered with vegetation or with
                        green trees, but on vacant land instead.
                       Trees cannot be cut down unless explicitly authorized in the vegetation
                        clearing plan.

                        used during construction.

                       The contractor will establish a transparent process for workers to express
                        concerns and file grievances, including anonymous complaints.
                       The contractor will ensure that there will be no retaliation or discrimination
                        against those that express grievances.
                       Management will treat the grievances seriously and take prompt, appropriate
                        action.
                       Any grievance mechanism will not replace other channels as defined by law
                        or collective bargaining agreements.
                      Child labor, school dropout and forced labor
                       The minimum age of project workers eligible for any jobs provided by the
                        Project (including construction works) is 18.
                       The contractor shall not employ child labor and forced labor, which consists
                        of any work or service, not voluntarily performed, that is exacted from an
                        individual under threat of force or penalty, and includes any kind of
                        involuntary or compulsory labor, such as indentured labor, bonded labor or
                        similar labor-contracting arrangements.
                       To prevent the participation of underage workers, all contracts must integrate
                        the provisions on worker’s age commitment, including penalties for non-
                        compliance. The contractor shall maintain a list of contracted workers with
                        their age.
                      GBV
                       Jobs under the project will be based on the gender equality and will have no
                        discrimination for any gender-related employment, recruitment, conditions
                        for recruitment (including jobs, salary and benefits), termination and access
                        to training courses.
                       In order to address the risk of excluding vulnerable groups (such as women
                        and people with disabilities) from employment opportunities, the
                        Contractors are required to recruit such groups as part of unskilled
                        employees.

                       Contractors will be required to comply with the National Law on gender

                        maternity and nursing leave; and restrooms and toilets must be separate from
                        men and women.
                       Contractors will also be requested to address potential sexual exploitation or
                        harassment issues in recruiting or keeping skilled or unskilled female
                        workers for supporting the project.

                        diseases especially HIV/AIDS within 2 weeks prior to the commencement
                        of packages for construction items lasting at least 6 months.
                       Provide training in first-aid skill and first-aid kit to workers and site engineer

                       Regularly exam worker’s health to ensure occupational health

                       Provide workers with PPE such as masks, gloves, helmets, shoes/boots,
                        goggles, safety belt, etc. and enforce wearing during working especially
                        working at heights and in dangerous areas.
                       Limit or avoid working in extreme weather conditions, e.g. too hot, heavy
                        rain, strong wind, and dense fog.
                       Provide sufficient lighting when carrying out construction activities at night.

                       Locate noise-generating sources and concrete mixing plants far enough from
                        and downwind of residential areas and camps.
                       Provide the camps with sufficient supplies of clean water, power, and
                        sanitary facilities. There must be at least one toilet compartment for every
                        25 workers, with separate toilets for males and females. Workers’ beds must
                        be provided with mosquito nets so as to prevent dengue fever. Temporary
                        tents will be unacceptable.
                       Clean camps, kitchens, baths, and toilets and sanitize regularly, and keep
                        good sanitation. Provide dustbins and collect wastes daily from the camps.
                        Clear drainage ditches around the camps periodically.
                       Stop all construction activities during rains and storms, or upon accidents or
                        serious incidents.
                       Contractor to ensure compliance with the requirements and procedures of
                        the project Labor Management Procedure (LMP).
                      Risk of fire, explosion and leakage of fuel
                       Store fuels and chemicals in areas with impermeable ground, roofs,
                        surrounding banks, and warning signs at least 50 m far from and downwind
                        of residential areas and the camps.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                          271   Page
                                                                                                                                                           Confidential
                                                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
    E&S issues                                     Mitigation measures                                   Vietnam code/   Implementation Responsibility Supervision
                                                                                                          regulation          time
                       Provide training in fire-fighting to workers and fire-extinguishers for the
                        camps.
                       Prepare an emergency plan for chemical/fuel spill incident risk before
                        construction begins.
                       Safely install power lines at offices and in construction sites and do not lay
                        connectors on the ground or water surface. Electric wires must be with plugs.
                        Place outdoor electric panels in protection cabinets.
                       Provision of proper eye protection such as welder goggles and/or a full-face
                        eye shield for all personnel involved in, or assisting, welding operations.
                        Additional methods may include the use of welding barrier screens around
                        the specific workstation (a solid piece of light metal, canvas, or plywood
                        designed to block welding light from others). Devices to extract and remove
                        noxious fumes at the source may also be required.
                       Special hot work and fire prevention precautions and Standard Operating
                        Procedures (SOPs) should be implemented if welding or hot cutting is
                        undertaken outside established welding work stations, including ‘Hot Work
                        Permits, stand-by fire extinguishers, stand-by fire watch, and maintaining
                        the fire watch for up to one hour after welding or hot cutting has terminated.
                        Special procedures are required for hot work on tanks or vessels that have
                        contained flammable materials
                      Risk of security forces
                       The contractor will be required to seek to ensure a proper procedure for
                        recruiting and maintaining the security force at site
                      Risks of community’s health and safety
                       The Contractor will have to conform to regulations in Law No. 55/2024/
                        QH15 on fire and rescue; Law No. 84/2015/QH13 on Occupational Safety
                        and Health and Circular No. 03/2019/TT-BXD by the Ministry of
                        Construction on safety in construction.

                       The Project owner and contractor are to cooperate closely with the local
                        government in performing effective community sanitation in case of
                        epidemic symptoms breaking out in the area.
                       The Project owner and contractor are to cooperate with local authorities in
                        preventing and fighting against social evils.
                       Do not disturb or cause trouble to community

                       Use static compacting when the road base is constructed near areas with
                        many households and weak temporary works to restrict vibration.
                       The Project will cooperate with the local health agency in developing and
                        implementing plans for control of diseases among workers.

                       Delineate the discovered site or area;

                       Relevant local or national authorities would be in charge of protecting and
                        preserving the site before deciding on subsequent appropriate procedures.

                        This would require a preliminary evaluation of the findings to be performed.
                        The significance and importance of the findings should be assessed
                        according to the various criteria relevant to cultural heritage; those include
                        the aesthetic, historic, scientific or research, social and economic values;
                       Decisions on how to handle the finding shall be taken by the responsible
                        authorities. This could include changes in the layout (such as when finding
                        an irremovable remain of cultural or archeological importance)
                        conservation, preservation, restoration and salvage;
                       If the cultural sites and/or relics are of high value and site preservation is
                        recommended by the professionals and required by the cultural relics
                        authority, the Project’s Owner will need to make necessary design changes
                        to accommodate the request and preserve the site;
                       Decisions concerning the management of the finding shall be communicated
                        in writing by relevant authorities;
                       Construction works could resume only after permission is granted from the
                        responsible local authorities concerning safeguard of the heritage.

                       Disseminate Project information to affected parties (for example local

                       Provide a community relations contact from whom interested parties can
                        receive information on site activities, Project status and Project
                        implementation results.

                        understandable to the general public and in a form of useful to interested
                        citizens and elected officials through the preparation of fact sheets and news
                        release, when major findings become available during Project
                        implementation phase.
                       Monitor community concerns and information requirements as the Project
                        progresses.
                       Respond to telephone inquiries and written correspondence in a timely and
                        accurate manner.
                       Inform local residents about construction and work schedules, interruption
                        of services, traffic detour routes and provisional bus routes, blasting and
                        demolition, as appropriate.
                       Limit construction activities at night. When necessary ensure that night work
                        is carefully scheduled and the community is properly informed so they can
                        take necessary measures.
                       At least five days in advance of any service interruption (including water,
                        electricity, telephone, bus routes) the community must be advised through
                        postings at the Project site, at bus stops, and in affected homes/businesses.

                        community, especially a sketch of the construction area and the ESMP of the

 3. Due to operation
   of the sluices
 a) Degradation of       Engage a consultant to establish an operations Operation                        Kien Giang
    surface   water       scheme (for approval) for regulating salinity and Units                         (now    An
    quality               limiting salt intrusion into agricultural areas.                                Giang) DAE
                         Closely consult with local authorities and people

                          on development of operational regulation of the 23
                          sluices and early inform on the closing time of the
                          23 sluice gates for local.
                         Conduct      water/ecology monitoring at the
                          monitoring sites to provide baseline data prior to
                          sluice gate operations, no later than 1 month
                          before completion of construction.
                         Communicate,        raise local awareness of
                          environmental protection, especially water quality
                          in canals.
                         Collect packages, excessive foods during the

                          argicultural and aquacultural production; prohibit
                          littering in surrounding area or water sources
                         Radically      collect and prevent domestic
                          wastewater of local people from flowing into
                          surrounding canals.
                         Measures can be taken in pond systems to mitigate

                          impacts on water quality (i) reduce the amount of
                          contamination of the effluent; (ii) prevent pond
                          effluent from entering surrounding water bodies;


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                                289   Page
                                                                                                             Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
 E&S issues             Mitigation measures                                       Responsibility Supervision
                         and (iii) treat the effluent before its release into the
                         receiving waters to reduce contaminant levels,
                         including:
                           Feed: (i) Ensure that pellet feed has a minimum
                            amount of “fines” or feed dust. Fines are not
                            consumed and add to the nutrient load in the
                            water; (ii) Match the pellet size to the species’
                            life-cycle stage (e.g. smaller pellets should be
                            fed to fry or juvenile animals to reduce the
                            unconsumed fraction); (iii) Regularly monitor
                            feed uptake to determine whether it is being
                            consumed and adjust feeding rates accordingly.
                            Feed may be wasted due to overfeeding or not
                            feeding at the right time of day; (iv) Where
                            feasible, use floating or extruded feed pellets as
                            they allow for observation during feeding time;
                            (v) Store feed in cool, dry facilities and ideally
                            for no longer than 30 days to avoid reduction in
                            vitamin contents. Moldy feed should never be
                            used as it may cause disease; (vi) Spread feed as
                            evenly as possible throughout the culture
                            system, ensuring that as many animals as
                            possible have access to the feed. Some species
                            are highly territorial, and uneaten feed adds to
                            the nutrient load; (vii) Feed several times a day,
                            especially when animals are young, allowing
                            better access to food, better feed conversion
                            ratios and less waste; (viii) Halt feeding at a
                            suitable interval before harvest to eliminate the
                            presence of food and / or fecal material in the
                            animal’s gut; and (ix) During harvesting,
                            contain and disinfect blood water and effluent
                            to reduce the risk of disease spread and to
                            contain effluent matter.
                           Suspended solids: (i) Avoid discharging
                            waters from ponds while they are being
                            harvested with nets as this will add to the
                            suspended solids in the effluent drainage; (ii) If
                            feasible, use partial draining techniques to
                            empty ponds that have been harvested. The last
                            10–15 percent of pond water contains the
                            highest quantities of dissolved nutrients,
                            suspended solids, and organic matter. After
                            harvest, hold the remaining water in the pond
                            for a number of days before discharge, or
                            transfer to a separate treatment facility.
                           Fertilizers: (i) Plan the rate and mode of
                            application of fertilizers to maximize utilization
                            and prevent over-application, taking into
                            account predicted consumption rates; (ii)
                            Increase the efficiency of application and
                            dispersion through such practices as dilution of
                            liquid fertilizers or solution of granulated
                            fertilizers prior to application. Other options
                            include the use of powdered fertilizers or the

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                     290   Page
                                                                                                  Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
 E&S issues             Mitigation measures                                    Responsibility Supervision
                           placement of powdered fertilizer bags in
                           shallow water to allow solution and dispersion;
                           (iii) Consider the use of time-released fertilizer
                           in which resin coated granules release nutrients
                           into the pond water, with the rate of release
                           corresponding to water temperature and
                           movement; (iv) Avoid the use of fertilizers
                           containing ammonia or ammonium in water
                           with pH of 8 or above to avoid the formation of
                           toxic unionized ammonia (NH3); (v) Depending
                           on the system (e.g. freshwater aquaculture),
                           grow organic fertilizer (e.g. natural grass) in the
                           pond basin after harvest; (vi) Initiate pond
                           fertilization only in static ponds with no pond
                           water overflow that can impact downstream
                           waters and watersheds; and (vii) Conduct pond
                           fertilization to avoid or minimize consequences
                           of potential runoff due to floods or heavy rain
                           and avoid application to overflowing ponds.
                          Chemicals and antibiotics: (i) Design the pond
                           depth to reduce the need for chemical control of
                           aquatic weeds and reduce thermal stratification;
                           (ii) Do not use antifoulants to treat cages and
                           pens. The chemically active substances used in
                           antifouling agents are very poisonous and
                           highly stable in an aquatic environment. Clean
                           nets manually or in a net washing machine. The
                           toxic agro-chemicals in Annex 1 of Circular No.
                           26/2018/BNNPTNT of MARD (now the MAE)
                           dated 15 November 2018 are absolutely banned
                           for use in aquaculture; and list of drugs
                           presented in Annex 3 of Circular No.
                           15/2009/BNN is just restricted for use in
                           aquaculture.
                          Prevention of effluent from entering
                           surrounding water system: (i) Reuse water from
                           harvested ponds by pumping it into adjacent
                           ponds to help complement their primary
                           productivity, provided that the level of BOD is
                           controlled; This process is called “bloom
                           seeding,” and requires careful timing of
                           harvests; and (ii) Consider the hydrology of the
                           region in the design of the pond system and
                           ensure that the pond embankments are high
                           enough to contain the pond water and prevent
                           loss of effluent during periods of increased
                           rainfall and potential flooding.
                          Process Wastewater Treatment: Techniques
                           for treating industrial process wastewater in
                           aquaculture sector        include grease traps,
                           skimmers or oil water separators for separation
                           of floatable solids; flow and load equalization;
                           sedimentation for suspended solids reduction
                           using clarifiers or settling ponds; biological
                           treatment, typically aerobic treatment, for

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                   291   Page
                                                                                                Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
 E&S issues             Mitigation measures                                   Responsibility Supervision
                            reduction of soluble organic matter (BOD);
                            biological nutrient removal for reduction in
                            nitrogen and phosphorus; chlorination of
                            effluent when disinfection is required;
                            dewatering and disposal of residuals; in some
                            instances composting or land application of
                            wastewater treatment residuals of acceptable
                            quality may be possible. Additional engineering
                            controls may be required (i) for removal of
                            residual     feed    supplements,      chemicals,
                            antibiotics, etc. which may pass through the
                            wastewater treatment system, and (ii) to contain
                            and neutralize nuisance odors. For sea water
                            applications, unit operations for wastewater
                            treatment may have to be suitably adapted to the
                            relatively high salinity of the water.
                        Solid waste management: (i) Provide dustbins to
                        collect domestic solid waste separately from
                        hazardous wastes (chemical bags, containers) at
                        farming sites. Organic wastes can be disposed to
                        landfills for treatment, while hazardous wastes
                        could be backed to the producers for reusing or
                        recycling; and (ii) Sludge generated from shrimp
                        ponds after harvesting should be disposed to
                        landfills or used as raw materials for producing
                        composts for agricultural production if possible.
 b) Impacts of saline Salt-tolerant construction materials are chosen in PPMU               Kien Giang
    and/or brackish the design stage of the sluice                                          (now    An
    water on the                                                                            Giang) DAE
    sluices

                        Consider the hydrology of the region in the design

 e) Impacts        on Impacts on biodiversity and temporary blockage Operation             Kien Giang
    aquatic life      of fish passage                                        Units         (now    An
                       The aperture of the sluices has been calculated to                 Giang) DAE
                        maintain a specific flow velocity within the sluices
                        to facilitate the movement of aquatic species from
                        the main rivers into the project’s area. In
                        particular, the ideal flow velocities vary according
                        to the following criteria: 0.2 to 0.30 m/s for small
                        shrimps and prawns; 0.4 to 0.6 m/s for larger
                        shrimps and small fish (sizes 2-6cm); 0.6 to 0.8
                        m/s for medium-sized fish (sizes 6-25cm); and 0.6
                        to 1.1 m/s for large fish (greater than 25cm). The
                        fish are carried away by the water current when
                        the velocity surpasses 1.1 m/s. When the velocity
                        exceeds 1.1 m/s, the fish will be swept away by
                        the water flow.
                       Division of Water Resources of Kien Giang (now

                        An Giang) province will conduct aquatic life
                        monitoring program every 3 months for at least 2
                        years after construction is completed to observe
                        the potential impacts (positive and negative) of the

   households          Plan a separate area of nursing and raising such                     Giang)
                        species with confined water sources to supply for                    PPC
                        the model activities.
                       As semi-intensive and intensive shrimp farming

                        models require large budgets, good experience
                        and capacity to capture the transferred high
                        technology, while the poor and EM often lack
                        such resources, demonstrated models of the
                        extensive shrimp farms, ecological shrimp
                        farming in mangrove forests or combined raising
                        shrimp in rice field need to be developed where
                        the land funds are available to support the poor
                        and EM in accessing the successful models for
                        application. In addition, the poor and EM are
                        potential to be left behind the development in case
                        they have no aquaculture land available or just a
                        very small land area in the planned shrimp
                        farming. In this context, the project/subprojects
                        should support in improving their capacity on

5.2. ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL COMMITMENT PLAN
529. The project owner has developed an Environmental and social commitment plan (ESCP).
The project ESCP considers the results of the ESIA, the WB's E&S due diligence, and
stakeholder engagement. It provides an accurate summary of the material steps and actions to
avoid, minimize, reduce, or otherwise mitigate the potential E&S risks and impacts of the
project. In addition, it outlines a procedure for the adaptive management of project changes and
unforeseen circumstances. It has been agreed upon with the Bank and disclosed locally at the
project sites as well as on the WB's external website. It will be included in the project's legal
agreement.
530. The Kien Giang (now An Giang) PPMU will diligently implement the measures and
actions identified in the ESCP within the specified timeframes and will monitor and report on
the status of its implementation in accordance with the ESSs. The PPMU will promptly notify
the Bank of any proposed changes to the project's scope, design, implementation, or operation
that are likely to result in an adverse change in the project's environmental or social risks or
impacts. In that case, the PPMU will conduct additional assessments and stakeholder
engagement as appropriate under the ESSs. Based on the findings of such assessments and
consultation, the PPMU will propose changes to the ESCP and relevant management tools for
the WB’s approval. The updated ESCP will be disclosed.
531. The PPMU will monitor the project's E&S performance per the legal agreement,
including the ESCP. The extent and mode of monitoring will be proportionate to the nature,
E&S risks and impacts, and compliance requirements of the project, and will be agreed upon
with the Bank. The PPMU will ensure that adequate institutional arrangements, systems,
resources, and personnel are in place for monitoring. Where appropriate and as specified in the
ESCP, the PPMU will involve stakeholders and third parties, such as independent experts, local
communities, or non-governmental organizations (NGOs), to supplement or verify its
monitoring activities. Where other agencies or third parties are in charge of managing specific
risks and impacts and implementing mitigation measures, the PPMU will work with them to
establish and monitor such mitigation measures. See details in the ESCP.
