5.3.2.   Environmental and social compliance framework

5.3.2.1. Environmental and Social duties of the PPMU/Detailed design consultants

  When preparing TORs for consulting services and construction bidding documents, the
   PPMU will work closely with consultants/contractors to ensure: (i) contract packaging and
   cost estimations, including ESMP implementation, the services of independent E&S
   monitoring, environmental sampling/monitoring and compliance supervision, reporting,
   etc.; (ii) ESCOPs, as well as site-specific mitigation measures, are incorporated into the
   bidding documents; (iii) environmental supervision and training are included in the scope of
   works assigned to the CSC.
  In the FS/detailed design stage, the PPMU shall work closely with the FS consultant and the
   detailed design engineers to ensure that the greening/landscaping, environmentally friendly
   solutions, and relevant mitigation measures proposed in the ESIA/ESMP are considered and
   incorporated into the detailed engineering design as appropriate.



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                     301   Page
                                                                                                  Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
  During the construction phase, the PPMU shall work closely with the CSC to monitor the
   compliance of contractors and report to relevant authorities and the WB. The PPMU will
   also direct the CSC and contractors on the actions to be undertaken in the case when issues,
   incidents, or accidents arise. Besides, PPMU will be responsible for ensuring the effective
   implementation of the LMP during construction.
  Throughout project implementation, the PPMU will appoint at least one qualified individual
   as EO and one qualified individual as SO, in which:
      The EO will oversee environmental issues and monitor standards compliance of the
       project. The EO will be supported by the IMC, the EOs of the CSC as well as contractors.
      The SO will oversee and coordinate the social/resettlement aspects of the project.

5.3.2.2. Environmental and Social Duties of the Contractor

533. The contractor firstly shall adhere to minimize the impact that may be a result of the
project construction activities and secondly, apply the mitigation measures under ESMP to
prevent harm and nuisances on local communities and the environment caused by the impacts
in construction and operation stages.
534. Remedial actions that cannot be effectively carried out during construction should be
carried out on completion of the works (and before issuance of the acceptance of completion of
works)
535. The duties of the Contractor include but not limiting to:
  Compliance with relevant legislative requirements governing the environment, public health
   and safety;
  Work within the scope of contractual requirements and other tender conditions;

  Organize representatives of the construction team to participate in the joint site inspections
   undertaken by the Environmental Supervisor (ES) of the CSC;
  Carry out any corrective actions instructed by the Environmental Control Officer (ECO) of
   the PPMU and ES;
  In case of non-compliance/discrepancies, carry out investigation and submit proposals on
   mitigation measures, and implement remedial measures to reduce environmental impact;
  Stop construction activities, which generate adverse impacts upon receiving instructions
   from the ECO and ES. Propose and carry out corrective actions and implement alternative
   construction methods, if required, to minimize the environmental impacts; Non-compliance
   by the Contractor will be cause for suspension of works and other penalties until the non-
   compliance has been resolved to the satisfaction of the ECO and ES.

5.3.2.3. Contractor's Environmental and Social Management Plan (C-ESMP)

536. Following contract signing, each contractor will be required to prepare and submit a C-
ESMP, which will be reviewed and cleared for alignment with ESF requirements by the CSC,
PPMU, and WB.
537. The C-ESMP aims to provide information for environmental and social management
during the project construction on-site to ensure that the contractor and any sub-constructors
have minimal environmental impact. It specifies how the contractor will reduce impacts caused

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               302   Page
                                                                                            Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
by the construction of the project and documents the contractor’s response to supervision,
monitoring, verification, internal audit, and correction or improvement of E&S performance. It
must be site-specific and include information on the control measures that will be applied on-
site to mitigate any potential E&S impacts of the proposed works/activities.
538. If the proposed works/activities outlined in the C-ESMP are changed or modified over
the term of the contract, the contractor will be expected to update the C-ESMP to reflect these
changes or modifications. The C-ESMP should comprise the following sections:
 A  statement of policy, defining the contractor’s E&S policy and an indication of a
   commitment to the execution of its C-ESMP.
  A brief of the document, including the date of issue; revision status; and list of preparation
   people with their signature.
  Applicable   laws and regulations associated with the requirements in the ESMP and
   identification of the contractor licenses, permits, and approval associated with the CESMP.
  Details on how the E&S impacts and risks identified in the ESIA will be managed on-site,
   including (i) the site-specific measures to mitigate impacts during construction; (ii) ECOPs;
   (iii) the C-ESMP to be developed after the contractor is selected and before construction
   starts; and (iv) the contractor’s dredging management plan and the contractor's vegetation
   clearance management plan that the contractor is required to develop.
  Details on E&S training for all on-site staff of the contractor (including subcontractors) is
   required. As a minimum, all contractor staff working at the project sites must: (i) be familiar
   with and understand the C-ESMP for their works; (ii) be aware of their environmental and
   social responsibilities and legal obligations on-site; and (iii) receive OHS and emergency
   response training.
  Specific   capabilities, support mechanisms, and resources necessary to satisfactorily
   implement the C-ESMP. Detailed environmental and social responsibilities of all contractor
   staff, including subcontractors working on-site with appropriate knowledge, skills, and
   training for specific tasks shall be identified.
539. The contractor shall be responsible for preparing monthly environmental reports as part
of the progress reports required in the bidding document, including any accident and incident
reporting, for submission to the project owner. These reports may contain the following
information: (i) Implementation of the C-ESMP per the agreed program; (ii) Any difficulties
encountered during C-ESMP implementation, as well as recommendations for future solutions;
(iii) The number and type of non-compliance, as well as suggested corrective actions; (iv)
Reports from subcontractors involved in the C-ESMP implementation, including minutes of
meetings and discussions undertaken by the contractor; (v) Minutes of meetings and discussions
held by the project owner regarding C-ESMP implementation; and (vi) Implementation of the
workers’ CoC and OHS management plan, including community safety.

5.3.2.4. Contractor’s Safety and Environment Officer (SEO)

540. The contractor shall be required to appoint a competent individual as the Contractor’s on-
site safety and environment officer (SEO). The SEO must be appropriately trained in
environmental management and must possess the skills necessary to transfer environmental
management knowledge to all personnel involved in the contract. The SEO will be responsible


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                303   Page
                                                                                             Confidential
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
for monitoring the contractor’s compliance with the ESMP requirements and the environmental
specifications. The duties of the SEO shall include but not be limited to the following:
     Carry out environmental site inspections to assess and audit the contractors' site practice,
      equipment, and work methodologies with respect to pollution control and adequacy of
      environmental mitigation measures implemented;
     Monitor compliance with environmental protection measures, pollution prevention and
      control measures, and contractual requirements;
     Monitor the implementation of environmental mitigation measures;
     Prepare audit reports for the environmental monitoring data and site environmental
      conditions;
     Investigate complaints and recommend any required corrective measures;
     Advise the contractor on environment improvement, awareness, and proactive pollution
      prevention measures;
     Recommend suitable mitigation measures to the contractor in the case of non-compliance.
      Carry out additional monitoring of noncompliance instructed by the ECO/ES;
     Inform the contractor and ECO/ES of environmental issues, submit the contractor’s ESMP
      Implementation Plan to the ECO/ES, and relevant authorities, if required;
     Keep detailed records of all site activities that may relate to the environment.

5.3.2.5. Independent Monitoring Consultant (IMC)

541. To minimize the environmental impacts during the construction stage of the Project, the
Project owner shall ensure that environmental quality monitoring requirements are established
for the project. An IMC appointed by CPMU shall carry out the monitoring.
  IMC will be responsible for carrying out environmental sampling, monitoring and marking
      reports during all stages of the Project. Environmental quality monitoring will be reported
      periodically to PPMU (every 06 months in the construction stage and operation stage).
  IMC will also supply specialized assistance to CPMU and ECO in environmental matters.


5.3.2.6. Environmental Supervision during Construction

542. During the construction stage, a qualified CSC reporting to the PPMU shall carry out the
environmental supervision. The CSC is responsible for inspecting, and supervising all
construction activities to ensure that mitigation measures adopted in the ESMP are properly
implemented and that the negative environmental impacts of the Project are minimized. The
CSC shall engage a sufficient number of Environmental Supervision Engineers with adequate
knowledge of environmental protection and construction project management to perform the
required duties and to supervise the Contractor’s performance. Specifically, ES will:
  Review and assess on behalf of the PPMU whether the construction design meets the
      requirements of the mitigation and management measures of the ESMP,
  Supervise      site environmental management system of contractors including their
      performance, experience, and handling of site environmental issues, and provide corrective
      instructions;




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                     304   Page
                                                                                                  Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
  Review the ESMP implementation by the contractors, verify and confirm environmental
   supervision procedures, parameters, monitoring locations, equipment, and results;
  Report  ESMP implementation status to the PPMU and prepare the environmental
   supervision statement during the construction stage; and
  Approve invoices or payments.


5.3.2.7. Compliance with Legal and Contractual Requirements

543. The construction activities shall comply not only with contractual environmental
protection and pollution control requirements but also with the environmental protection and
pollution control laws of Viet Nam.
544. All the works method statements submitted by the Contractor to the ECO for approval
shall also be sent to the ES to see whether sufficient environmental protection and pollution
control measures have been included.
545. The ES shall also review the progress and program of the works to check that relevant
environmental laws have not been violated, and that any potential for violating the laws can be
prevented.
546. The Contractor shall copy relevant documents to the SEO and the ES. The document shall
at least include the updated work progress report, the updated work measure, and the application
letters for different license/permits under the environmental protection laws, and all the valid
licenses/permits. The SEO and the ES shall also have access, upon request, to the Site Log-
Book.
547. After reviewing the documents, the SEO or the ES shall advise the ECO and the
contractor of any non-compliance with the contractual and legislative requirements on
environmental protection and pollution control for them to take follow-up actions. If the SEO
or the ES concludes that the status of the license/permit application and any environmental
protection and pollution control preparation works may not comply with the work measure or
may result in a potential violation of environmental protection and pollution control
requirements, they shall advise the Contractor and the ECO accordingly.

5.3.2.8. Environmental Claims and Penalty System

548. In the compliance framework, if non-compliance with environmental regulations is
discovered by ECO/CSC/ES/IMC during the site supervision, 2% values of interim payment of
the contractor of this month will be held back. The Contractor will be given a grace period
(determined by CSC/ES) to repair the violation. If the Contractor performs the repairs within
the grace period (confirmed by CSC/ES), no penalty is incurred, and keeping money will be
paid. However, if the Contractor fails to successfully make the necessary repairs within the
grace period, the Contractor will pay the cost for a third party to repair the damages (deduction
from keeping money).
549. In case IMC/CSC/ES is not detected as non-compliance with the environmental
regulations of the contractor, they will be responsible for payment to repair the violation.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               305   Page
                                                                                            Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
5.4. REPORTING

CPMU monthly. At the project level, the CPMU will prepare an E&S monitoring report twice
per year for submission to the WB describing the project progress and compliance with the
ESMF and other requirements. The reporting requirement is described in Table 98.

mitigation measures and other ESMP requirements under contractor responsibility in the
bidding and contractual documents; iii) monitoring and supervision on implementation
performance of contractors, CSC, and PPMU according to ESMP, ESCOPs, and COC on SEA;
ESCP implementation and update and iv) any challenges in E&S implementation, solutions,
and lessons learned.

5.5. ENVIRONMENTAL MONITORING AND SUPERVISION PROGRAM

5.5.1.    Waste generation monitoring

  The   content of waste generation monitoring includes the quantity and composition of
     domestic solid waste and wastewater, construction waste, and wasted oil.
  Monitoring frequency: Every 3 months.

  Implementation     period: Starts from the site preparation phase until the end of the
     construction phase.
  This task is directly carried out by the contractors. The project owner and CSC will monitor
     the performance of the contractors and report to the Department of Agriculture and
     Environment after each monitoring period.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               306    Page
                                                                                             Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
5.5.2.   Other monitoring

5.5.2.1. During construction phase

  Surface water monitoring:

      Frequency: Once every 3 months.
      Monitoring sites: 1 site/sluice x 6 sluices = 6 sites.
      Monitoring parameters: pH, BOD5, COD, TSS, DO, Total N, Total P, Total Coliform and
       Ecoli
      Applied standards: Table 3 of the QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT - National technical
       regulations on surface water quality.
  Wastewater monitoring:

      Frequency: Once every 3 months.
      Monitoring sites: 6 construction sites.
      Monitoring parameters: pH, BOD5, TSS, TDS, H2S, NH4+, NO3-, Oil and grease, Total
       surfactants, PO43-, Coliform.
      Applied standards: QCVN 14:2008/BTNMT- National technical regulations on
       wastewater (column A, K=1).
  Sediment monitoring:

      Frequency: Once every 3 months.
      Monitoring sites: 1 site/sluice x 6 sluices = 6 sites.
      Monitoring parameters: pHKCl, Cu, Fe, Mn, Zn, Cd, As.
      Applied standards: QCVN 43:2017/BTNMT - National technical regulations on sediment
       quality (saltwater and brackish water sediment).
  Air monitoring:

      Frequency: Once every 3 months.
      Monitoring sites: 8 sites (6 sites at sluice construction sites and 2 sites at road construction
       sites)
      Monitoring parameters: Noise level, TSP.
      Applied standards: QCVN 05/2023/BTNMT- National technical regulation on ambient
       air quality; QCVN 26/2025/BNNMT- National technical regulation on noise.
  Canal bed elevation and erosion monitoring

      Frequency: Once every 3 months.
      Monitoring sites: within sluice construction sites
      Monitoring parameters: elevation and erosion.
      Comparison requirements: changes over time in the elevation of the canal bed terrain
       and the evolution of the shoreline of the sluice construction areas.



Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                     307   Page
                                                                                                  Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
5.5.2.2. During operation phase

552. Because the project comprises the construction of sluices, roads, and electricity lines, no
environmental monitoring stations are set up during its operation. The operating unit will
conduct sluice settlement monitoring 4 times a year to take prompt action to prevent damage to
project items. Specifically:
  Frequency: Once every 3 months.

  Monitoring sites: within the system of 23 sluices.

  Monitoring parameters: elevation and erosion.


5.6. CAPACITY BUILDING                      FOR        ENVIRONMENTAL          AND       SOCIAL
     MANAGEMENT

5.6.1.   Current Kien Giang (now An Giang) PPMU’s capacity for environmental and
         social management

553. Kien Giang (now An Giang) PPMU is currently a team with experience in managing and
implementing projects using IBRD funds such as Management of Irrigation Systems for Rural
Development in the Mekong Delta Project (WB6), the Mekong Delta Integrated Climate
Resilience and Sustainable Livelihoods Project (WB9), the Mekong Delta Sustainable
Aquaculture Development Project (WB), the Phu Quoc Sustainable Water Management Project
(WB), etc. All of these projects have been implemented in Kien Giang (now An Giang)
province and managed by the PPMU. They were evaluated by the MARD (now the MAE) and
donors as well-executed, scheduled and highly effective projects. Therefore, it can be assessed
that the PPMU is capable of organizing and managing the project implementation under the
safeguard policies and international practices.
554. However, the PPMU’s capacity needs to be improved because existing staff lack
experience with projects involving the WB’s new ESF. Furthermore, when the project is built,
other environmental and technical personnel will be engaged, some of whom may be unfamiliar
with the WB’s safeguard policies and ESF. As a result, there is a risk that the resources allocated
to addressing environmental and social issues in this project will be insufficient, negatively
impacting environmental performance/compliance with the ESSs.
555. Also, although the PPMU attended many WB-organized trainings, an E&S capability
assessment completed during project preparation highlighted a need for additional capacity
building in specific ESSs throughout project implementation. As part of the ESMP, a capacity-
building program will be implemented to address such gaps.

5.6.2.   Environmental and Social Capacity Building Program

556. The capacity of PPMU, CSC, and contractors’ staff in charge of ESMP implementation
and supervision will be enhanced. They must be aware of the project's environmental
management goals, methodologies, and practices. Therefore, the project will address the lack
of capacities and knowledge in environmental and social management through (i) institutional
capacity building and (ii) training in the following ways:
   Institutional strengthening: Some measures will be implemented to increase PPMU's ability
    to coordinate environmental and social management efforts. A qualified environment and


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 308   Page
                                                                                              Confidential
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
    social staff will be appointed by the PPMU. The staff will be in charge of ESMP
    coordination, including GRM and coordination of environmental and social impact
    monitoring, training, reporting, etc. The staff will be strengthened capacity directly by the
    WB or IMC.
   Training: The project will be applied the WB's new ESF and the WB will provide annual
    safeguard policy training. As a result, once mobilized, all environmental and social staff of
    the PPMU, CSC, and contractors will get the necessary training for the application of the
    new ESF. Other trainings will be also conducted for important stakeholders during the
    construction phase.

5.8. GRIEVANCE AND REDRESS MECHANISM (GRM)

5.8.1.   Project Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM)

562. Within the Vietnamese legal framework citizen rights to complain are protected. As part
of the overall implementation of the project, a GRM will be established by the PPMU. The
GRM will identify procedures, responsible persons, and contact information will be readily
accessible to the public, and will handle grievances and resolve them at the lowest level as
quickly as possible. The mechanism will provide the framework within which complaints about
environmental and social issues can be handled, grievances can be addressed, and disputes can
be settled quickly. The GRM will be in place before the project construction commences.
563. During construction, the GRM will be managed by the Contractor under the supervision
of the CSC. The Contractor will inform the communities and communes affected by the project
about the GRM in place to handle complaints and concerns about the project. This will be done
via the information disclosure and consultation process under which the Contractor will
communicate with the affected communities and interested authorities regularly: hold meetings
at least quarterly, publish a monthly information brochure, place announcements in local media,
post notices of upcoming planned activities, and so on.
564. All complaints and corresponding actions undertaken by the Contractor will be recorded
in the project safeguard monitoring report. Complaints and claims for damages could be lodged
as follows:
   Verbally: direct to the CSC and/or the contractor safeguard staff or representative at the
    project office
   In writing: by hand-delivering or posting a written complaint to the address specified

   By telephone, fax, email: to the CSC, the contractor safeguard staff or the contractor’s
    representative.
565. On receipt of a complaint, the CSC, contractor safeguard staff, or representative will
register the complaint in the Complaints File and maintain a Log of events about it thereafter
until its resolution. Immediately after receipt, three copies of the complaint will be made. The
original will be kept in the File, one copy will be used by the contractor’s safeguard staff, one
copy will be forwarded to the CSC, and the third copy to the PPMU within 24 hours of the
complaint being made. Information to be recorded in the Complaints Log will include (see
suggested form in Annex 5):
   The date and time of the complaint;

   The name, address, and contact details of the complainant;

   A short description of the issue of complaint;

   Actions taken to address the complaint, including persons contacted and findings of each
    step in the complaint redress process;
   The dates and times when the complainant is contacted during the redress process;


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               313   Page
                                                                                            Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
   The final resolution of the complaint;

   The date, time, and manner in which the complainant was informed thereof; and

   The complainant’s signature when a resolution has been obtained.

566. Small complaints will be dealt with within one week. Within two weeks (and weekly
thereafter), a written reply will be delivered to the complainant (by hand, post, fax, e-mail)
indicating the procedures taken and progress to date.
567. The main objective of the GRM will be to resolve an issue as quickly as possible by the
simplest means involving as few people as possible, at the lowest possible level. Only when an
issue cannot be resolved at the simplest level and/or within 15 days, will other authorities
become involved. Such a situation may arise, for example, when damages are claimed and the
amount to be paid cannot be resolved or the cause of the damages determined.

5.8.2.   Grievance Redress Mechanism for Workers

568. As mentioned above, for this project, the project GRM will be in place to address the
concerns of project-affected parties. However, the nature of workers' workplace concerns is
typically different, such as demand for employment opportunities; labor wage rates and
payment delays; disagreement over working conditions; and health and safety concerns in the
workplace. As a result, to meet the requirements of ESS2, a separate GRM will be developed
for project workers and outlined in the LMPs.
569. Grievances should be handled objectively, promptly, and in response to the needs and
concerns of the aggrieved workers. It should be possible for employees to submit grievances in
a variety of ways, including in person, via phone, text message, mail, and email. The grievance
should be recorded and acknowledged within a day. Even though the timing for remedy will
depend on the nature of the grievance, health, and safety concerns in the workplace and other
critical matters should be resolved immediately. When a grievance cannot be resolved within a
reasonable timeframe, the worker should be notified in writing so that he or she might consider
filing a claim with the state's labor inspection agency (see below for more details). In addition,
the mechanism will enable anonymous complaints to be made and addressed. Individuals who
submit comments or complaints may request that their identities remain confidential. The
mechanism will incur no cost ensure no retribution, and will not prevent access to judicial or
administrative remedies.
570. Concerns of direct workers: Periodically, Kien Giang (now An Giang) PPMU will
organize team meetings to discuss any workplace issues. Direct workers' complaints will be
documented along with the PPMU’s remedy actions. The summary of grievance cases will be
reported to the WB as part of progress reports. If an aggrieved direct worker wishes to escalate
his issue or raise his concern anonymously and/or to someone other than his line
manager/PPMU, he may do so with relevant provincial authorities (e.g., DOHA).
571. Concerns of contracted workers. At the end of each working day, the site manager and
the OHS officer (or any other appropriate officer) of contractors will hold a team meeting with
all on-site contracted workers to discuss any workplace complaints. Any grievance will be
recorded with the contractor's response. The summary of grievance cases will be included in
the contractor's periodic report to the PPMU and CSC. When suitable and available, contracted
workers should be permitted to use a GRM already in place within their contractor. If the
aggrieved workers prefer to escalate their issues or voice their complaints anonymously or to


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                314   Page
                                                                                             Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
someone other than their immediate supervisor, they may do so with the PPMU and/or the CSC.
Prior to the start of work, contracted workers will be informed of the labor GRM. Contact
information for the PPMU and/or the CSC will be provided to contracted workers.
572. State Inspection on Labor, Sanctions Against Violations of Labour Legislation.
According to the Labour code, the labor inspector is responsible for receiving and resolving
employee complaints and denunciations of labor law violations as prescribed by law.
573. Grievance Handling Procedure. The section provides different scenarios with
responsibilities of grievances relating to the complaint handling linked to labor issues. The key
purpose of this exercise is to present the GRM process in an effective and user-friendly manner
(Table 104).

574. Grievances related to Gender Based Violence (GBV). To avoid the risk of stigmatization,
exacerbation of mental/psychological harm, and potential reprisal, the GRM shall have a
different and sensitive approach to GBV-related cases. The GRM equally applies to workers
who experience GBV. Where such a case is reported to the GRM, it should immediately be
referred to the appropriate service providers, such as medical and psychological support,
emergency accommodation, and any other necessary services. It should also be reported to the
safeguard staff of the PPMU who can advise on relevant service providers. Data on GBV cases
should not be collected through the GRM unless operators have been trained on the empathetic,
non-judgmental, and confidential collection of these complaints. Only the nature of the
complaint (what the complainant says in her/his own words) and additional demographic data,
such as age and gender, can be collected as usual. More details are referred to the project LMP.

5.8.3.   World Bank’s Grievance Redress Service (GRS)

575. Communities and individuals who believe that they are adversely affected by a WB-
supported project may submit complaints to the existing GRM of the project or the WB’s
Grievance Redress Service (GRS). The GRS ensures that complaints received are promptly
reviewed to address project-related concerns. Project-affected communities and individuals
may submit their complaints to the WB’s independent Inspection Panel which determines
whether harms occurred, or could occur, as a result of WB's non-compliance with its policies
and procedures. Complaints may be submitted at any time after concerns have been brought
directly to the WB’s attention, and Bank Management has been allowed to respond. For
information on how to submit complaints to the WB’s corporate GRS, please visit
www.worldbank.org/grs. For information on how to submit complaints to the World Bank
Inspecition Panel, please visit www.inspectionpanel.org.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                        316    Page
                                                                                                      Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
CHAPTER 6. PUBLIC CONSULTATION AND INFORMATION
           DISCLOSURE
6.1. PUBLIC CONSULTATION
576. The WB’ ESS5 and ESS10 require consultation and notification to affected people and
local authorities on social and environmental issues throughout project implementation.
577. Public consultation for the ESIA report must also adhere to the requirements outlined in
Decree No. 08/2022/ND-CP of the Government dated 10 January 2022 and Circular No.
02/2022/TT-BTNMT of the MONRE (now MAE) dated the same date, concerning
environmental impact assessment and the environmental protection plan.
578. The purpose of public consultation is to: (i) share information about the project's items
and activities with the community and stakeholders in the project area; and (ii) collect
comments and concerns about characteristics and environmentally and socially sensitive issues
in the project area from the government and local communities. On that basis, public concerns
will be included in the ESIA report and project mitigation measures.
579. Project stakeholders are defined as individuals, groups, or other entities who: (i) are
impacted or likely to be impacted directly or indirectly, positively or adversely, by the project
(also known as “affected parties”); and (ii) have an interest in the project (“interested parties”).
They include individuals or groups whose interests may be affected by the project and who
have the potential to influence the project outcomes in any way.
580. To conduct consultations for the project, open meetings, workshops, focus group
discussions, and interviews are utilized in the 3 stages of the project, including the preparation
stage (before project appraisal); project implementation stage (pre-construction and
construction) and operation stage. The public consultation strategy, which includes targeted
groups/attendees, subjects, formats, timeline, and responsibilities, is shown in Table 118.

6.1.1.   During preparation stage

6.1.1.1. Public consultation

a) Public consultation process

a.1). Consultations through posting on the website
581. The project owner sent Official letter No. 122/BQLDA-ĐHDA on April 2, 2024, to the
Kien Giang (now An Giang) DONRE (now DAE), requesting the publication of the project's
ESIA report on the website of DONRE (now Department of Agriculture and Environment).
  Managing Agency of the Website: Kien Giang (now An Giang) DONRE (DAE).

  Link to the Consultation Content:

   https://stnmt.kiengiang.gov.vn/Trang/DuThaoVanBan/ChiTietDuThaoDN.aspx?nid=64
  Date of consultation: April 10, 2024.

  Duration of consultation: 15 days, from April 10, 2024, to April 26, 2024.

a.2). Consultations through meetings
582. After completing the draft ESIA report, the project owner sent Document No.
27/BQLDA-DHDA dated January 23, 2024, along with the draft report, to the Binh Giang and
Binh Son CPCs, requesting coordination to organize community consultation meetings on
January 24-26, 2024 (Figure 100 and Figure 101).
583. The consultation results are summarized in Table 106. The list of participants and meeting
minutes are included in Appendix 2.

6.1.1.2. Consultations with experts, scientists, and professional organizations

586. The project does not fall under the category requiring consultation with experts, scientists,
and professional organizations.

6.1.2.   During implementation stage

587. Project implementation. At this stage, the details of proposed project components and
specific project-affected stakeholders will be determined. The consultation will focus more
specifically on people who are impacted by the project, especially those adversely impacted, to
address their concerns through the implementation of project-specific E&S plans. In addition,
the project will ensure that the GRM is disseminated and active to promptly address any
grievances. Special attention will be paid to vulnerable/disadvantaged groups to ensure
inclusion, non-discrimination, and access to project benefits. Consultations will also focus on
job opportunities, specific action plans, etc. (see Table 105).
588. Project operation. At this stage, the consultation will focus on feedback from
stakeholders, particularly affected ones, on the performance of E&S impact and risk
mitigations, results of grievance resolution conducted during the implementation stage as well
as general project operations, including implementation of the operational procedure of the
sluice gates. The consultation on employment opportunities for operation and maintenance
(O&M) of the project works will be also conducted. Also, areas for improvement and lessons
learned should be identified for the project. Furthermore, dissemination and reporting back to
stakeholders on the outcome of the monitoring and evaluation of the project will be also
arranged (see Table 105).

6.2. INFORMATION DISCLOSURE
589. To comply with WB’s information disclosure policy, all the draft E&S documents for the
project (ESIA/ESMP, RP, EMDP, ESCP, LMP, and SEP) in Vietnamese will be properly
disclosed at the project site and Kien Giang (now An Giang) province’s website on 15 August
2025. These documents in English were also disclosed on the WBs external website on 15 May
2025. The final project safeguards instruments will be disclosed locally at the project sites, Kien
Giang (now An Giang) province’s website, and the WB’s external website on 15 September
2025 as well.




Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                 328   Page
                                                                                              Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
CONCLUSIONS, RECOMMENDATIONS, AND COMMITMENTS
1.     CONCLUSIONS
590. The project is implemented under the political, economic, cultural, and social
development conditions of Kien Giang (now An Giang) province. It is a feasible and socio-
economically efficient project aligned with the investment policy for infrastructure
development in Vietnam in general and Kien Giang (now An Giang) province in particular,
including:
  Proactively transitioning from traditional rice cultivation to shrimp-rice farming to adapt to
     climate change and sea level rise.
  Enhancing livelihoods for residents.

  Improving infrastructure and transportation connectivity.

591. The ESIA report has fundamentally identified and quantified most of the sources of
waste; and proposed practical and feasible measures to minimize environmental and social
impacts and risks, ensuring effective treatment of waste sources. The report has developed an
environmental management and monitoring program suitable for each stage of the project and
particularly emphasizes environmental incidents throughout the construction and operation of
the project.
592. The ESIA report indicates several issues that need to be closely controlled during the
project implementation with specific characteristics such as:
  Domestic     and construction waste will be gathered, collected, transported, and treated
     according to current regulations;
  Signs    of environmental pollution will be strictly controlled by controlling sources of
     pollution and the surrounding environment;
  Criteria for labor safety and environmental sanitation will be met;

  Regional traffic safety will be controlled;

  Safe operation, maintenance, and periodic maintenance will be met.

593. The ESMP will be strictly implemented by the project owner. The objective of the plan
is to closely manage and mitigate negative environmental and social impacts and risks while
maximizing the positive impacts of the project.
594. With strict adherence to current environmental laws and proposed mitigation measures in
this ESIA report, the project will certainly operate effectively, bringing positive outcomes for
the economy, society, and the environment.

2.     RECOMMENDATIONS
595. The Project Owner respectfully requests:
  The relevant departments and agencies of Kien Giang (now An Giang) province to assist in
     the project implementation.
  Local    environmental management authorities support providing capacity building on
     environmental protection for the project's workers and local communities.


Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                               329   Page
                                                                                            Confidential
                   ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
  All levels of the province support the project owner and contractors in workers management
     (if applicable) and responses in case of safety and environmental incidents.
  Local authorities and security units support the project owner in ensuring security and traffic
     safety in the area.
  The local authorities responsible for natural resources and environment regularly inspect and
     provide specific guidance on environmental monitoring in the project area and the
     implementation of environmental commitments.

3.      COMMITMENTS
  The    project owner commits to complying with the current environmental protection
     regulations in Viet Nam: the Environmental Protection Law of 2020, related laws, and
     regulations (Government Decree 05/2025/ND-CP dated 06 January 2025, Decree No.
     08/2022/ND-CP dated January 10, 2022, Circular No. 07/2025/TT-BTNMT dated 28
     February 2025, Circular No. 02/2022/TT-BTNMT dated January 10, 2022), as well as other
     provisions during the project implementation.
  The project owner commits to strictly implementing proposed mitigation measures outlined
     in the report and adhering to relevant national technical regulations during project
     implementation. Some key aspects include:
      Commitment to collect, transport, and treat all types of solid waste, hazardous waste, and
       household waste generated during the project implementation to ensure compliance with
       safety and environmental sanitation requirements stipulated in Decree 05/2025/ND-CP
       dated 06 January 2025, Decree No. 08/2022/ND-CP dated January 10, 2022, Circular No.
       07/2025/TT-BTNMT dated 28 February 2025, Circular No. 02/2022/TT-BTNMT dated
       January 10, 2022.
      Commitment to implement construction organization measures and appropriate technical
       solutions to minimize dust, noise, water turbidity, and rainwater runoff during the project
       construction.
      Commitment to apply technical and management measures to ensure waste disposal
       meets environmental safety and sanitation requirements; and restore the environmental
       landscape of temporary land acquisition areas during project construction.
      Commitment to cooperate with relevant agencies to develop plans for preventing and
       responding to environmental incidents caused by project activities; implement awareness-
       raising measures for project workers regarding environmental protection.
      Commitment to implement an environmental management and monitoring program as
       outlined in the approved ESIA report and maintain data for state environmental protection
       management agencies to conduct inspections when necessary.
      Adherence to regulations on traffic safety, environmental sanitation, fire and explosion
       prevention, and labor safety during project implementation according to current legal
       provisions.
      Ensuring funding for environmental protection activities, and environmental monitoring
       programs, to fulfill commitments as outlined in the ESIA report.
  Commitment to the accuracy and honesty of the information, data, and documents provided
     in the ESIA report.

Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                330   Page
                                                                                             Confidential
                  ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
  Commitment that if any incidents occur during the implementation of the project that affect
   the environment, structures, and the health of workers and nearby residents, the project
   owner will provide compensation and remedy the consequences.
  Commitment to ensure the feasibility of fulfilling the responsibilities of the project owner
   after the ESIA report is approved by the competent authority following legal regulations.
  Adherence    to regulations on traffic safety, environmental sanitation, fire prevention,
   explosion prevention, and labor safety during project implementation according to current
   legal provisions.
  Ensuring funding for environmental protection activities, environmental monitoring, and
   surveillance programs, to fulfill commitments as outlined in the ESIA report.

     planning of Kien Giang province for the period 2021 - 2030 and vision to 2050.
5.   People’s Committee of Kien Giang (now An Giang) province, 2022. Decision No. 1711/QD-
     UBND dated July 7, 2022 on Land Use Planning until 2030 and land use plan in 2022 of Hon
     Dat district (former), Kien Giang (now An Giang) province.
6.   People's Committee of Kien Giang (now An Giang) province, 2020. Report on reviewing,
     adjusting and supplementing water resources planning to serve agricultural production in
     Kien Giang province to 2020 and orientation to 2030.
